title: Re: February 2026 Spotify for Developers update: thread
body:
First, I'd like to thank the Spotify Developer team for everything they've built over the years. MatchBeats simply wouldn't exist without the Spotify APIs.

 

I'm the solo developer behind MatchBeats, a social music discovery platform designed to help people discover new music through other listeners with similar taste. Users can compare compatibility, discover artists and songs from people who share their music taste, follow discussions, and share real-time "Current Vibes" with friends.

 

One of the things I'm most proud of is that MatchBeats doesn't compete with Spotify—it complements it. Every recommendation ultimately encourages users to go back and listen on their streaming service.

 

When the new API restrictions were announced earlier this year, I understood why changes were necessary. Protecting the platform from abuse is important.

 

However, I'm struggling to understand the path for legitimate early-stage developers.

 

Today, development mode limits me to just five Spotify users, while production access requires a demonstrated user base on a scale that's impossible to achieve without first allowing real users to experience the product.

 

To continue validating the idea, I launched support for Apple Music. We've already started building a small but engaged community. People are returning, connecting with one another, sharing their music, and giving incredibly valuable feedback that's helping shape the product.

 

The challenge is that Spotify listeners make up such a large part of the music community that it's difficult to validate a product built around music discovery when only five Spotify accounts can participate.

 

I'm not asking for unlimited production access or special treatment.

 

I'd love to see Spotify consider a middle ground for legitimate startups—perhaps a small "startup beta" program that allows a few hundred real users to test products that demonstrably add value to the Spotify ecosystem while still protecting the platform from abuse.

 

I'd be grateful if the Developer Relations team would consider taking a look at MatchBeats. Even if the answer is "not yet," I'd love to better understand what milestones an application like mine should realistically achieve to become eligible in the future.

 

If anyone from Spotify is interested, I'm happy to share a demo, metrics from our Apple Music beta, or discuss how MatchBeats encourages music discovery and drives listeners back to artists and streaming services.

 

Thanks for taking the time to read this, and thank you for continuing to support the developer community.

author: vcvc

title: MIXING FEATURE: Suggest compatible songs to mix for selected song
body:
When building a mixed playlist, I often add a large number of songs that might work before narrowing them down. The biggest challenge is finding the perfect song to transition into when one just isn't working.

Spotify already suggests similar music at the playlist level, but I'd love to see recommendations based on the specific song I'm currently working with while editing the playlist.

One reason this would be so valuable is because the Smart Reorder feature can only work with the songs already in the playlist. If my playlist doesn't have a wide enough variety of compatible songs, Smart Reorder does its best but is inevitably forced to place tracks with poor BPM, key, or energy compatibility next to each other.

At that point, my workflow becomes trial and error. I have to manually search through my library and add songs that I hope might bridge the transition, often without knowing their BPM or key ahead of time.

Instead, Spotify could suggest a handful of songs from my Liked Songs (or even my library) that are highly compatible with the song I'm currently trying to transition from or to. Compatibility could consider BPM, key, energy, genre, mood, and listening history.

This would save a huge amount of time spent scrolling through hundreds of saved tracks trying to remember what might fit. Since Spotify already has detailed audio analysis for my library, it seems like a natural extension of the existing recommendation system.

Ideally, this could appear as a "Suggested Transition Songs" or "Bridge This Transition" section while editing playlist order. Rather than just optimizing the songs already in the playlist, Spotify could proactively help fill the gaps by recommending tracks from my own library that create smoother transitions.
author: Peterhurst17

title: Feature Request: Separate Song Likes from Recommendation Preferences
body:
I love Spotify's recommendations and Smart Shuffle. Sometimes Spotify introduces me to songs that fit my current vibe perfectly, but I don't necessarily want to add them to my Liked Songs.

 

My Liked Songs playlist is a mix of different genres and moods. If I add every recommended song I enjoy, it starts to dilute the identity of that playlist.

 

What I'm missing is a way to tell Spotify: "I like this song and want more recommendations like it, but I don't want to save it to my library."

 

The current Like button does two things at once:

 

1. Saves a song to my collection.

2. Teaches the recommendation algorithm.

 

I think these should be separate actions.

 

For example, there could be a "Vibe" or "Keep in Rotation" button. Pressing it wouldn't add the song to Liked Songs, but it would tell Spotify that I enjoy this style, mood, and energy and want similar recommendations in the future.

 

Sometimes a song is perfect in the context of a specific mix, artist radio, or Smart Shuffle session. I love hearing it when Spotify brings it up, but it's not necessarily a song I want permanently added to my main playlist.

 

This feature would help Spotify understand not just what songs users save, but what vibes they connect with. I don't know if i clearly explained it..
author: Purple9

title: Community-Driven Tags for Individual Songs (Like Steam Game Tags)
body:
Spotify should add community tags to songs, similar to Steam tags.


Spotify's recommendation system is good, but discovering music by specific characteristics is still difficult.


A feature I'd love to see is community-driven tags for songs, similar to how Steam tags games. Users could add tags such as:


- Brostep
- Female vocals
- Melancholic
- Heavy bass
- Atmospheric
- Cybermetal

- Jazz

etc...


The most upvoted tags would appear on the song's page, and users could click any tag to find other songs with the same characteristics, exactly like how steam tags work.


This would make music discovery much more precise than relying solely on genres, playlists, and Spotify's algorithm.


Steam's tag system works extremely well for helping users find exactly what they're looking for, and I think a similar system could do the same for music.
author: T1mo777

title: Restore a permanent Recents row on the desktop Home page
body:
The desktop app used to provide quick and reliable access to recently played albums and playlists through a dedicated Recents section on the Home page. In recent versions, the Recents row appears inconsistently and often disappears altogether, making it much harder to return to content that users have recently been listening to.

I would like Spotify to restore a permanent, always-visible Recents row on the desktop Home page, similar to the experience offered in the mobile app. This section should remain available regardless of what recommendations, personalised playlists, podcasts, audiobooks or other promotional content Spotify chooses to display elsewhere on the Home page.

Many users use the Home page primarily as a starting point for returning to albums and playlists they already know and listen to regularly. Having to search through activity feeds or recommendation modules to find recently used content adds unnecessary friction to a task that should be immediate.

Please restore a dedicated, permanently visible Recents row on the desktop Home page.
author: hfsbro

title: Artist and Genre Algorithm needs improvement
body:
Dear Spotify Team,

I have been a Spotify Premium subscriber for many years and have always appreciated the platform's ability to help me discover new music and artists. Unfortunately, over the past several months, I've noticed a significant decline in that experience.

Many of the playlists and recommendations being presented to me now feel repetitive and stale. Different playlists often contain many of the same songs, resulting in very little actual music discovery. Instead of being introduced to new artists or tracks, I find myself hearing the same selections over and over again.

Additionally, the genres and artists being recommended increasingly do not align with my listening habits. It feels as though the algorithm has become less accurate and less personalized than it once was. Rather than helping me explore new music within the genres I enjoy, it often suggests music that has little relevance to my tastes.

One of the primary reasons I subscribe to a music streaming service is to discover new music. Lately, that aspect of Spotify feels largely absent, and the overall listening experience has become predictable and, frankly, boring.

At the same time, subscription prices continue to increase. As a paying customer, I expect ongoing improvements to the service, particularly in areas like personalization and music discovery. Instead, I feel the experience has moved in the opposite direction.

I wanted to provide this feedback because I genuinely enjoy Spotify and would prefer to remain a customer. However, if the recommendation and discovery experience continues to decline, I will seriously consider exploring alternative streaming platforms that do a better job of introducing listeners to new music.

Thank you for taking the time to read my feedback. I hope it can be shared with the teams responsible for recommendations, playlist curation, and user experience.
author: mbotic1

title: Regional Focus: Allow users to select preferred locales/regions for algorithm recommendations
body:
Current Behaviour:
Right now, Spotify's automated discovery algorithms (Discover Weekly, Release Radar, Smart Shuffle, and Autoplay) default heavily toward mainstream global hits, which are overwhelmingly dominated by American artists. Even if a user’s personal listening history consists primarily of UK, European, or other regional music, the algorithm frequently overrides these habits to push US-centric music. There is currently no setting to specify which geographic music scenes you want to discover or prioritise.

My Suggestion for Improvement:
I propose adding a "Preferred Regions/Locales" setting within the app preferences, or a localized filter toggle directly on automated playlists.

This feature would allow users to select specific regions (e.g., "UK & Ireland", "Europe", "Nordic", etc.) that they want the algorithm to actively prioritize. Selecting a region would adjust the recommendation weights, preventing the feed from constantly defaulting to American charts.

As a global platform, Spotify should allow listeners to explicitly support and discover talent from their own regions or specific cultures they are interested in, rather than pushing a one-size-fits-all, US-heavy music feed.

title: Revert to old model for recommended tracks
body: Hi there, I have been wanting to give feedback for months. Something very bad is happening with your recommendation systems. Discovery and serendipity have dropped dramatically and it is horrible. It means I am in an echo chamber of music. Not only that, but another weird thing I have noticed is that when I put on my 60's/70's rock playlist, when it ends it moves onto recommended tracks. This used to continue on with the same taste profile of the playlist itself which often would surface music along the same profile and was a great outcome. author: GP-L

title: Block genres
body:
After six months of Discover Weekly trying to convert me, I have come to the conclusion that my Spotify account may be possessed by the spirit of Jesus Christ.

Every Monday I perform an exorcism by hiding tracks, skipping songs and blocking artists. Yet the following week He returns. Stronger. More determined. More CEDM.

Meanwhile, Daylist, AI DJ and my Mixes understand my taste perfectly. Only Discover Weekly seems convinced that my true calling is "Jesus Is The King (D&B Remix)".

My suggestion: please give users the option to block an entire genre from recommendations. Sometimes I don't dislike a few artists—I simply never want recommendations from a specific genre cluster.

For now I have blocked every Christian EDM artist I could find. If next week's Discover Weekly is still full of worship drum & bass, I will assume Spotify's recommendation engine has achieved sentience and is actively trying to save my soul.
author: JillesvA

title: Deep Discovery Mode (or Obscure Shuffle Mode)
body:
Deep Discovery Mode (or Obscure Shuffle Mode)

When enabled, Spotify would deliberately prioritise:

  • Deep cuts

  • B-sides

  • Album tracks

  • Live recordings

  • Demos

  • Rare releases

  • Lesser-played songs from artists we already follow

The goal would be to help users explore the full depth of an artist's catalogue rather than repeatedly hearing the same top 20–50 tracks.

Additional options could include:

  • "Play Entire Artist Catalogue"

  • "Exclude Top 50 Most Popular Songs"

  • "Deep Cuts Only"

  • "Shuffle All Releases"

  • "Show Hidden Gems"

Many of us subscribe because Spotify has access to millions of songs, yet the recommendation system often leads us back to the same familiar tracks. This feature would encourage genuine music discovery while helping listeners reconnect with forgotten and lesser-known material from artists they already enjoy.

I would use this feature every day.
author: Tkik

title: Formal Complaint Regarding Spotify Duo Address Verification Process and Customer Support Advice
body:
Plan

Duo

Country

Uk

Device

IPhone 15, IPad

 

My Question or Issue

Dear Spotify Customer Support,

I am writing to formally complain about the address verification process for Spotify Duo and the advice I received from your customer support team.

While attempting to add my partner to my Spotify Duo subscription, I was required to enter my residential address. However, my actual address does not appear in your system’s list of available addresses. After following several troubleshooting steps suggested by your support representatives, I was ultimately advised to select the nearest available address, despite it being located in a different town and not representing my actual place of residence.

I find this recommendation concerning for several reasons:

  1. It requires customers to provide inaccurate personal information in order to access a service they are paying for.
  2. It undermines the purpose of address verification for Duo accounts.
  3. It raises potential privacy and security concerns, as account information would no longer accurately reflect the user’s actual address.
  4. It places customers in a position where they may unknowingly breach Spotify’s own terms and conditions by entering incorrect details.

I believe the appropriate solution is for Spotify to correct and improve its address database and verification system, rather than instructing customers to provide false information.

I would appreciate a formal response addressing:

  • Why my valid residential address is not recognised by your system.
  • Whether Spotify considers it acceptable for customers to enter inaccurate address information.
  • What steps will be taken to correct this issue and prevent similar problems for other users.

I have been a paying customer and expect a system that accurately accommodates legitimate addresses and customer support guidance that complies with Spotify’s own policies.

I look forward to your response and a satisfactory resolution.

title: Re: Autoplay/ Radio not working properly
body:
I can confirm a similar issue on the same Spotify desktop version.

Plan:
Premium

Device:
PC

Operating System:
Windows 11 64-bit

Spotify version:
1.2.90.451.gb094aab0

The issue seems related to playback context handling after the recent Autoplay / Radio problems.

Scenario:
Artist discography playback.

Spotify recently improved the desktop experience by adding better Discography sorting, including the ability to sort albums from oldest to newest. This finally allowed users to listen through an artist's catalog chronologically.

Steps to reproduce:

  1. Open an artist page in the desktop app.

  2. Go to Discography.

  3. Sort albums from oldest to newest.

  4. Start playing the first album in the sorted discography.

  5. Wait until the album finishes.

Expected behavior:
Spotify should preserve the selected Discography playback context and continue through the artist's albums according to the chosen sorting.

Actual behavior:
Spotify leaves the Discography context and switches to recommendation / Artist Radio behavior instead.

This makes the recently added Discography sorting feature much less useful. The sorting works visually, but the playback queue no longer follows the user's selected context.

This is not a request to remove Autoplay or recommendations. They are useful features when intentionally selected.

The problem is that playback started from a sorted Discography view should behave differently from starting a radio or recommendation session.

Please check if the recent Autoplay / Radio changes affected Discography playback context handling.

author: Thorny2

title: AI DJ 2.0: Make AI DJ Feel Like a Real Radio Host
body:
Title: AI DJ 2.0 – A Truly Personal Radio Host Experience

 

I really enjoy Spotify AI DJ, but I believe it could become much more personal, immersive and intelligent.

 

My suggestions:

 

• When AI DJ talks about a song, it could mention both when the song was originally released and when the user listened to it the most. For example: “You listened to this song a lot in 2024, but it was originally released in the 1960s.” This would provide useful context and help users better understand the music they love.

 

• AI DJ could adapt more to the listener’s situation by taking into account factors such as the time of day, day of the week, season, weather conditions, driving mode and listening habits.

 

• Users could choose between a fixed DJ voice or an adaptive DJ mode. In adaptive mode, Spotify would automatically adjust the voice, tone and presentation style according to the music being played, the time of day, the season and the listener’s habits. The goal is not simply to change voices, but to make the DJ feel natural and appropriate to the moment, like a real radio host specialized in that type of music. Users who prefer a single voice could simply keep the fixed voice option.

 

• Users could choose how much the DJ talks: minimal, standard or radio-style commentary.

 

• Add an optional “Mix Radio” mode. This mode would create a personal radio station built primarily from the user’s playlists, liked songs and listening history, while occasionally introducing carefully selected discoveries. The DJ would introduce tracks, create transitions and share short stories or insights, making the experience feel like a genuine radio show rather than an algorithmic playlist.

 

The overall goal is to make AI DJ feel less like a recommendation engine and more like a truly personal radio host that understands both the listener and the moment. It should not only know what music we like, but also understand when, why and in which context we enjoy listening to it.
author: Max209

title: Discography listening experience feels undermined by current playback behavior
body:
Hello Spotify Team,

I would like to share feedback regarding the current artist and album listening experience in the desktop application.

For years, many users requested better discography browsing, especially the ability to view and explore releases in chronological order. Spotify's community forums contain years of feedback asking for proper oldest-to-newest sorting, and more recently improvements were introduced to make discography navigation much better.

As someone who enjoys discovering artists through their complete body of work, this was exactly the kind of improvement I had been waiting for.

Unfortunately, the value of these changes now feels reduced because the playback experience increasingly pushes listeners away from album-focused listening.

I often want to start with an artist's earliest album and continue through their catalog, album by album, experiencing their evolution over time. The recent addition of chronological sorting finally made that possible from a navigation perspective.

However, when playback behavior starts steering users toward autoplay, artist radio, recommendations, or algorithm-generated content, it works against the purpose of exploring a discography in the first place.

If I open an artist page, sort releases from oldest to newest, and start listening to albums, my intention is very clear: I want to listen to that artist's albums.

Not radio.

Not recommendations.

Not a personalized stream.

If I want artist radio, I will deliberately start artist radio.

What makes this especially frustrating is that Spotify has already invested effort into improving discography organization and chronological browsing. Those were positive changes that acknowledged how many listeners still care about albums as complete works. The playback experience should reinforce that goal rather than interrupt it.

I would love to see Spotify continue improving album-focused listening by:

  • Supporting seamless album-to-album discography playback.

  • Respecting chronological listening workflows.

  • Keeping album listening clearly separated from recommendation-driven listening.

  • Bringing advanced discography features to mobile apps rather than simplifying desktop behavior toward a more radio-focused experience.

Albums are one of the foundations of music culture. Many listeners still enjoy experiencing music the way artists released it, one record at a time.

Thank you for your consideration.

Plan

Premium

Country

PL

 

Operating System

(iWindows 11)

 

My Question or Issue
author: mikcio2

title: One-Tap Surprise Play
body:
A very simple feature that, as a long-time Spotify user, I truly feel is missing: a single big Play button when opening the app.

 

Sometimes I don’t want to choose a playlist, genre, artist, or album. I don’t want to think about what to play. I just want to open Spotify, tap one button, and let the app surprise me.

 

The idea would be for Spotify to instantly start playing a smart mix of songs based on my taste — favorite tracks, forgotten gems, new discoveries, different genres, old and new songs mixed together — creating a personalized “just press play” experience.

 

Something effortless, spontaneous, and refreshing.
A feature for users who trust Spotify’s recommendation system and simply want music without decision fatigue.

 

Just one simple button: PLAY.

author: _stan_

title: ​New Ad Format: "Sponsored Songs" for Free Users to Discover New Artists
body:
I have an idea for a new advertisement model that could improve the Free tier experience, generate a new revenue stream, and solve the "discoverability" issue for emerging artists.

Currently, non-Premium users listen to traditional commercial audio ads (brands, products, etc.) between songs.

​My proposal is to introduce a hybrid ad format where independent artists or record labels can purchase ad slots to promote their tracks.

​How it works: While a Free user is listening to music, the upcoming ad break could consist of a 30-second highlight or a full track from an emerging artist whose style matches that user's taste profile (leveraging Spotify's recommendation algorithm).

Why This is a Win-Win-Win:

​For Free Users: Instead of getting frustrated by disruptive commercial ads, users stay in a "listening mood." The ad break turns from an interruption into a discovery zone.

​For Artists & Labels: It creates a direct-to-consumer ad marketplace. Artists can visually and audibly pitch their music straight to their target audience inside the app.

​For Spotify: It opens up a massive new advertising revenue stream directly powered by the music industry itself.

​To ensure this doesn't discourage users from upgrading to Premium (since the Free tier might become "too pleasant"), Spotify could:

1-Limit these "sponsored songs" to a maximum of 1 or 2 per hour.

2-​Only play a 30-to-60-second preview of the sponsored track, prompting the user with a "Click to add to your library" or "Listen to the full song" button.

3-​Combine them dynamically with existing commercial ads.

​I think this aligns perfectly with Spotify’s mission to help artists live off their art while keeping listeners engaged.

​What do you all think? If you'd like to see this feature, please drop an upvote!
author: emirianyigit

title: Add an option to pause briefly before Autoplay starts after an album
body:
When Autoplay is enabled, Spotify automatically starts playing recommended songs after the last track of an album. This is useful, but when listening to a full album, it can be hard to tell by ear when the album itself has ended and when Autoplay has started.

This is especially noticeable when listening while working, commuting, or using a Bluetooth speaker or smart speaker. In those situations, I often listen without looking at the screen, so I may not realize that the album has ended and that Spotify has already moved on to recommended tracks.

My idea is to add an optional setting that inserts a short pause before Autoplay starts after an album finishes.

For example, after the final track of an album ends, Spotify could wait a few seconds before starting the first Autoplay recommendation. This would make it much easier to notice that the album has finished, without having to turn Autoplay off completely.

I do not want to disable Autoplay, because it is useful for discovering new music and keeping playback going. I would just like a clearer audible separation between the album and the Autoplay recommendations.

This would improve the experience for users who enjoy listening to full albums, while still keeping Autoplay available for continuous listening.
author: guijty15699

title: Allow users to pin homepage recommendation trays
body:
Sometimes you open up Spotify and there's a recommendation tray you really like. Today I got "Albums featuring songs you like". But then you refresh the page, or come back the next day, and it's nowhere to be found!

 

My suggestion is to give users the ability to pin these trays. It would be great to slowly customise our homepage to our liking, to find the artists and music we enjoy. And I'd encourage a limit on this so users are constantly exposed to new recs and trays. But just regularly being able to check if there's new music in one of these categories would be class.
author: NedInYaHead

title: Genre-Based Recommendation Algorithm Improvement
body:
Since January 1, 2025, Spotify’s recommendation system has become less accurate for many users. Genres that users actually listen to are no longer properly recognized in listening statistics and personalized recommendations.

 

For example, I mainly listen to Uzbek Pop, Tajik Pop, and Persian Pop, but Spotify now recommends unrelated European, Turkish, and Arabic genres in the “Made For You” section and personalized playlists. Because of incorrect genre detection, recommendations no longer reflect real listening habits like before.

 

I suggest making genre one of the main priorities in the recommendation algorithm again. Spotify should focus more on users’ real favorite genres and long-term listening history to improve recommendation accuracy.

 

Suggested improvements: — restore previously recognized user genres; — improve genre classification accuracy; — remove unrelated genres from user statistics; — improve personalized playlists and discovery; — improve recommendations for Central Asian and Persian Pop listeners.

 

This would help make Spotify recommendations more accurate and personalized again.
author: OBIDJON_ABDIEV

title: Smart Heart Rate Adaptive Music & Sleep Feature
body:
I think Spotify could create a really innovative feature by integrating heart rate data from smartwatches like Apple Watch, Galaxy Watch, Pixel Watch, Fitbit, and others directly into the recommendation algorithm.

The idea is that Spotify could analyze a user’s real-time heart rate and activity level to intelligently adjust upcoming songs. For example:

- During workouts, if the user’s heart rate is rising, Spotify could automatically shift toward higher-energy songs with faster BPM to maintain motivation.
- If the user’s heart rate begins calming down, the app could transition into smoother or more relaxing tracks.
- While studying or focusing, Spotify could detect stress spikes and subtly adjust playlists toward calmer music.
- During nighttime listening, Spotify could combine heart rate trends with sleep detection to create a smarter sleep timer:
- gradually lower tempo and volume,
- fade into ambient or sleep sounds,
- automatically stop playback once the watch detects the user is asleep.

This could make Spotify feel more personalized and “alive,” almost like the music understands the listener’s physical state in real time.

Potential feature names:

- Spotify Pulse
- Adaptive Listening
- BioSync
- HeartBeat Mix
- MoodFlow

This could also work well with Spotify Wrapped by showing insights like:

- “Songs that lowered your heart rate the most”
- “Your highest-energy workout tracks”
- “Music that helped you fall asleep fastest”

I think this would make Spotify stand out as a next-generation AI-powered music experience rather than just a streaming platform.
author: Monsoon06

title: Playlist Creation mode toggle to restrict to USERS LIBRARY only
body:
Hi Spotify Team,

I love Spotify’s playlist-building and recommendation features, especially the newer AI/natural-language playlist creation tools. They’re already great at understanding mood, activity, genre, and overall vibe.

One feature I would really love to see improved is the ability to create playlists that are strictly limited to a user’s own library — especially Liked Songs or specific personal playlists.

For example, I wanted to create an upbeat “house cleaning” playlist using only songs from my Liked Songs, with certain artists included. Instead, the generated playlist seemed to pull mostly from recently played songs or recently added liked songs, and it did not reliably include the requested artist.

It would be extremely useful to have options like:

  • “Create this playlist from my Liked Songs only”
  • “Create this playlist from this specific playlist only”
  • “Use my library as the only source”
  • “Do not add songs outside my saved songs/playlists”
  • “Prioritize songs I already know and like, not new recommendations”

Spotify is already excellent at building mood-based playlists. Adding a true library-restricted mode would make the feature much more practical for people who want curated playlists from music they already love, rather than broader recommendations.

Thanks for considering it!
author: JoVersify

title: MTV-Style 1980s Music Video Channels / “Linear Nostalgia” Mode
body:
Spotify’s AI DJ proves there’s strong interest in guided, curated listening experiences that go beyond playlists. I’d love to see Spotify take that idea further with optional linear “music television” channels inspired by early MTV and MuchMusic.

 

Not just playlists, but actual always-on channels:

  • MTV 1983
  • MTV 1985
  • 120 Minutes
  • Alternative Nation
  • MuchMusic 1989

Complete with:

  • continuous music-video playback
  • era-authentic sequencing
  • retro branding/bumpers
  • VJ-style interstitials
  • themed programming blocks
  • passive “what’s on right now?” viewing

The appeal is not just nostalgia for the songs. It’s nostalgia for discovery, pacing, atmosphere, and shared cultural flow. Modern streaming is highly personalized, but many people miss simply turning something on and letting curated music culture happen around them.

 

This feels like a natural evolution of/companion to Spotify’s AI DJ concept: less about algorithmic recommendation alone and more about immersive music-era experiences.

Younger audiences are already gravitating toward retro streams, VHS aesthetics, and ambient TV experiences, while Gen X and older millennials would likely leave channels like this running for hours.

 

A partnership with legacy brands like MTV could make this a genuinely unique streaming feature.

author: Velveteen

title: Stop using AI - The product is a shell of itself
body:
I am writing as a long‑time user who is deeply frustrated by the direction your product has taken. What was once an excellent music discovery and listening platform has been materially degraded by your increasing reliance on opaque, intrusive AI-driven algorithms.

Your recommendation systems no longer serve the listener. Instead of enhancing discovery, they override user intent, repeatedly surface unwanted content, recycle the same tracks, and prioritize algorithmic guesswork over explicit user preferences. Even direct actions—likes, follows, skips, and manual playlist curation—are routinely ignored.

The result is a product experience that feels manipulative rather than personalized. Spotify now behaves as though it knows better than the user, forcing a narrow, repetitive interpretation of taste instead of allowing organic exploration. The irony is hard to miss: the more “intelligent” the system claims to be, the less control the user actually has.

This is not innovation. It is erosion of trust.

If these changes are intentional, then Spotify has lost sight of why people chose the platform in the first place. If they are not intentional, then that points to a more serious lack of quality control and product accountability.

Either way, the current experience is actively worse than it was years ago. I strongly encourage your product and leadership teams to reassess the overreach of your AI systems and restore meaningful user control before further damaging what was once a genuinely great service.

author: OBIDJON_ABDIEV

title: Expand MCP Server capabilities for AI assistant integration (Claude, etc.)
body:
Hi Spotify Team,

I've been using the Spotify MCP (Model Context Protocol) connector with Claude AI, and while I appreciate the integration, the current feature set feels quite limited compared to what the Spotify Web API actually offers.

Currently available via MCP:

  • Search (tracks, artists, albums, playlists, podcasts)
  • Create new playlists from natural language descriptions
  • Read listening history and library

What's missing — and what would make this integration genuinely useful:

  1. Playback control — Play/pause, skip, set volume, play a specific track/album/artist/playlist on an active device. This is the most obvious gap. Being able to say "play Dark Side of the Moon on my living room speaker" is the core value proposition of an AI assistant integration.
  2. Playlist editing — Add tracks to existing playlists, remove tracks, reorder. Currently I can only create new playlists from a prompt, but cannot modify them or any others.
  3. Read playlist contents — Retrieve the actual track list of a playlist (mine or shared ones). Without this, the AI can see that a playlist exists but cannot work with its contents — making tasks like "copy my songs from this collaborative playlist" impossible.
  4. Library management — Save/unsave tracks, follow/unfollow artists, save albums.
  5. Queue management — Add tracks to the playback queue, view current queue.

Why this matters: Right now the MCP connector is essentially a search-and-recommendation tool with a "create playlist from vibe" feature on top. That's nice, but it doesn't take advantage of what makes AI assistants powerful: handling multi-step tasks across the user's actual Spotify data. The Web API already supports all of the above for Premium users — the limitation is purely in what the MCP server exposes.

Competing AI assistant integrations (and direct Web API access) offer these features. It would be great to see Spotify lead here rather than lag behind.

Thanks for considering!

author: Minska

title: Personal Radio - Sync Listening with Friends
body:
Imagine turning every Spotify Premium user into their own live radio station.

Introducing Personal Radio, a feature that allows your friends and followers to tune directly into whatever you’re listening to in real time. Whether it’s a perfectly curated late-night playlist, a new album obsession, or a podcast episode you can’t stop recommending, your followers can instantly “drop in” and listen along from wherever they are.

Users could build an audience around their taste, gaining followers who receive notifications or can see when their station is live. A lightweight social layer could show who’s currently tuned in, creating the feeling of live communal listening without the pressure of active hosting.

The experience would remain fully user-controlled:

  • A simple privacy toggle enables “private listening” whenever desired.
  • Users choose who can tune in by approving followers or friends.
  • Music and podcasts stream live through the station feature.
  • Audiobooks remain private; if a listener tunes in during audiobook playback, they instead see a message displaying the title currently being listened to and are invited to check back later.

This would transform Spotify from a primarily individual listening platform into a more interactive social discovery experience; reviving the feeling of live radio, shared music culture, and organic recommendation in a modern, personalised way.

Exclusively available for Spotify Premium subscribers, Personal Radio could become a major retention and engagement feature, encouraging users not just to listen, but to broadcast their taste and build communities around it.

title: Replace Search with a "Shuffle" button in the bottom bar swipe down to access Search.  Category: Mob
body:
Summary

Replace the Search icon in the bottom navigation bar with a Shuffle button that plays a continuous, personalized stream of music — similar to Apple Music's "Station" or radio feature. Search would still be one swipe away: pulling down on the Shuffle banner reveals the current Search view, unchanged.

The problem

A lot of the time I open Spotify, I don't actually know what I want to listen to. I just want music to start. Right now this requires several taps:

  1. Open the app
  2. Tap Search (or Home)
  3. Scroll to find a Daily Mix, a playlist, or "Made For You"
  4. Tap to start

Apple Music's radio/station feature solves this with one tap: instant, personalized, lean-back listening. Spotify has the data and recommendation quality to do this even better — it just lacks a single dedicated entry point.

Meanwhile, Search is a deliberate action. When I want to search, I already know what I'm looking for. It doesn't need to be the default state of the bottom bar for the "I just want music" moments.

The proposal

Bottom bar change: Replace the current Search tab with a Shuffle tab (icon: shuffle arrows or a play-style symbol).

Behavior on tap: Instantly starts a personalized, endless mix based on listening history, recent likes, and time of day — no decisions required. Think of it as "Daily Mix, but infinite and one tap away."

Accessing Search: The Shuffle screen would behave like a banner / pull-down sheet. A small handle or chevron at the top indicates it's swipeable. Swipe down → the full current Search experience appears, exactly as it works today (Browse All, recent searches, search bar, genres). Swipe up or tap a result to dismiss back to Shuffle.

Mockup description:

  • Bottom bar: Home · Shuffle (new, replacing Search) · Your Library · Premium
  • Tap Shuffle → full-screen now-playing-style view with the song that just started, a "Why this track?" hint, and skip/like controls
  • Top of the Shuffle screen: a thin pull-tab or chevron icon (↓) suggesting downward swipe
  • Swipe down → Search slides in from the top as a sheet, covering Shuffle. The shuffle session keeps playing in the background.
  • Swipe up on the Search sheet (or tap a search result) → returns to Shuffle

Use cases

  1. Commuting / driving — open app, one tap, music starts. No browsing.
  2. Working or studying — want background music without committing to a specific playlist or mood.
  3. Discovery — Shuffle pulls from edges of taste profile, surfacing tracks the user wouldn't actively search for.
  4. Social settings — at a party or with friends, host doesn't have to curate — Shuffle handles it.
  5. Decision fatigue — the "I don't know what I want" moments where users currently end up replaying the same playlist.

Why this is better than current options

  • Smart Shuffle exists, but it's buried inside a playlist — you have to first pick a playlist, which defeats the point.
  • Daily Mixes require navigating to Home and scrolling.
  • DJ is great but is a specific, narrated experience — not the same as a clean, instant mix.

This idea is about a single tap from anywhere in the app to instant, personalized music, plus keeping Search fully accessible with a natural gesture.

Why move Search rather than add a new tab?

  • The bottom bar is already crowded; adding a 5th tab hurts ergonomics.
  • Search is a goal-directed action — users who want it know they want it, and a swipe gesture is a small price for that intent.
  • Lean-back listening is the more frequent state for many users and deserves the more prominent slot.

Optional refinements

  • Long-press on Shuffle → choose a mood/genre seed (chill, hype, focus, throwback)
  • A small "Tune" control on the Shuffle screen (familiar/adventurous slider, like Smart Shuffle already has)
  • Setting to opt out and restore Search to the bottom bar for power users who prefer the current layout

TL;DR: Replace Search in the bottom bar with a Shuffle button that instantly plays a personalized mix. Swipe down on Shuffle to reveal Search exactly as it is today. One tap to listen, one swipe to search.

title: Request for Improved Audio Quality & Loudness Control
body:
Subject: Request for Improved Audio Quality & Loudness Control

 

Dear Spotify Team,

 

I appreciate the platform and its excellent recommendation system, but I’d like to highlight concerns regarding audio quality and perceived loudness.

 

Currently, Spotify’s loudness normalization and compression can make tracks feel less dynamic and quieter compared to competitors like Apple Music, youtube music especially at maximum volume. This affects the listening experience for users who prioritize sound clarity and impact.

 

I would strongly suggest the following improvements:

 

1. Introduce a true Hi-Fi / lossless audio tier with higher bitrate streaming options.

2. Provide more granular control over loudness normalization (e.g., customizable LUFS targets or a full bypass option).

3. Enhance dynamic range preservation, allowing music to retain its original punch and depth.

4. Offer clearer in-app audio diagnostics/settings guidance so users can optimize playback easily.

 

These changes would significantly improve Spotify’s competitiveness in audio quality while maintaining its strengths in discovery and usability.

 

Thank you for your continued innovation and for considering user feedback.

 

Sincerely,

A Spotify User
author: ImeTs

title: Give users more control over podcast recommendations and reduce clickbait-style content
body:
Feedback:
One of the things I value about Spotify is the possibility of discovering high-quality music, podcasts, and long-form audio. But I’m increasingly concerned that the recommendation experience, especially around podcasts and video podcasts, is moving toward the same attention-maximizing pattern as YouTube, Instagram, or TikTok.

I do not want Spotify to become another addictive feed optimized for whatever is most clickable, sensational, or emotionally engaging. I want recommendations that respect my intent, my time, and my intellectual standards.

A recurring issue is that low-quality, clickbait-style podcasts or videos are recommended repeatedly. By “low quality,” I mean content that feels sensationalized, shallow, or not intellectually rigorous, even if it is highly engaging or popular. I would genuinely appreciate strong recommendations for thoughtful, well-produced, rigorous podcasts — but that is often not what I am seeing. The recommendations can feel like they are drifting toward the lowest common denominator.

What I would like:
- More control over the types of podcast channels and topics that are recommended.
- A way to say “show me less content like this,” not just hide one individual item.
- Controls for excluding certain formats, channels, topics, creators, or recommendation styles.
- An option to prioritize higher-quality, more thoughtful, less clickbait-driven recommendations.
- Better feedback tools that let users actively shape the recommendation system around their own values and listening goals.

The underlying issue is not that I dislike recommendations. I actually want good recommendations. But I want recommendations that feel aligned with my values and intentions, rather than recommendations that seem optimized for compulsive engagement.

I want to emphasize that I broadly love Spotify and would much rather see this improved than leave the product. But this issue matters enough to me that I have already had to aggressively delete apps or install extensions for other platforms to protect my attention. If Spotify moves further toward an addictive recommendation-feed model without giving users strong in-app controls, I may eventually be forced to cancel my subscription, even though I would prefer not to.

A good outcome would be:
When I open Spotify, I should feel like the product is helping me listen intentionally — not nudging me into the same kind of addictive browsing loop that exists on YouTube, Instagram, and TikTok.

author: Lev_Voloshin_MD

title: Full Account Reset Option (Clean Slate Mode)
body:
Idea Description:

I’d like to suggest a feature that allows users to completely reset their Spotify account to a “brand new” state.

Right now, recommendations, liked songs, playlists, and listening history all accumulate over time. This becomes problematic when:

  • An account has been shared with others who have very different music tastes
  • A user wants to completely restart their music journey
  • The account’s data no longer reflects the user’s preferences at all

In these cases, there is currently no way to fully start fresh without creating a new account.

Proposed Solution:
Introduce a “Clean Slate” or “Full Account Reset” option in account settings that:

  • Deletes all listening history
  • Removes all liked songs
  • Deletes or clears all playlists
  • Resets recommendations (Discover Weekly, Daily Mixes, Release Radar, etc.)
  • Restores the account to a true “new user” state

Optional Safeguards:

  • A clear warning and confirmation step before reset
  • Option to export or back up playlists before deletion

Benefits:

  • Gives users full control over their account and data
  • Eliminates the need to create a new account just to start fresh
  • Helps users who previously shared accounts
  • Improves recommendation accuracy from a clean starting point

This feature would provide a simple, user-friendly way to completely restart the Spotify experience without losing the account itself.

Thanks for considering this idea!
author: rj_devil_killa_91

title: Option to Reset Listening History & Recommendations
body:
Idea Description:

I’d like to suggest a feature that allows users to fully reset their listening history and music recommendations on Spotify.

Currently, recommendations such as Discover Weekly, Release Radar, and Home feed suggestions are heavily influenced by past listening activity. This becomes an issue in cases where:

  • An account is shared between multiple users with very different music tastes
  • A user’s preferences significantly change over time
  • The account has been used temporarily by someone else

In such situations, recommendations become inaccurate and no longer reflect the primary user’s preferences.

Proposed Solution:
Introduce a “Reset Listening History” or “Start Fresh” option in account settings that:

  • Clears all listening history
  • Resets algorithmic recommendations (Discover Weekly, Daily Mixes, etc.)
  • Optionally allows users to retain saved songs and playlists

Benefits:

  • Gives users control over their recommendation system
  • Improves personalization accuracy
  • Helps users who share accounts or undergo taste changes
  • Enhances overall user satisfaction

Additional Suggestion (Optional):
Spotify could also consider introducing separate user profiles under one Premium account, similar to other streaming platforms, to avoid this issue altogether.

Thanks for considering this feature!
author: rj_devil_killa_91

title: Add option to lock queue order in Jam sessions
body:
Spotify Jam is a great idea, but the current behavior makes it very frustrating to use when you actually care about song order.
Right now, if someone taps a song instead of selecting “Add to queue,” Spotify replaces or rebuilds the entire queue based on that song. This can completely override the original playlist or queue without warning. There is no undo, and it’s very easy to trigger accidentally.
This is especially problematic when a Jam is started from a playlist. Users expect the playlist order to remain intact, with additional songs simply being added to the queue—not for the entire session to shift into a radio-style recommendation mode.
Please consider adding:

 

  • A “Lock Queue” or “Preserve Playlist Order” option for Jam sessions
  • A confirmation prompt before replacing the current queue
    A clear distinction between “Play Now (replace queue)” and “Add to queue” in Jam
  • An undo option after queue replacement


Jam is great for collaboration, but it should not come at the cost of losing control over playback order.
Thank you.
author: kuracha

title: Re: Request for Extended API Access for AI-Based Spotify Thesis Project
body:
Hi @HrissTsvetanova 
Thanks for sharing your project. It's clear you've put a lot of work into it already!
There are just a couple of important limitations to be aware of as you move toward the next stage:

  • You mentioned future ideas like "Hey Spotify, recommend me tracks for chilling with friends at home." Spotify's Developer Policy doesn't allow building a voice‑enabled assistant that can control Spotify (section III.3).
  • Several of your current and planned features rely on training AI or machine‑learning models using Spotify data or example mood detection, genre inference, and advanced recommendation logic. The policy explicitly prohibits using Spotify content or metadata to train or feed AI/ML models (section III.14).
  • About your question on extended or academic access: Spotify doesn't offer special research‑level access or broader catalog data beyond what’s available through the public Web API, so there's unfortunately no path to request deeper catalog‑wide metadata.

You can definitely keep experimenting with the data the API already provides, but anything involving voice control or AI models trained on Spotify data would fall outside what’s allowed at the moment.

Wishing you the best with the rest of your thesis!
author: Ximzend

title: Request for Extended API Access for AI-Based Spotify Thesis Project
body:
Hello,

I am currently developing an AI-powered music assistant as part of my university thesis project, focused on enhancing the way users interact with Spotify through intelligent analysis, recommendations, and conversational interaction.

At this stage, my system is already functional and operates using the data currently available through Spotify’s API (primarily liked/saved tracks). Based on this data, I have implemented the following capabilities:

  • Storage and structuring of liked tracks, including artist, album, release year, and metadata
  • Automatic genre inference (especially for Drum & Bass subgenres such as neurofunk, jungle, liquid, techstep, etc.)
  • Mood detection based on audio features (energy, valence, danceability, acousticness, etc.)
  • Classification of tracks into mood categories such as: aggressive, melodic, atmospheric, energetic, uplifting, dark, etc.
  • Sorting and organizing tracks by genre, mood, and historical period
  • Identification of most frequent (top) artists based on listening data
  • Basic recommendation engine based on:
    • genre (e.g. "recommend neurofunk tracks")
    • mood (e.g. "I want something aggressive")
    • combined mood + genre queries

Additionally, I have implemented a natural-language query system that supports prompts such as:

"show all my Spotify tracks sorted by genre"

"sort all tracks in my Spotify liked songs by genre"

"recommend tracks"

"suggest jungle tracks"

"recommend me drum and bass classic"

"recommend me drum and bass only"

"I want something aggressive"

"I want something melodic"

"I'm in the mood for dark Drum"

"suggest something atmospheric"

"recommend emotional liquid"

"I want energetic jungle"

"which artist appears most in my liked songs"

"what Drum and Bass genres exist"

"when did jungle appear"

The system also supports historical genre analysis and era detection, allowing users to explore the origins and evolution of specific styles.

 

The next stage of my project (currently conceptual and partially designed) includes:

  • A voice-based AI assistant (similar to Alexa), for example:

"Hey Spotify, recommend me tracks for chilling with friends at home."

"Hey Spotify, give me music for a late-night party."

"Hey Spotify, what did I listen to most this week?"

  • Advanced mood and atmosphere detection based on listening patterns and context
  • Weekly and monthly listening recaps (beyond the current annual Spotify recap)
  • Discovery of new artists based on deep preference analysis
  • Context-aware playlist generation (e.g. social events, studying, nightlife, etc.)
  • More advanced recommendation logic using full catalog data
  • Potential future extension: intelligent mixing / track sequencing (for advanced usage)

 

However, in order to fully realize these ideas, I would need broader access to Spotify data.

At the moment, my system is limited to what Spotify currently allows through the API (mainly liked songs). While this is sufficient for building a prototype, it significantly restricts the ability to:

  • recommend new tracks outside of the liked library
  • analyze the full spectrum of available genres and artists
  • build more advanced AI-driven discovery features
  • implement deeper personalization and context-aware recommendations

For this reason, I would like to ask:

  • Is it possible to obtain extended access to Spotify’s data for research or academic purposes?
  • Are there programs, partnerships, or special permissions for thesis-level or experimental AI projects?
  • What would be the appropriate path to request broader metadata or catalog-level access?

I believe this project aligns with the future direction of music platforms toward more intelligent, conversational, and personalized interaction with music.

Thank you very much for your time and consideration. I would be happy to provide additional details, documentation, or a demonstration of the system if needed.

Kind regards,
Hristina

title: Feature Proposal: Context-Aware Al Playlists via Natural Language
body:
Dear Spotify Team,

​I am a dedicated long-time user of your platform. While I appreciate the continuous innovation at Spotify, I frequently encounter a specific pain point: the current recommendation algorithms often struggle with nuanced transitions in mood and language within a single playlist. It is frustrating to have a high-energy track followed by a melancholy one when the "vibe" should remain consistent.

​The Proposal:

I suggest implementing a feature that allows users to generate playlists using natural language prompts. A user should be able to specify exact requirements, such as: "A chill 90s British rock selection for deep work, instrumental only, excluding any high-tempo tracks."

​Integration with Advanced AI:

To streamline this, rather than building a proprietary model from scratch, Spotify could integrate with state-of-the-art AI models (like Gemini). These models are already capable of understanding complex human emotions, cultural contexts, and linguistic nuances. Combining Spotify’s massive music library with such "advanced brains" would create a seamless, near-perfect personalization experience that goes far beyond simple genre tagging.

​This integration would save development time and immediately offer users a level of curation that feels truly human.

​I would love to see this implemented and am open to discussing this further

Best regards ALEXEY
author: ALEXEY13

title: Suggestion for improving the recommendation model based on volume interaction
body:
Good morning, team,

I would like to share an idea to improve our music recommendation model. If something similar is already in place, please let me know. I believe it could still be a valuable complementary signal.

The proposal is to explore a very common and spontaneous user behavior: interaction with the volume control during music playback.

In general, when a song creates a stronger emotional connection, users tend to increase the volume. On the other hand, when the song is less engaging, they may lower it. These actions are often unconscious and can represent a relevant real-time signal of the user’s immediate perception of the listening experience.

In this context, the suggestion is to use volume variations, both increases and decreases, as an additional indicator of real-time emotional engagement.

The idea would be to capture these interactions and transform them into a complementary signal for the recommendation algorithms, enabling:

  • Identification of peaks of interest through volume increases
  • Detection of potential drops in engagement through volume decreases
  • A richer understanding of the user’s emotional response to each track

Unlike other established signals such as likes, skips, or playlist additions, this approach focuses on a more immediate and implicit user response, potentially adding a new layer of sensitivity to the model.

Thank you, and I look forward to your feedback.

Best regards,
Wanderson
author: WandersonDourado

title: Music-taste test to enhance personalisation
body:
Spotify should offer a dedicated “music taste test” to improve recommendation accuracy, since the current algorithm often feels inconsistent. The feature could live in its own tab and begin by showing users a stream of completely random songs. Instead of a simple like/dislike system, users would rate each song across five categories: “Horrible,” “Bad,” “Neutral,” “Good,” and “Great.”

 

The “Great” rating should carry especially high weight, because that is what users actually want to hear more of. The other ratings would help the algorithm learn the boundaries between levels of preference: what makes a song “Good” rather than “Great,” what separates “Neutral” from “Bad,” and which elements consistently trigger strong rejection or strong approval.

 

To make this genuinely effective, Spotify should not rely mainly on broad genres or categories. It should analyze finer details such as instrumentation, dynamics, rhythmic patterns, harmonic structure, complexity, tempo, and mood. The goal is for the system to understand taste at a deeper level, not just match surface-level labels. What this does? The algorithm takes into consideration the songs actual character, not their popularity, genre, trendiness etc. In short: What actually matters.

 

The test should also never stop completely. From time to time, Spotify should introduce new and slightly unfamiliar songs to broaden the user profile and prevent the algorithm from becoming too narrow. It could measure how long users listen, whether they skip quickly, and how they rate these more experimental recommendations. That would help the system learn not only what users already like, but also what they are open to discovering.

 

The format could be inspired by Instagram Reels: an endless, swipe-based flow of suggestions that becomes more personalized over time. The difference is that, instead of passive scrolling, it would be an active taste evaluation tool. In other words: continuous suggestions, more precise feedback, and deeper pattern recognition, combined with occasional novelty to keep the model from stagnating.

 

Why? Because then, Spotify can suggest Playlists by other users that fit the personalised taste profile best and in general suggest new songs more accurately. This would have huge effects, because accurate recommendations are THE key to a more satisfying experience. A big flaw right now, is the exaggerated repetition. People want NEW songs. It makes no sense to recommend what they know already, unless the last time they listened to them is years ago.

 

Spotify should furthermore distinguish between active and passive listening, because background playback currently distorts what the algorithm interprets as actual preference. That’s why my suggestion actually matters: the active rating design ensures representation of the users actual preferences.

 

The Core of my suggestions in summary:

Spotify should learn what users truly love — and why they love it.

 

1.Spotify should introduce a dedicated music taste test that collects explicit, high-quality feedback instead of relying mostly on passive listening data.

 

2.Users could rate random songs on a detailed scale from “Horrible” to “Great,” giving the algorithm a much clearer picture of real preference.

 

3.That matters because background listening can distort the data: a song played while studying, talking, or falling asleep is not the same as a song actively chosen and enjoyed.

 

4.Spotify should therefore distinguish between passive playback and genuine engagement, and weight the latter much more heavily.

 

5.At the same time, the system should go beyond broad genres and surface labels. It should analyze the actual musical structure of songs — instrumentation, dynamics, rhythm, harmony, tempo, complexity, and mood — so it understands taste at a deeper level rather than just matching categories or popularity.

 

6.The test should remain ongoing, with occasional unfamiliar songs to keep broadening the profile and reduce repetition.

 

Spoiler
In the end, the goal is simple: Spotify should stop guessing from noisy signals and instead learn true user preference with precision, depth, and variety.
author: birkfy07

title: Feature Proposal: Bi-Directional Sync – "Instagram Notes to Spotify Liked Songs" Pipeline
body:
The Vision

Currently, music discovery within Instagram Notes is a passive experience. When a user finds a track they love in a friend’s Note, they must manually memorize the song title, switch apps, search for the track, and then "Like" it. This multi-step friction causes significant "discovery drop-off." I am proposing a high-level integration where the "Save" action within Instagram Notes acts as a direct API trigger to the user's Spotify Liked Songs library.

Functional Overview: The "One-Tap" Pipeline

The Entry Point: Within the Instagram Notes interface, next to the music status, implement a dedicated Spotify "Save" Icon.

The Save Line: Once a user clicks "Save" on an Instagram Note, the song is added to their Instagram "Saved" collection. Simultaneously, through a background sync, that metadata is sent to the linked Spotify account.

The Result: The song instantly reflects in the user’s "Liked Songs" on Spotify. No searching, no app-switching, and no manual entry required.

Technical & Visual Implementation

Visual Confirmation: Upon tapping the save button in the Note, a high-fidelity animation (e.g., the Spotify heart icon turning green) should provide the user with immediate feedback that the track is now secured in their library.

Integrated "Save Line": For users who prefer to curate first, the Instagram "Saved" folder should feature a "Sync All to Liked Songs" toggle. This allows a batch-transfer of every song saved from Notes directly into a dedicated Spotify playlist or the Liked Songs queue.

Strategic Advantages for Spotify

Elimination of Friction: This creates a "frictionless funnel" from social discovery to platform consumption. The faster a song is liked, the faster it enters Spotify’s recommendation algorithm.

Data Enrichment: Spotify gains deeper insights into the social context of music discovery—understanding which tracks are trending in interpersonal "Notes" versus global charts.

User Retention: By becoming the "storage backend" for Instagram’s social features, Spotify solidifies itself as the primary destination for a user's musical identity.

Conclusion

This is not just a shortcut; it is a bridge between social interaction and music utility. By implementing this direct pipeline, Spotify transforms Instagram Notes from a static text field into a powerful, automated discovery tool that populates user libraries in real-time

title: 🎧 “Live Moments – Flow Mode”
body:
💡 Concept

Evolve the “Live Moment” idea from a single-song experience into a continuous, real-time listening flow.

Instead of a live session ending after one track, users can stay together in an ongoing stream of music — while still preserving the feeling of sharing specific emotional moments.

🔄 How It Works

1. Start a Live Session (“Go Live”)

User taps “Go Live” while listening to a song

This starts a shared listening session

Other users can join and listen in sync

2. Continuous Flow (Not Just One Song)

When the current track ends, the session continues automatically

The next track can come from:

The user’s queue

Or Spotify’s recommendation algorithm (based on vibe)

👉 The session becomes a Flow, not a one-time moment

🎯 Key Insight

People don’t just want to share a single song.

They want to share a feeling that lasts longer than one track.

🎶 “Moments Within the Flow”

Even though the session continues:

Each song acts as its own mini-moment

Users can join or leave at any time

Reactions reset or shift with each track

This keeps:

The emotional impact of individual songs

While allowing a deeper, ongoing experience

🫥 Low-Pressure Interaction

To avoid turning this into a social network:

No full chat system

No direct messaging

Instead:

💬 Minimal Reactions Only

Emojis (❤️ 💔 😶‍🌫️ 🔥)

Or one short sentence per user

Focus stays on the music, not conversation.

⏳ Optional Session Limits

To preserve the “lightweight” feeling:

Sessions can naturally end after a set time (e.g. 10–20 minutes)

Or when the host leaves

This prevents fatigue and keeps the experience intentional.

👀 Passive Presence

Users can see:

“X people are listening with you right now”

Even without joining, this creates a subtle sense of shared experience.

🔐 Privacy Options

Anonymous mode

Friends-only sessions

Public sessions

Users control their level of visibility.

🎯 Why This Works

Balances momentary connection with continuous experience

Keeps Spotify calm and personal (not a noisy social feed)

Allows users to share emotions without pressure to communicate

Creates a new form of “live listening” that feels human, not performative

title: Shared Listening on Buses and Trams with Social Connection for Spotify Users
body:
Live Listening Zones: A Real-Time, Location-Based Music Discovery and Social Layer for Spotify

Overview

Public transport environments such as trams, buses, and metro systems represent high-density clusters of music streaming users, particularly on Spotify. In these shared spaces, individuals are physically close yet remain digitally isolated, each engaged in their own listening experience. This creates a missed opportunity for shared discovery and interaction through music.

The concept of Live Listening Zones introduces a real-time, location-based feature within Spotify that transforms these environments into dynamic listening ecosystems. The feature enables users to discover what others around them are listening to, participate in shared playlists, and optionally connect through music in a controlled and privacy-conscious manner.

Problem Statement

Users often experience repetition and disengagement in their listening habits, particularly during routine activities such as commuting. While Spotify’s recommendation system is highly personalized, it remains largely individual-centric and does not incorporate real-time social or environmental context.

There is currently no mechanism within the platform to leverage ambient listening behavior in shared physical spaces. Existing social features, such as shared playlists or collaborative sessions, require prior connection and are not designed for spontaneous, situational interaction. As a result, Spotify does not fully utilize the potential of collective listening moments occurring in everyday environments.

Proposed Solution

Live Listening Zones introduces a set of features that allow users to engage with their immediate listening environment in an intuitive and optional manner.

In its most passive form, users can view anonymized, real-time insights into what people around them are listening to. This may include trending tracks within a tram or bus, or dominant genres in that specific environment. This mode requires no interaction and maintains complete privacy.

For users seeking a more interactive experience, the feature allows participation in a shared, real-time playlist within that location. Users can contribute songs, react to tracks, and influence the direction of the playlist as it evolves dynamically.

An additional layer introduces music taste matching between users in proximity. When enabled, the system identifies overlaps in listening preferences and notifies users of high compatibility. Interaction remains optional and is only enabled through mutual consent, ensuring that privacy and user control are maintained.

Value Proposition

For users, this feature enhances the listening experience by introducing variety, spontaneity, and a subtle sense of shared presence. It reduces monotony and creates opportunities for organic music discovery without requiring active social effort.

For Spotify, Live Listening Zones has the potential to increase user engagement and session duration by transforming passive listening moments into interactive experiences. It also provides a point of differentiation in a highly competitive streaming market by introducing context-aware listening. Additionally, it lays the groundwork for future monetization opportunities through premium features or sponsored listening environments.

Privacy and Safety Considerations

The feature is designed with privacy as a core principle. Participation is entirely optional, with default settings set to non-participation. No precise location data is shared with other users, and all presence within a listening zone is anonymized unless explicitly permitted.

User interactions are based on mutual consent, and all sessions are temporary, ensuring that no long-term exposure or unwanted connections occur. This approach aligns with user expectations in privacy-conscious markets such as Sweden.

Strategic Fit

Live Listening Zones aligns with Spotify’s broader strategic evolution from a content delivery platform to an experience-driven ecosystem. It extends personalization beyond the individual level and introduces situational awareness into the listening experience.

By integrating real-time social context in a lightweight and non-intrusive manner, the feature enhances user engagement without shifting Spotify’s core identity. It leverages existing strengths in recommendation algorithms while opening new pathways for innovation.

Future Opportunities

Sponsored zones can allow brands or artists to engage users in specific environments. Integration with artificial intelligence can further refine group listening experiences based on collective mood or behavior.

Additionally, the development of broader “live vibe maps” across cities could allow users to explore music trends geographically, further enhancing discovery.

Conclusion

In modern urban environments, particularly in countries like Sweden, individuals frequently share physical spaces while remaining digitally isolated. Live Listening Zones bridges this gap by introducing a low-friction, music-driven layer of connection.

By transforming everyday moments into shared experiences, this feature has the potential to redefine how users interact with music, their environment, and each other.

title: Backstage Intro - Meet Nate
body:
Our Community Stars can be featured in Backstage Intros. To find out more about the Star Program click here.

 

Hi everyone! This month, we're happy to introduce you the newest member of our Star team: Nate aka @JBayudz!

Hi, Nate! First of all, welcome to the band! Could you tell a bit more about yourself?

Sure... I'm Jonathan P. Bayudang. My friends call me Nathan or Nate. I work in the BPO industry as a customer service lead / client experience specialist, with several years of experience leading teams, coaching reps, and using tools like CRM and salesforce to solve messy customer problems in a structured way. Outside of work, I'm usually tinkering with new tools, learning something online, listening to music, or just hanging out with communities I'm part of.

 

That’s cool, Nate! Since you enjoy being part of communities, what motivated you to move from 'just a listener' to an active member here? Also, what makes you feel you truly belong in an online space?

I think what pushed me from just 'listening' to actually participating was realizing that communities are way more meaningful when you contribute. I realized I could learn more and enjoy by actually engaging. I feel like I belong in a community when, in addition to having the opportunity to connect with others with similar interests, people are supportive, curious, and open to different perspectives. If the vibe is positive and I feel comfortable speaking up, that’s home for me.

 

Since you’re into tinkering with new tools and learning online, has that curiosity ever led you to find a 'hidden' Spotify feature or a specific way of organizing things that most people might miss? What’s your favorite Spotify feature lately?

Yeah, it's not exactly a 'hidden' feature, but I stumbled across Spotify for devs and thought it was really cool. I even registered an app to try integrating it into one of my Discord bots. There are still a lot of limitations on what a bot can actually do with the Spotify API, so I couldn't build everything I imagined. I'm hoping they open up even more functionality in the future.

 

Impressive! Not everyone dives into the API and Dev docs... Since you’ve seen 'under the hood' of Spotify, if you had the power to add just one specific functionality to the API for your bot or tools, what would it be? What’s the dream feature you’re waiting for?

I like that question, and that one thing I would add (if I could), is the ability to stream lossless music directly from Spotify libraries. I would implement it to require authentication and limit it to premium subscribers only. Coz right now, based on my understanding, Spotify only acts as a 'middleman' or something like that. The Discord bot pulls data such as the song title and artist, Spotify will then find the exact song somewhere else, and then render the song back to the Discord bot to play. Adding this feature would be really really great.

 

That’s a great technical take, Nate! Since we’ve gone pretty deep into the API and features, let’s switch gears to some quick-fire questions to get to know 'Nate' outside the dev world. To start, are you a coffee or tea person? Also, do you prefer working in total silence or with a background playlist, and would you say you’re more of a strictly organized playlists type of guy or do you just hit shuffle on your 'Liked Songs' and let it ride? Oh, and are you an early bird or a night owl?

 

  1. Definitely a coffee person. Coffee for the win! ☕
  2. I prefer working in silence, but when I have no control over the environment or what's going on around me, I plug in my headset and listen to music while working.
  3. BOTH! I do let my 'liked songs' play in a shuffle most of the time, but I do have quite a few organized playlists too that I listen to some other time.
  4. I'm a night owl, though I'm also an early bird as I manage to arrive early at work. 😁

     

A coffee-fueled early bird who’s actually a night owl... That’s the ultimate BPO survival mode! Since you mentioned you use your headset as a ‘shield’ to focus at work, what’s that one go-to album or artist that always helps you lock in and get things done? We’d love a recommendation to wrap this up!

I don't have any particular album or artist, I have quite a few playlists saved and this is my go-to list. Would definitely recommend this one.

 

Thanks for letting us a bit more about you! It was nice!

 

Thanks for sticking around! Stay tuned for the next Backstage Intro coming soon.

Our Community Stars can be featured on Backstage Intros. To find out more about the Star Program click here. 

 

***

title: Music recommendations tailored to your mood using wearable fitness data.
body:
I had an idea about integrating Spotify with wearable fitness trackers such as Garmin, Fitbit or Whoop. These devices already track useful biometric data like heart rate, stress levels and physical activity, which provide insight into a user’s current mood and energy level. If this data could be synced with Spotify, the platform could learn what music people tend to listen to during different states, such as when they are exercising, relaxing, feeling stressed or feeling motivated.

Over time, Spotify could analyse these patterns and use them to improve the recommendations made by the Spotify DJ. For example, if the system recognises that a user is currently working out or experiencing higher stress levels, it could automatically suggest songs that match that activity or mood. This would create a more dynamic and personalised listening experience, where music recommendations adapt not just to listening history but also to a user’s real-time physical and emotional state.

By combining wearable technology with music streaming, Spotify could create a smarter recommendation system that better understands how music fits into people’s daily lives and activities.
