title: Add a "Clear Listening Data" Button to Reset Recommendations
body:
Currently, temporary listening habits—like playing white noise for sleep, kids' songs, or exploring a new genre briefly—permanently mess up the recommendation algorithm (Discover Weekly, Release Radar, etc.). Right now, there is no way to refresh our recommendations. We either have to endure irrelevant music for months or create a brand new account, which forces us to lose all our carefully curated playlists and liked songs.

I suggest adding a simple "Reset Recommendations" or "Clear Listening Data" button under Settings > Privacy or Account. Clicking this should wipe the historical listening data used for the algorithm, giving users a clean slate for their personalized mixes, while safely keeping all saved playlists, followed artists, and liked songs intact.

title: Re: February 2026 Spotify for Developers update: thread
body:
Hi Team,

 

I want to share a specific case study of how these changes are affecting a real business built on Spotify's ecosystem, because I think it illustrates a gap that might not be visible from the inside.

 

My name is Zack Fischmann. I've been a working musician for 25 years and a software engineer for 10. I have 5 albums on Spotify as an artist and have been using the app since ~2013. A few years ago I started a company called Magic Nothing to help independent artists navigate music marketing because all my music buddies were asking me for help. Our motto is Music Marketing Doesn't Have to Suck.

 

One of our biggest challenges has been helping artists get over their skepticism about Spotify and see what the platform has to offer. We've spent years doing that. We built a 76K organic follower community on Instagram around Spotify marketing education, helped thousands of artists design and launch ad campaigns that drive millions of people off social media and onto Spotify, and have been active advocates for the platform because we genuinely believe in what it offers artists. We built a business around this education work, and it became a full-time job within 2 weeks of announcing the project.

 

That work led us to build the Magic Nothing platform, a curation marketplace connecting independent artists with genuine playlist curators. We went to market on the dev mode API because we needed to move fast. In under 3 months we went from $1.5K to $16.7K in weekly revenue, with 600+ active curators and 3,500 artists on the platform. 50-75% of our revenue goes directly to curators, and everyone works together releasing, curating, and promoting music on Spotify to listeners all over the world. It's effectively a free advertising engine for Spotify that also rewards artists and quality curators.

 

Here's where it gets complicated.

 

On the same day Spotify for Developers announced these API changes, Spotify for Artists reached out to us about a paid partnership to create content promoting Spotify's tools. We're literally being asked by Spotify to champion the platform while simultaneously losing the API access that powers our platform's core intelligence. With these updates, we're losing playlist quality scoring, track verification, genre analysis, and our recommendation engine.

 

We've been building fallbacks for March 9. We'll survive. But the intelligence layer of our platform goes dark -- we lose the ability to verify that curators are providing legitimate information, to score playlist quality, and to match artists with the right playlists/curators. This ultimately pushes us away from Spotify in order to build replacements. 

 

The natural answer is Extended Quota Mode, but the application requires a minimum of 250K MAUs. We're at 15K according to Google Analytics. The form literally blocks submission below the 250k threshold. We don't want to lie to submit it.

 

We've spent weeks reaching out through every channel we can find. Friends and former colleagues at Spotify, the S4A team that initially reached out to us, developer community contacts. Every path leads back to the same form we can't submit. One of the developers who helped design the form told us directly not to bother with it. He was laid off from Spotify two years ago.

 

I want to be direct about what this feels like from the outside: we built a business that drives users to Spotify, advocates for the platform, and is being asked by Spotify itself to promote Spotify. And we're being cut off from the tools we need with no viable path to Extended Quota because of a hard MAU floor that doesn't account for businesses like ours.

 

Everybody loses here. Our artists lose better curation. Our curators lose quality tools. Spotify loses a platform that actively drives listeners to it. And we lose the intelligence that makes our marketplace trustworthy.

 

Is there a path for sub-250K MAU apps with real commercial usage and demonstrated investment in Spotify's ecosystem? We're not asking for special treatment... just a door that isn't locked by a threshold we'll hit in a few months but can't meet today.

 

Appreciate you creating this space for feedback. Happy to provide any additional context about our platform, usage patterns, or the specific endpoints affected.

 

Zack Fischmann
Founder, Developer & CEO, Magic Nothing

title: Re: Mark / Disable AI Generated Songs
body:
I’ve been a happy Spotify subscriber for many years, first joining as one of the first users when it was a brand new product. Up until recently I’ve been super impressed by the way the app has evolved and the recommendation engine. However, I had an extremely depressing experience recently which made me rethink the whole thing. I checked out my discover weekly for the first time in quite a while. I couldn’t help but notice that several of the tracks sound suspicously AI-generated. A few tracks in, there was a song which is blatantly an exact 1:1 rip-off of a song from an artist I listen to but re-made using some AI tool. This artist’s profile pic is also blatantly generated by AI. All their other tracks are blatant lazy rip offs of the same artist. They have 15k monthy listeners. There are no tags indicating the songs are AI generated. They have a made up bio. The original artist is not credited. The songs have different names to the original songs but the vocals are the same.

 

I go to report the artist, but there's literally nothing to report other than 'I don't like it' or 'I am raising a copyright claim against this artist' 😠

 

It feels like Spotify’s method of curating and presenting music is now effectively hyper optimised for presenting me with the perfect AI generated music for me. Sure, it sounds pretty good if you don’t listen too closely. But that’s just not what music is about, and to me, feels extremely dystopian. Having something perfectly generated for me is absolutely not the reason I listen to music.

 

Based on reading up on Spotify’s public attitude towards AI music I decided enough was enough. Until some real action is taken I’m moving to a competitor which better curates its library.

Yes, removing songs of a genre/style is what I’m doing. Unless there’s a mystical “Delete Genre” option I don’t know about, that’s what it means.

author: entfy

title: Re: Shuffle mode playing the same songs all the time
body: The issue with Spotify's random shuffle seems to be related to it's recommendations and listening history. As long as it uses this, it could never be truly random. Using the Like system and liking any songs will absolutely ruin random shuffle for instance. The fewer repeat feature accomplishes nothing as it still cycles through the same songs on reset. You can try training the algorithm by clicking individual songs, but it is very slow at adapting. That is not all because it prioritizes songs based on their own recommendation algorithm. Some newly added songs to the playlist will mysteriously play right away when some others never seem to show up. The issue isn't fewer repeats and whatever "more human" is supposed to mean here. You need to simply not weigh in listening history and your own recommendation algorithm in random shuffle and offer "true random". I created a new small playlist and hitting play resulted in playing the exact same song as the very first. That is obviously not random when the first song is the exact same one. How about a simple playtest where the first song being played is actually random? Fewer repeats only after the first set of the same 100 songs is not what we want. Allow the option to not use listening history and your own damn recommendations. It has nothing to do with device limits and smaller chunks of data. It has to do with you forcing and weighing certain songs over others. Just like your limited selection of recommended tracks offering the same group of artists. Let us opt-out of your tracking years of our listening history. I think you know exactly what the problem is, but refuse to offer a true solution.

title: Expand the list of recently searched songs
body:
Right now, the list of songs in the "recently searched" section caps out at about 30 songs. One of my personal favorite ways to listen is to search a song, and let Spotify's wonderful auto play feature play recommended tracks. The recently searched section is awesome, because I can pick a song I like and get a similar, fresh mix of music without having to navigate to a radio or anything. Problem is, once there's more than 30 songs in there, it starts overwriting the oldest song with your latest search, and my recommendation is to remove that 30 song cap, and let me have all my recently searched songs stick around, as many as possible. 

 

This should be a very quick and easy fix for the team, just changing or removing that limit.
author: Elias_B

title: Add a “Super Like” to Improve Recommendations
body:
Description:

The current “Like” button treats all positive feedback the same. But not all likes are equal — some songs are casual approvals, others are core favorites.

 

I’d like to see a “Super Like” option that signals a stronger preference.

 

For example, a Super Liked song could:

 

  • Heavily influence Daily Mixes and AI DJ sessions.

  • Increase weighting for similar artists/genres.

  • Anchor radio stations and autoplay sessions.

  • Be prioritized in future recommendations.

 

 

This would:

 

  • Improve recommendation quality by increasing signal strength.

  • Help users shape their taste profile more precisely.

  • Reduce the need to repeatedly skip or manually curate to influence the algorithm.
author: dicen

title: Contextual Interactive Tags for Spotify Songs
body: I propose a feature for Spotify where every song displays visible, clickable contextual tags directly on its page. These tags would describe not just the genre, but also when the song is best listened to, such as late night, workout, study, or road trip, along with the mood it represents like chill, motivational, heartbreak, or party, and even the best season to enjoy it such as summer, monsoon, winter, or autumn. When users click on any tag, they are instantly taken to a dynamically generated playlist of songs with the same context. This transforms music discovery from passive recommendation into active exploration, allowing users to navigate music based on real-life situations and emotions. Since Spotify already uses audio analysis and machine learning for personalization, this feature can be implemented using existing data systems while significantly increasing engagement, session time, and discovery depth.

title: Missed recommendation recap
body:
The idea is about implementing in Spotify short audio previews of songs suggested by Autoplay / Radio / Smart Shuffle

 

Idea

 

Sometimes Spotify recommends great songs during everyday listening (work, driving, running), but users cannot save them in time.

Currently, once the session ends, these recommendations are effectively lost.

 

I propose a weekly recap containing short previews (10–30 seconds) of tracks that Spotify recommended to the user but were not liked or saved.

 

This recap would appear on the Home screen as a temporary playlist or story-style card:

“Missed Recommendations This Week”

 

---

 

How it works

 

Spotify already tracks recommended tracks during listening sessions (Autoplay, Radio, Smart Shuffle, Search suggestions).

 

Once per week Spotify would:

 

1. Collect recommended tracks played but not saved

2. Select the most relevant ones

3. Generate short preview snippets (10–30 seconds)

4. Present them as a swipeable recap

 

For each track the user could:

 

- ❤️ Like

- ➕ Add to playlist

- ❌ Dismiss

 

---

 

Why this matters

 

Many users listen while busy (sports, commuting, work).

Discovery is passive — saving is active.

 

Currently discovery quality is high, but retention of discovered music is low because users cannot react in the moment.

 

This feature:

 

- prevents losing good recommendations

- increases saves and engagement

- improves trust in the recommendation system

- creates a lightweight weekly ritual similar to Wrapped, but frequent

 

---

 

User value

 

Spotify becomes not only a player but a memory of discovery.

Users rediscover what they almost liked.

 

---

 

Optional extension

 

Daily short recap or auto-generated “discovery mashup” shareable as a story.

ATHLETE SOUNDTRACK™

A New Fan Engagement Platform Connecting Sports, Music and Live Events

Athlete Soundtrack™ is a new fan-engagement concept that integrates music into live sports experiences by allowing fans to discover and listen to the playlists that inspire athletes during competition.

The concept creates a new interaction layer between athletes, sports organizations, broadcasters and music streaming platforms.

Through on-screen graphics, QR codes and direct links, viewers can instantly access an athlete’s official playlist while watching a live event.

title: Allow users to manually choose what features show on their home page
body:
Occasionally Spotify shows me a section called 'Album picks' which recommends albums I have not listened to previously, by artists I may or may not know. The picks are clearly personalized based on my recent listening history. 90% of the time this is exactly what I want to listen to- albums I don't know recommended based on my taste. However, this only shows up on my home page occasionally and with no discernible pattern. As far as I know there is no way for me to access this feature without it appearing on my home page. Instead I get a bunch of features that I'm not interested in recommended to me, seemingly arbitrarily, by an algorithm that I guess thinks it knows better than I do how I'd like to discover music.

 

Some ability to 'lock' a feature so it always shows on your home page would be great but I'm just spitballing. You have product managers. I just want to be able to use the spotify features I want to use without having to wait for the 'home page algorithm' to decide I'm allowed to.

body:
Public Spotify Jams

title: JJ's Bizarre Blog #81 - Beauty of the Beat
body:
Greetings, friends! Time to kick off the next chapter in the Bizarre Saga. And I think we're gonna have a blast with this one, literally! For this entry, I bring you yet another installment of my series covering some of my favourite musicians across different instruments. The topic this time - drums! Potentially the most important but also perhaps the most overlooked instrument. Drummers are the backbone of a song, keeping a steady rhythm and setting the beat that everyone else follows along to. It's a very challenging task and it's easily the most physically demanding instrument too, taxing the entire body of the player. A good drummer is the solid foundation that the band can rely on for stability and support, but a great drummer can use some mindblowing skills to truly push songs to the next level! And because it's so exhausting, I feel that exceptional drummers are just that much more impressive compared to any other instrument! Yet despite that, as they sit at the far back of the stage, often obscured by darkness, strobe lights and smoke machines, drummers often get neglected and overlooked. So now, let's give these beasts their due! Here are some of my personal favourite drummers of all time!

 

We start with an absolute legend that is severely underrated, that being Ian Paice of the one and only Deep Purple! Ian is the perfect example of a drummer being the band's reliable backbone! Deep Purple are kind of infamous for their turbulent history, yet despite all that, Paice is the only founding member that is still with the band to this day, he has never left and is therefore the only member to perform on every album! And speaking of performance, the man is just incredible! He has a solid core of rock 'n' roll and blues that can be felt throughout Purple's music, a steady and catchy groove that is crucial to making their songs as memorable as they are. But that's not all, because Paice is also an absolute beast and even back in the early 70s, he would play with tremendous intensity and much more speed and aggression compared to the standard of the day. Many of their songs blew the competition out of the water and helped usher in the speed metal genre! Paice can be insanely fast and heavy, but he's also a technical master that can bust out some crazy jazzy rhythms to fit the band's proggier moments! Groove, speed, precision, complexity, he's got it all! Deep Purple are a band with a very diverse array of sounds and Ian Paice has the perfect techniques for each and every one, always playing at the highest level and quietly doing exactly what's needed without complaint. And to this day, even at his age, he hasn't lost a step!

 

spotify:track:3qnlwueYpz5WZ06FKaXUoT:small  spotify:track:1shQu8Wqk50TnHek8LEOKk:small  spotify:track:3uMmllZo1AfoEnVT4ENCD3:small 
spotify:track:1iW2ktyrQHNKZwFTvgP0Ta:small  spotify:track:6ScEumQTVt6Z4QToiu9Bcl:small  spotify:track:27WqFiOFm2u1hax2ksdyqK:small 
spotify:track:1VJBQdDrOblSLmoZMeh1xh:small  spotify:track:5xu6aPH06QRtfTqwckG7Pb:small  spotify:track:2Vim06YsRb4uJRpbeq6NR6:small 

 

Deep Purple helped birth heavy metal, but there are two other bands that complete the Unholy Trinity and their drummers need recognition! Firstly of course is Black Sabbath and their most iconic (and original) drummer, Bill Ward! While Sabbath had a very bluesy sound at the start, Ward was always notable for playing with a lot more intensity than was the standard of the day, his energetic and dynamic beats really helping make the songs feel heavy! Along with Geezer Butler on the bass, they would form one of the greatest rhythm sections in metal and helped pioneer how the genre should be played! And then, there's Led Zeppelin and their legendary drummer, the late great John Bonham! He can truly be credited as the guy that ushered in the "beat it like it owes you money" style of play that formed the core of heavy metal drumming! His intensity, speed and aggression were above and beyond the norm of the day and really captivated all listeners. He had a fantastic groove infusing elements of funk into his style and was more than capable of keeping his speed under control for the band's slower and more progressive tunes. A legend gone too soon, one whose sound was so fundamental to the band's brilliance, that Led Zeppelin disbanded after his death, because they knew he was irreplaceable. These three men mentioned thus far helped evolve hard rock drumming into heavy metal drumming and even beyond that, they cast long shadows and their influence on the world of music is immeasurable!

Another classic legend I can't get enough of is the one and only Phil Collins! Now, today we all know him more as a singer thanks to his incredibly distinctive voice and litany of iconic songs to his name, but we mustn't forget, Collins is a drummer first! In fact, his singing career was kind of a happy little accident - while playing drums for the iconic progressive rock titans Genesis, their legendary frontman Peter Gabriel departed in 1975, one thing lead to another and Phil decided to have a crack at being the new singer. Needless to say, it worked out pretty well for him! With Genesis, he played some amazing prog rock that was complex and nuanced, but simple and approachable, the kind of brilliance that movement was known for! After he took over as frontman, the band started transitioning into their pop rock oriented sound and found tremendous commercial success because of it! And then once he became a solo artist in the 80s, it was just hit after hit and he became one of the most ubiquitous artists of the era! As for his drumming, it's the kind of deceptively simple playing you'd think wouldn't be noteworthy, but Collins seems to fit each beat perfectly where it needs to be and achieve a fantastic emotional feel with each rhythm! I mean, you all know the absolutely legendary moment from "In the Air Tonight", right? It's simple, nothing fancy, but it hits just right and you never forget it. That is what his drumming is like, a form of subtle genius!

Keeping with the classics, here's a criminally underrated master that I really wanted to talk about and that's the American legend Vinny Appice. Now, it's a bit of a running joke that drummers are really hard to come by, which is why many of them play in a metric ton of bands at the same time. Vinny fits that bill perfectly. He's been a core member of many iconic bands and is a prolific session drummer too. His most iconic work is alongside the one and only Ronnie James Dio across various projects - Dio, Black Sabbath, Heaven & Hell, Last in Line and more. Especially on the early Dio albums is where Vinny did his finest work. His drumming is energetic and full of power, really gets you grooving  and makes you wanna move! He has some amazing fills and creative beats that liven up every song and go above and beyond the mere necessities. He doesn't just give each song the perfect beat to make it work, he spices it up to make it stand out and really grip you tight! Dio's once in a lifetime voice will always make a song unforgettable, but the drum foundation will instantly grip you and help you feel the rhythm in your soul! A clear evolution of the styles pioneered in the 70s, polished and refined further. When you look deeper, it's amazing to see just how much iconic metal music has Vinny Appice behind the kit!

Onto a similar and even more prolific man, another New York legend, Mike Terrana! Also having played in a zillion bands throughout his career, his most iconic work is his lengthy stint with the German rock legend Axel Rudi Pell, as well as his multiple collaborations with Finnish icon Tarja Turunen, including the album "Beauty & The Beat", where the two cover classical pieces, pop songs and more! Terrana is mostly known in the realms of classic heavy metal, power metal and hard rock, being adept at rock solid riffage, gentle ballad rhythms and blistering energetic speed! Whatever the song needs, he delivers every time! He's also unbelievably world travelled! He's played in Germany with the aforementioned ARP as well as the monumental Rage and other amazing bands like Masterplan. He went to Sweden to work with the one and only Yngwie Malmsteen. He's had stints in Italy with Vision Divine and Spain with Avalanch. He's even gone as far as South Korea to play with their own Downhell! All of this without mentioning the at least dozen or so bands he plays with in his native USA. I've had the pleasure of seeing this mohawk-sporting legend with a Croatian AC/DC tribute band! This man has seemingly done it all and shows no signs of slowing down!

Let's stay in New York for a while, but get a little heavier! As the thrash metal era came to be in the 80s, a new paradigm of speed and heaviness was born, the birth of extreme metal! One of the bands at the forefront of the movement were Anthrax with their legendary drummer Charlie Benante! While most of their songs aren't as fast as many other thrash bands, this doesn't mean they can't keep up! They demonstrate as much on several songs. Across Anthrax' discography, Charlie has demonstrated his phenomenal skills - keeping a rock solid rhythm, great precision with his beats and fills, with tons of speed in reserve when things go crazy, taking the best of heavy metal and punk rock drumming and combining it into a mean, potent sound. Still aggressive as thrash metal demands, but more bouncy and fun, perfect for Anthrax' tongue-in-cheek style. On his other band Stormtroopers of Death (S.O.D.), Charlie fully demonstrated how fast and intense he can be with the right music. These days, he's also filling the kit for the late great Vinnie Paul in Pantera, a daunting task but one he succeeds at with flying colours!

But for many bands, the mission statement of thrash metal was to push the speed and heaviness to the absolute maximum and at the spearhead of that were none other than Slayer! And a lot of that came to their unbelievable drummer, Dave Lombardo! This Cuban machine was able to play at such blistering speed, conjuring so much chaos that he still somehow kept under control! He's known for his incredible double kick patterns and complex, wild fills that feel like explosions of sound! Lombardo absolutely changed the game and showed a whole new generation of drummers what's possible behind the kit and it's no overstatement to say that a huge chunk of the fast and heavy styles of today derived their approach from him!

Along the same lines, let me introduce you to Gene Hoglan! He first cut his teeth on the Bay Area thrash scene as well, even working as a drum tech and roadie for Lombardo early on before finding success with his own band Dark Angel! Despite releasing only a handful of albums, they became well known for playing much faster, heavier and darker songs than most thrash and became a cornerstone of early thrash! Hoglan would then parlay this into one of the most legendary drum discographies of all time! Gene is known for his incredible speed and flawless technique, being able to play extremely complex songs at insane speed without sacrificing precision. His air-tight double kick patterns are among the finest and an immediately recognizable aspect of his playing! I mean, you don't earn nicknames like "The Atomic Clock" and "The Human Drum Machine" for nothing! He initially worked with several other thrash bands, including over a decade-long stint in the legendary Testament, before expanding into death metal with none other than the pioneering Death! As they were transitioning into their more technical era, Hoglan joined and dropped some of the most unbelievable drum performances of all time, meshing blast beats and classic death metal heaviness with complex polyrhythms and dynamic tempos!

He would first dip his toes into industrial territory with Devin Townsend's Strapping Young Lad and showcase his proggy side on DT's solo albums. He would go full industrial with Fear Factory on Mechanize, showing how he can drum better and more precisely than even a computer (should've ditched him lads, big loss here). He even had a crucial role in helping me get into extreme music thanks to his work with Dethklok of all things, the fictional melodic death metal band from the Metalocalypse series! And all of this is just a sliver of the long list of bands he has delivered absolute brilliance with. Gene can play anything, with the only uniting factor being the mindblowing complexity and inhuman accuracy with which he executes it all! Truly a machine!

Speaking of machines, I mentioned Fear Factory, so let's stay on that wave and talk about their founding drummer, "Maximum Effective Pulse Generator" Raymond Herrera! On their early albums, his playing was crucial in helping the band create their iconic mechanical cybernetic sound, a machine-like crushing rhythm unlike anything else! Herrera used complex beats, start-stop compositions, precise double kick patterns synced to the guitars and trickery with time signatures to get that artificial sound! I mean, when many people's reaction to hearing your work is to say "Nuh uh, no way a human actually played that!", you know you did something special! His stint with Fear Factory is legendary and his performances to this day remain unmatched! He's far from a one-trick though, as he's fully indulged his love of death metal and grindcore as well with his blistering beats with the likes of Brujeria and Asesino. He can play fast and loose or lock in for some superhuman precision, simply mindblowing either way!

The complexity and technical proficiency is growing more and more. But we can go further! It's time to talk about one of the most mindblowing individuals to ever grab a pair of sticks. A man whose work haunts young drummers the world over - Tomas Haake! This Swedish superhuman is the founder and rhythm-smith of the unstoppable Meshuggah! Pioneers of djent, they are known for their ultra-low guitar riffs and for having some of the most complex and technically sophisticated songs in all of metal. Tomas' drumming is arguably the most impressive component of that! Absolutely unreal rhythms, non-repeating patterns that melt the brains of mere mortals, time signatures out of a mathematical equation, riffs never even conceived as possible until now. There are moments in their songs where Haake is playing a different rhythm with every limb simultaneously. Global satellite networks can synchronize their clocks to this man's right hand. He plays as if he has a MIDI cable going into his brain or something. Meshuggah are in a league of their own and Tomas Haake is something beyond everything. If we were to call him a drummer, what should we call all the other poor saps trying to play this instrument?

Ok, let's calm down a bit. But not really, because the next guy is really out here ready to give Haake a run for his money. Alongside Meshuggah, another European heavy prog metal outfit has been redefining metal for the past two decades and that's France's greatest metal export, Gojira! And their genius stems in no small part to their unbelievable drummer Mario Duplantier! Gojira's music is known for technical complexity and incredible riffage that shifts rhythms on a dime and morphs in all kinds of ways while remaining catchy and memorable. Mario's drum work remains consistently phenomenal and he's known for his superb double kick patterns (that he sometimes holds for entire songs), effortless rhythmic shifts, unique and creative drum fills and unmatched precision. He sounds like a machine gun that can do maths and he's absolutely breathtaking to experience!

Continuing on the same trajectory but with something a little different, here's somebody I glazed in just the last blog - Uruguayan wonder Martin Lopez! One of the most multi-faceted drummers in the world, he can do blast beats, he can play heavy and fast, soft and gentle, technical, simplistic, catchy, chaotic, he can do it all! Some of his finest work came during his stint in progressive metal beasts Opeth, where he was a uniquely perfect fit, seeing how he is equally as adept at death metal brutality and quiet jazzy polyrhythms, allowing both powerful facets of the band's sound to shine. With his current band Soen, he indulges the latter even more and broadens his skill set even further! From ballads, to proggy complexity to anthemic bangers, each song presents a unique new journey! This is a man that goes through ten different drum beats per song, each one being unbelievable on its own, never settles to just do the average and always goes above and beyond to make the song special and memorable. It's like he tells a story with each song with the way he shifts between beats and adapts to the melodies. If Lopez is behind the kit, you will notice and you will remember what he played!

Riding on the prog wave, I can't make a blog about drummers and not talk about the one and only Mike Portnoy! Easily one of the most gifted and all-round capable masters to ever pick up the sticks, he can play some of the most complex and challenging riffs ever heard, the types of stuff most young drummers set as a personal milestone. As in "If I can learn to play this monstrosity, then I am officially a good drummer!". But Portnoy is far from just a technical master! He loves laying down a sweet beat for lovely ballads, dabbling with oldschool rock 'n' roll and classic metal and has shown love and appreciation for all genres! He's like a spiritual successor to the likes of Paice or Bonham, carrying that classic spirit but upgraded with all the technical proficiency that modern music demands! Portnoy is most known as a founding member and core songwriter for Dream Theater, arguably the greatest progressive metal band of all time, where he was able to explore every avenue imaginable thanks to the bottomless creativity and rich, dynamic songs DT are beloved for. The side project Liquid Tension Experiment alongside other members allowed him to get even more experimental! He has since returned to Dream Theater after a long absence, but in the meantime, he has played with a multitude of bands, allowing him to have a crack at all manner of genres and truly showcase the full arsenal of skills at his disposal. Any song Portnoy plays on, he enhances! Easily one of the most complete performers to ever sit behind the kit.

With some delicious proggy complexity, bountiful polyrhythms, yet also enough catchy grooves to form the emotional core, Mangini was probably the best fit DT could've asked for!

And on the topic of classic prog, if there's one band that can compete with Dream Theater for modern kings of prog, it has to be Tool! Just like DT, each member of the band is one of the best in the world at their respective instrument. Tool have this special quality of letting each member shine bright and showcase their skills without outshining the others and always remaining in service of the song and nowhere is this more true than their master drummer Danny Carey! A technical genius, a master of rhythms, a man who dances through weird time signatures and bizarre tempo changes almost effortlessly. He mixes rock, blues, funk, jazz, metal and God knows what else into a fine blend that befits Tool's weird but captivating sound. His drumming is like a mathematical equation that is somehow beautiful and elegant. If you need proof of his skill, know this - several drummers I've covered in this blog (including Portnoy), all of which are amazing as we've established, have tried to play Danny Carey drum parts and struggled a lot with it! That tells you enough I feel.

Alright, let's kick the intensity back up with some death metal. When it comes to it, there's one band that has remained the gold standard for the genre and that is Cannibal Corpse! They have countless amazing albums under their belt and on every single one, founder and key songwriter Paul Mazurkiewicz can be heard tearing it up on the drums! His playing is blisteringly fast, brutally heavy and absolutely relentless, befitting of death metal! Yet it's also clear, controlled and focused, always dropping the perfect beat for every nasty riff and holding it steady as long as it takes. Just like how Cannibal Corpse are the standard of excellence for the genre, Paul continues to set the bar for what death metal drumming needs to be like.

When it comes to founding extreme metal bands, few are as influential as Brazilian legends Sepultura and across the golden years of their discography, they have one man to thank for their blistering beats - Igor Cavalera! He brings amazing speed, solid technique and unfiltered energy to every performance and regardless if the band played death metal, black metal, thrash metal or even nu metal, he always overdelivered and helped turn their songs into the shots of pure adrenaline we know and love! He's not just one of the most solid and dependable thrashers out there, but he's also known for tapping into that South American blood and utilizing loads of tribal drums and traditional percussion, giving Sepultura that primal, feral sound! Alongside his brother Max on many other projects like Cavalera Conspiracy and Nailbomb, Igor has been blessing us with phenomenal metal music for decades and decades to come!

And lastly of the pioneering extreme metal bands, the fastest and most merciless genre of grindcore was pioneered by Birmingham monsters Napalm Death and we have a lot of that innovation came from their legendary drummers! Firstly, we have the wild man Mick "Hurricane" Harris who slayed away on their early records. Credited with pioneering blast beats and helping popularize them in metal and punk, Mick was and still remains one of the best drummers in the world when it comes to raw speed and unmatched aggression! His successor though was no slouch, this being Danny Herrera (no relation to Raymond)! Befittingly, he has one of the tightest and most intense blast beats in all of music and his raw, pure energy is perfect for the band's sound! He's also quite technically sound, which has allowed Napalm Death to diversify their approach over the years and try their hand and many different styles, forging quite a varied and interesting discography!

 

spotify:track:64syMK7oo9VPihJBj1IBGH:small  spotify:track:5oIHVkR1P4y277ftTPLpWj:small  spotify:track:5PWC0SulWeIkOiVdJqE7a7:small 
spotify:track:6pTvh9yMeJQ87WraFHFOW8:small  spotify:track:2P16weldDuaBSINayQGTQR:small  spotify:track:01G2VTNQnk6E3tCJjcwW55:small 
spotify:track:5KUquxYX4L86p197AaPikA:small  spotify:track:6CviCNyxTDA9TxpyHuGxj7:small  spotify:track:4xrZLwofthSj9EwqXg0Ep6:small 

 

From death metal through grindcore, let's mention black metal! It is naturally one of the most intense and grueling styles when it comes to drumming. The constant blast beats and often lengthy songs are insanely taxing on the body, equal or even more so than death metal, so my heart goes out to all of them out there! But if there's one black metal drummer that always blows my mind, it's Maciej Kowalski aka Darkside, most notably of Polish titans Mgła and their side project Kriegsmaschine! Not only does he have some of the tightest and most well maintained blast beats in the genre, but his fills, cymbal flourishes and rhythm changes are just otherworldly! Basically prog rock through a black metal lens. I've had the pleasure of seeing him live a couple times and was blown away by how he alternates his hands on certain beats to use a multitude of hi-hats and cymbals all around him while maintaining a constant beat and never losing the core rhythm! He's like a machine with how precise and stable he is, yet he has this amazing feel and rich technique that is so captivating! Mgła have become a monumental act in recent years and become the new flag bearers of black metal and Darkside has helped showcase how to keep the core of the genre's iconic drumming intact, while introducing new elements and evolving into a new paradigm!

 

spotify:track:6xJYzuXI6M0XGoBJq2LYoU:small  spotify:track:4xO904zD4tRaOs6tLHSNDk:small  spotify:track:4ypaBvCPhkO3skH2jOvjps:small 
spotify:track:5EHkbreR3DQqdamBXUdx7V:small  spotify:track:0vLaxJNsJGYCIK0gedgAmM:small  spotify:track:4tkwtKq447Hl9fg9JTodsI:small 

 

Now, how about something a little more modern. Nu metal is a genre that introduced many (including me) into the world of metal and one band that acted as a huge funnel for people to get into the heavier side of things are SlipKnot! They're already known for their rich percussion sound thanks to all the custom miscellaneous things like barrels, buckets and assorted drums played by clown prince Shawn Crahan and wild card Chris Fehn. But even beside that, they already had one of the greatest drummers of all time leading the charge - the late great Joey Jordison! He's known for his absolutely blistering speed, relentless energy and chaotic, seemingly unhinged way of playing! Some call it sloppy, but it's in fact quite controlled and it allows Joey to lend the songs a kind of raw and primal energy that helped SlipKnot be the most intense and brutal outing of the nu metal era. Joey used the band's mainstream success to just casually expose an entire generation to blast beats and death metal drumming for the first time. Most were unprepared for this level of intensity but that only made it more enthralling! Joey was and always will be in a league of his own and when you dig through his further career with bands like the Murderdolls and Sinsaenum, you only discover what a talented and multi-faceted master he was! He will forever be #1!

 

spotify:track:0Y2i84QWPFiFHQfEQDgHya:small  spotify:track:6wqJeItl3Vc3az4ZicSQAB:small  spotify:track:5u2qiqQGUBy8fvXTvs9i2X:small 
spotify:track:3OYZWMm5m2DEwq2Tc1ukTh:small  spotify:track:61mWefnWQOLf90gepjOCb3:small  spotify:track:4nM7EwYTLp9cCR8LDIFiO3:small 
spotify:track:7AIrHJCHokLJF3MNngmGm0:small  spotify:track:1lDGqdRg7TOaidH0JBGnPj:small  spotify:track:0i6WJzYFhNrcQqBjBRYQQY:small 

 

And lastly, for something truly recent, I want to give a special shoutout to the young prospect Luke Holland! This lad is most prolific in the metalcore scene with bands like I See Stars and The Word Alive, so he quickly mastered modern syncopated rhythms, breakdowns and heavy riffage (continuing off of the work of many other men on this list). However, Luke is a very well-rounded drummer that branches out into all kinds of things. He's shown the kinds of catchy, memorable beats he can offer with his work with Hollywood Undead and the likes of Starset. And he's showcased his technical mastery thanks to his long standing collaboration with djent guitar virtuoso Jason Richardson on the latter's instrumental prog albums! You can even see him play more mainstream and poppy things on his own YouTube channel! Holland is a modern Swiss army knife of a player that excels at everything and always impresses with his fantastic technique and versatile approach befitting any genre, so he's naturally one of the young flag bearers of modern drumming!

 

spotify:track:4MO8nGGDBMvszs4IjBJupm:small  spotify:track:0KKbth4n6npBYzv6x5cThm:small  spotify:track:2HSZSG1htNmbDaP76Ixod2:small 
spotify:track:246oEeMOCQxn1sSUfQiiHW:small  spotify:track:5xxvjoAj4zUmrQTRU0MfRr:small  spotify:track:6QlhJB13hsa8N08OcbizIU:small 
spotify:track:6Iu5qB8A2lIRRDCw6skkmA:small  spotify:track:0cvy7FUx0vFpD6w8SIi9e9:small  spotify:track:6XWC2VlBF53z1uXJNKfWeZ:small 

 

So there you have it! Some of the best drummers I've ever come across! Drums really are a crucial part of a song and being the human metronome that the rest of the band has to work off of is no easy task. The majority of music is built around the beat, so in that sense, drums are the foundation, the bedrock that everything has to be built off of. And yet, drummers often go underappreciated, since they don't usually stand in the spotlight much. So let's give them their flowers! They have the most physically grueling job of any band, they always haul the most equipment and they put their entire bodies through hell for the love of the music! All band members have to be musicians, but drummers almost have to be athletes too. And let me tell you, when you find a drummer of exceptional abilities, it's something special! The way they keep up with this brutal pace, these mind-bending rhythms and physical challenges, the way some of them can keep several rhythms perfectly with different parts of their body, it's just incredible! The speed, the precision, the timing, the endurance, it never ceases to amaze me! So I hope you too can appreciate their efforts a little more now! Let's protect our drummers! After all, if we lose one, that's at least seven bands that will need a new one. So enjoy the beats and hit it like you mean it! Take care and see you all soon!

title: AI Music Match: Find people with similar music taste
body:
I would love Spotify to add an opt-in feature that helps users discover other people with similar music taste.

Spotify already helps us discover music, but music is also one of the easiest ways people connect with each other. Many people bond over shared artists, playlists, moods, genres, concerts, and listening habits.

The feature could be called AI Music Match.

Users could choose to turn it on, and Spotify could create a simple music compatibility experience based on things like:

  • shared artists
  • shared songs
  • similar playlists
  • favorite genres
  • listening moods
  • concert or festival interests
  • recent music taste
  • long-term music taste

Instead of only showing “you both listen to the same artist,” Spotify could use AI to explain the match in a more human way.

For example:

“You both seem to enjoy emotional electronic music, late-night playlists, melodic tracks, and festival-style energy.”

The feature could also suggest conversation starters, such as:

“You both listen to darker electronic music. What is one track you would play at 3 AM?”

Possible uses:

  • finding friends with similar music taste
  • finding concert or festival buddies
  • meeting people who enjoy the same music scene
  • discovering people through shared playlists
  • creating shared playlists between matched users
  • making Spotify more social in an optional and privacy-friendly way

Privacy is very important. This feature should be fully opt-in. Users should decide if they want to be visible, what information is shown, and whether they are looking for friends, music discovery, concert buddies, or something else. Users should also be able to block, report, or disable the feature at any time.

Spotify is already a place where people express who they are through music. This feature would make it easier for people to connect with others through that shared music identity.
author: Arvis1

title: Public Curated Playlists with Song Submission Requests (Owner is Moderator)
body:
I’d love to see Spotify add a new type of playlist: a Public Curated Playlist.

The idea is simple:

  • Anyone on Spotify can discover and follow the playlist.
  • Anyone can submit a request to add a song to the playlist.
  • The playlist owner (moderator) receive a queue of pending song requests.
  • Moderators can Approve or Deny each request to maintain the curation/vibe/theme of playlist.
  • Approved songs are automatically added to the playlist, while denied requests are removed.
  • Playlist owners can assign moderator permissions to trusted users so multiple people can help review submissions.

Why this would be useful

Current collaborative playlists allow everyone to add songs directly, which can quickly become cluttered or off-topic. This feature would allow communities to contribute while keeping playlists curated and high quality.

Some great use cases include:

  • Genre-specific community playlists
  • Festival or concert playlists
  • Workout playlists
  • Gaming playlists
  • Mood or study playlists
  • Local music scenes
  • Playlists for discovering new artists

It would create a fun way for listeners to contribute while giving playlist owners complete control over what gets added.

Bonus ideas

  • Show who submitted each song.
  • Allow moderators to leave a short reason when declining a submission.
  • Let users vote on pending submissions before moderators make a decision.
  • Notify users when their submission is approved or denied.

I think this would make playlist discovery much more interactive while still preserving the quality and identity of curated playlists.

Include Spam Protection Feature

For example, moderators could choose settings like:

  • Limit users to 3–5 pending submissions at a time.
  • Automatically filter duplicate song requests.
  • Require users to follow the playlist before submitting.
  • Optionally require accounts to be at least a certain age or have some listening history before they can submit.

Currently, if a Daylist or Daily Mix doesn't look appealing, i have to wait hours (for Daylists) or an entire day (for Daily Mixes) for a dynamic update.Additionally, these algorithmic playlists tend to loop and repeat the exact same songs frequently, instead of diving into Spotify's vast catalog. title: Artists Wanted Edition 19: Share music - discover music - like music
body:
🎶 Welcome to Artists Wanted Edition 19. 🎶

If you’re an independent artist and would like your music heard by the Spotify Community, we’d love to hear from you 👉

If you want to discover and support independent artists this is for you too ✔️

 

How it works:

Stage 1

• Submit one original song released within the last 12 months.
• Community members can listen and support their favourite submissions by giving them a “Like 👍”

Stage 2

• The Top 20 submissions by “Likes” will advance to the review stage 📈
• The Community Music Chat Team will shortlist 5 finalists 🏅

Stage 3

• 5 Finalists will be put forward for a Community vote 🗳️
• The winning artist will be featured by the Community 🙌

Important:

We recommend writing your submission in a text editor, document, or notes app before posting it to the Spotify Community.

This helps prevent the loss of your work in the event of a timeout or connection issue, and allows you to keep a copy of your submission for future use.

Write > copy and paste > add song link > post

 

Artist Engagement:

 

  • Artists must have a current, valid Spotify for Artists account, and have to be logged in to this community before submitting 💻
  • By participating in Artists Wanted, you're engaging with the community and asking the community to engage back 🤝
  • The picture you paint about yourself gives listeners a reason to press play. Tell us about your journey, your music, and what inspires you 🕺
  • Tell us your band name, where you’re from, and what kind of music you create 🎵
  • We want to get to know who’s behind the song 🤗

Community Voting:

  • Users need a valid Spotify account and have to be logged in to this community prior to voting ☑️

  • Take time to listen to as many or all of the submissions ☑️

  • There is no limit to the number of submissions you can support ☑️

  • You can come back to view more submissions, and continue giving a “Like” to the tracks that appeal to you ☑️


When will submissions and voting occur

 

Artists submissions: from June 15th to July 13th (Midnight Pacific Time)
Community vote: from June 15th to July 20th (Midnight Pacific Time)
Music Team selection: July 27th (Midnight Pacific Time)

Final Round (Community Vote): July 27th to August 3rd

 

To Participate:

 

Just reply to this post with your statement and qualifying song link. Please also provide a social media profile so we can contact you if needed 📲

Note - We ask artists to submit only ONE original song and not a cover. Also, artists should not post/embed videos, as we'd like to keep the ground fair for smaller artists with lesser budgets.

A track is ineligible if it was submitted to a previous Artist Wanted Edition.

We encourage everyone to listen to multiple submissions:

  • Discover new artists 🎶
  • Support them by giving "Likes 👍"

Come back to vote for your top artist in the final round thread 🔙


*Keep updated by subscribing to in the upcoming Artists Wanted Information Hub

author: Shir65-7

title: Ideas to Increase Premium Subscriptions Naturally
body:
Dear Spotify Team,

 

This isn't a complaint—it's a thank-you and a few ideas from someone who uses Spotify every single day.

 

I am a big fan of Spotify. Honestly, I think your idea is better than many other music apps out there.

 

A big part of my day runs through Spotify. Whether I'm working, doing house chores, cooking, or simply trying to relax, Spotify is always there with me. Music helps me focus, feel calm, and enjoy even the ordinary moments of life.

 

I understand that Premium subscriptions are an important part of your business. I also understand that advertisements help support the free version of the platform. I am not in a position to tell a company how to run its business, but as a loyal listener, I wanted to share a few ideas that might make Spotify even better.

 

One thing that can sometimes make the experience less enjoyable is hearing the same advertisements repeatedly. In my opinion, people do not usually subscribe just because they are tired of ads. Many users are naturally selective about subscriptions and often look for alternatives before spending money.

 

Because of that, I believe the best way to encourage Premium subscriptions is not through repetition, but through curiosity and engagement. When people discover content that genuinely interests them, they become more willing to explore and invest in the platform.

 

For example, instead of repetitive advertisements, Spotify could use personalized 30-second podcast previews. If a user enjoys comedy, they could hear a funny clip. If they enjoy business, horror, fiction, self-improvement, celebrity stories, trending discussions, or storytelling, they could hear a short preview that sparks their curiosity.

 

At the end of the preview, Spotify could display a message such as, "Want more content like this? Explore it with Spotify Premium." This approach would make users feel excited rather than interrupted, and it could naturally encourage more people to subscribe because they have discovered something they genuinely want to continue listening to.

 

Another idea is to make Premium feel even more rewarding. For example, subscribers could receive concert ticket coupons, artist merchandise discounts, exclusive fan experiences, early access to special content, or other benefits that make the subscription feel more valuable beyond ad-free listening.

 

I also noticed that songs often feel more exciting on social media because people hear the most memorable lyrical moments. Sometimes a 15-second lyrical clip creates more excitement than listening to a full song immediately. If Spotify could create something like a "Trending Lyrical Zone" where users can discover popular lyric snippets, trending song moments, viral lyrical highlights, or shareable lyric experiences, it could make music discovery even more engaging and fun.

 

These are just thoughts from a listener who genuinely loves Spotify and wants to see it continue growing. I hope some of these ideas are helpful.

 

Thank you for creating an app that has become such an important part of my daily life. Spotify has been there during work, relaxation, chores, and countless moments in between, and I appreciate the experience your team has built.

 

Wishing Spotify continued growth and success.

 

From,

 

A Spotify Zombie 🎧

title: Add a “Popular Episodes” Section to Podcast Pages
body:
I would like to suggest adding a “Popular Episodes” section to each podcast page, similar to Spotify's “Popular Songs” section on each artist.

This would help listeners quickly discover the most interesting or well-known episodes of a podcast, instead of only browsing chronologically. For example, in an interview podcast, the page could highlight interviews with the most famous guests.

This would make podcast discovery easier: new listeners could start from the strongest episodes, enjoy the content faster, and be encouraged to listen to more episodes from the same podcast.
author: Lorenzo96

title: Add a “Popular Episodes” Section to Podcast Pages
body:
I would like to suggest adding a “Popular Episodes” section to each podcast page, similar to YouTube’s “Popular videos” section on each channel.

This would help listeners quickly discover the most interesting or well-known episodes of a podcast, instead of only browsing chronologically. For example, in an interview podcast, the page could highlight interviews with the most famous guests.

It would also be useful if podcast creators could organize selected episodes into custom playlists or sections, such as categories based on guest type, topic, or theme.

This would make podcast discovery easier: new listeners could start from the strongest episodes, enjoy the content faster, and be encouraged to listen to more episodes from the same podcast.
author: Lorenzo96

title: Scrollable music discovery feed — like TikTok, but inside Spotify
body:
I’ve been a Premium subscriber for a while, and I genuinely love Spotify — but I keep finding new music on TikTok instead of here, and that feels wrong.

The problem isn’t that Spotify lacks discovery features. It’s that the algorithm keeps showing me variations of what I already listen to. Even when I hit skip repeatedly, I stay inside my own bubble. I want to find something completely new, not a cousin of what I already know.

What I’m suggesting is a dedicated scrollable feed where you can swipe through short clips of trending or new songs — 15 to 20 seconds each, with the single cover or a visual — and quickly decide if you want to save it or keep scrolling. No user-generated content needed, just the music itself.

The difference from existing features like Discover Weekly or Release Radar is that those are still algorithm-curated based on your history. This would be more open: trending songs, new releases, things completely outside your usual taste. You scroll, you skip, you save. Simple.

Premium users could tap through to the full track instantly. Free users could preview and then get an ad before the full listen.

Right now, the conversation about what’s new and what’s trending in music is happening on TikTok. Spotify could own that conversation instead.

title: Beefing Up the Library & Revolutionizing Audiobooks with Voice Tech
body:
The Vision

While Spotify has introduced audiobooks into the Premium ecosystem, the current selection feels heavily limited, dominated by older backlist titles and public-domain classics. To defeat dedicated giants like Audible and truly win over book lovers, Spotify needs to do two things: aggressively expand its catalog of modern, high-demand books and revolutionize the audio experience using advanced voice and AI technology.

Audiobook narration can be a more powerful, immersive, and engaging experience than visual media. Anyone who listened to Ray Porter narrate Project Hail Mary knows that a brilliant voice actor can make an audio experience literally thousands of times better than a movie. Spotify needs to treat voice actors like the rockstars they are, aggressively acquire top-tier content, and lead the industry in audio innovation.

1. The Library Expansion: Content is King

A massive catalog doesn't matter if users can't find the contemporary books they actually want to read.

  • Prioritize Modern Bestsellers: Spotify must negotiate aggressive licensing deals with major publishers to ensure day-and-date releases for the most anticipated new fiction, sci-fi, and non-fiction blockbusters, included directly in the Premium monthly hours.

  • Narrator-Centric Curated Hubs: Create "Director's Cut" or "Narrator Spotlight" hubs on the homepage. Let us browse and discover books based on the quality of the performance and the voice, helping hidden gems or newer titles get discovered through elite narration.

2. Implementing Next-Gen Voice-Enhancement Tech

Since Spotify is fundamentally an audio tech company, it should introduce features that enhance how the human voice actually interacts with our ears:

  • AI Spatial Audio & Remastering: Give older or poorly recorded audiobooks a "sonic facelift." Use AI to upscale lower-bitrate recordings, remove background hiss, and separate the narrator’s voice into spatial audio, making it feel like they are sitting in the room with you.

  • Voice Actor Profiles & Follow Buttons: Treat elite narrators (e.g., Stephen Fry, Ray Porter, Julia Whelan) like music artists. Give them official profiles, let users "Follow" them, and send push notifications whenever they release a new narration.

  • Dynamic AI Soundscapes: Implement an optional feature where a lightweight, context-aware AI generates subtle, dynamic ambient background elements tailored to the text (e.g., a faint hum of a spaceship engine during a sci-fi monologue, or distant rain during a moody mystery scene).

  • AI Customization for Classics: For those older, public-domain books that are in the library, allow users to apply different AI-generated voice profiles (adjusting tone, accent, or pacing) so a 19th-century novel can be read in a modern, engaging style that fits the listener's preference.

Why Spotify Can Do It Best

Spotify already possesses the most advanced audio streaming infrastructure and personalization algorithms on the planet. By combining an aggressive push for modern premium content with cutting-edge voice enhancement technology, Spotify won't just match the competition—it will entirely redefine what digital reading feels like.

title: Better parental controls
body:
Woof, where to even begin... With the kids app, parents are only able to filter based on two age ranges 0-5, 5-12. Otherwise, the option is to handpick playlists (or add an entire album as a new playlist) and manually share each one and then the parents have to use the child's device to select which "shared playlists" to allow. Or, on the other hand individually block specific content. You know, with all the free time parents have, this is no big deal, right? ...Right?

 

Furthermore, I'm not even seeing a "my library" page in the kids version of the app. Makes viewing, searching, or sorting through the music added to their library unnecessarily cumbersome and confusion. The UI is so flooded with your "suggestions" I guess you ran out of room. The app falls far short even as a basic music playing app, regardless of user age.

 

Honestly, I just want a way for my kids to experience and discover music that they like, but your tools are so thoughtless and clunky. The way your apps are executed I can only infer that you think someone's music collection is an open pipe that gushes out whatever you decide to serve that day rather than an expansive library that users curate to their taste and preference on an individual basis.

 

Wish I had hope that you'll even bother making these basic improvements, but there's probably no money in it for you.

title: Subject: Feature Proposal: "Opsi" – A Location-Based Interactive Playlist Experience
body:
Dear Spotify Product and Development Team,

I would like to propose a new, exciting location-based feature for Spotify called "Opsi" (derived from the Korean word "수없이" [su-eopsi], meaning "countless").

"Opsi" bridges the gap between daily routines and music, allowing users to co-create real-time playlists tied to specific physical locations like train cars, taxis, or vending machines.

1. Core Concept & User Experience (UX)

  • Step 1: Action & Scan
    Users scan a QR code inside a train car, a taxi, or on a vending machine.
  • Step 2: Instant Gratification
    When a user adds a song they want to share, that song plays instantly on their own headphones (or through the taxi's speakers). This provides immediate satisfaction and a sense of "hijacking" the space with their music.
  • Step 3: Endless Connection
    After the song finishes, it remains in that location’s shared playlist. It creates an emotional, anonymous bond: "Someone who rides this train after me might listen to the song I recommended."

2. Specific Use Cases

  • The Train Car Experience (Limited to 2 Songs Per Person)
    When passengers scan the QR code in a specific train car, they join the "Live Car Playlist." To keep it fair and engaging, each user can only add up to 2 songs. As people get on and off, the playlist constantly evolves, transforming a boring daily commute into a community-driven music festival.
  • The Taxi Experience
    Passengers can instantly play their favorite song through the vehicle's speakers, leaving it for the next passenger to discover.
  • The Vending Machine Experience
    Buying a drink unlocks the ability to add a song to that specific neighborhood's playlist (e.g., "Shibuya Station Vending Machine Playlist"), acting as a musical time capsule for the city.

3. Why This Will Succeed & Benefit Spotify

  • High Engagement: The "Instant Play" mechanism motivates users to actively participate rather than just passively listening.
  • Gamification via Limits: Restricting train playlists to 2 songs per person forces users to thoughtfully curate their absolute best recommendations, ensuring high-quality playlists.
  • B2B Partnership Opportunities: Spotify can partner with transit authorities, taxi companies, and beverage brands for major advertising campaigns and integrated sponsorships.

I believe "Opsi" would drive massive user acquisition and create an entirely new way for people to experience music together in the real world.

Thank you for your time and for continuously making Spotify the best place for music lovers. I would love to hear your thoughts on this idea!

Sincerely,

title: Spotify Nearby
body:
Hi Spotify Team,

I’d like to suggest a feature idea that could make Spotify more social in a natural and music-centered way.

The idea would be a feature similar to “Spotify Nearby” or “Nearby Listening,” where users could discover what people physically nearby are listening to, as long as those users have explicitly opted in. It would work somewhat like Bluetooth discovery, but focused on music taste and social connection.

For privacy and safety, this should never be enabled by default. Users would have to manually activate a “Visible Nearby” mode. When active, other nearby users could see limited information, such as the song, artist, genre, or overall music compatibility, without revealing the person’s exact location.

For example, someone nearby might see:

“Someone nearby is listening to Deftones.”

or

“Nearby listener: 82% music compatibility with you.”

Instead of allowing direct messages immediately, the feature could include a request system. A user could send a short music-based connection request, such as:

“Hey, I saw you’re listening to Deftones. Great taste — want to share playlists?”

Only if the other person accepts would a chat or playlist-sharing option become available. This would help prevent spam, unwanted messages, or uncomfortable interactions.

To make the feature safer, it could include:

  • Anonymous profiles until both users accept the connection.

  • No exact distance or live location shown.

  • Easy blocking and reporting.

  • A setting to show only music taste or artists, not the exact current song.

  • Age and privacy filters.

  • An option to only appear nearby in specific places or sessions.

A softer version of the feature could also show “Nearby Vibes,” where users see the general music mood around them without identifying specific people. For example:

  • 35% pop

  • 20% indie

  • 15% rap

  • 10% rock

  • 20% other

I think this could make Spotify feel more alive in public spaces like universities, cafés, airports, gyms, festivals, and concerts. It would create a new way for people to connect through music without feeling like a dating app or a traditional social network.

The key point would be consent: users should only appear if they choose to, and the experience should feel safe, optional, and music-first.

Thank you for reading my suggestion. I’d love to see Spotify explore more social discovery features like this in the future.

Best regards,
Carlos

title: Allow Spotify AI DJ to Fully Work in Jam Sessions
body:
I would like to suggest adding official support for Spotify AI DJ in Jam sessions.

My friends and I all have Spotify Premium, and before, we were able to use AI DJ while in a Jam. The DJ would choose songs for the group, and everyone in the Jam could listen together. It was one of the most fun ways for us to discover music and enjoy listening as a group.

However, recently AI DJ no longer works properly in our Jams. When one person starts DJ, sometimes only that person can hear the music while the other participants hear nothing. Other times the opposite happens. Spotify Support explained that AI DJ and Jam are separate features and are not officially designed to work together.

I would love to see Spotify add official compatibility between AI DJ and Jam sessions. AI DJ already feels like a group DJ when listening with friends, and combining it with Jam would make group listening more interactive and enjoyable.

title: Add a "Chart Mode" to Personal Playlists (Track Position Movements, Peaks, and Weeks)
body:
Hi Spotify Team,

There is a huge community of users (charts enthusiasts from forums like ATRL and music collectors) who use playlists to create personal weekly charts or countdowns. Currently, managing position movements is a completely manual task done in external spreadsheets.

I would love to see a "Chart Mode" toggle option for user-created playlists. When enabled, this feature should:

  • Track position movements: Show visual indicators next to the track number based on the playlist's reordering history (e.g., +4 for climbs, -2 for drops, = for static positions, and a DEBUT tag).

  • Display Chart Stats: Keep a subtle record inside the playlist of the song's Peak Position and Total Weeks on that specific playlist.

  • Manual/Weekly Update Trigger: A button to "Snapshot/Freeze" the current week so the app can calculate the differences when we rearrange the tracks for the next week.

This would be a game-changer for music charts lovers and would keep users deeply engaged in curating their libraries inside Spotify.

body:
Spotify helps people discover music through sound. What if it could also help people discover music through visual taste?

 

Spotify has one of the world's largest collections of album artwork, yet there is currently no way to explore music through visual characteristics.

 

I'd love to see Spotify expand it's search capabilities to allow users to search and browse music based on album cover attributes such as typography, illustration, portrait photography, black-and-white imagery, color palettes and other visual styles.
