title: Add a mode or playlist to play only songs not added to favorites
body:
I really love discovering new songs.
Currently, I use features such as Smart Shuffle, AI DJ, and Daily Mix to find new music.
I also feel that Discover Weekly is an excellent feature for encountering new music because it focuses on playing songs that I have not added to my favorites.

 

However, Discover Weekly is only updated once a week.
For users like me who listen to music for long hours every day, I finish listening to the recommended songs in about a day, and then I have to wait until the next update.
Therefore, I believe it does not fully satisfy the needs of users who want to discover new music continuously.

 

So, I propose a playlist feature called Discover Daily that is updated every day.
In this feature, the system analyzes data such as the user's playback history, favorite songs, and skipped tracks to recommend songs that the user has never heard before on a daily basis.

 

Furthermore, I think we could add a new playback mode like Discovery Mode in addition to a playlist.
In this mode, users can simply press the play button to listen to songs they have never heard before, one after another.
Since there is no need to wait for the Discover Weekly update, users can encounter new music at any time, which would make it a very convenient feature for those who want to actively discover music.

 

Also, by broadening the range of genres and artists, it can promote encounters with music that users do not usually listen to.
author: mtmtyu

title: Learn Languages Through Music with Live Lyric Translation & Pronunciation
body:
Core Idea: Add a language learning mode to Spotify that uses the lyrics to songs in the actual native lyrics and with the English transliteration on a line right below. 

 

Ideally, it would be songs with clear vocals, popular lyrics, or strong sing-along/karaoke potential

 

As someone who loves music and learning a foreign language, I find myself listening to A LOT of music on Spotify from musicians in that region or language. But I find myself constantly going back and forth between Spotify and Google Translate on my phone to understand the lyrics as well as pronouncing words. This would essentially eliminate that added step so I can stay fully in Spotify. 

 

Other Benefits:

* Promotes artists and musicians from all over the world.

* Exposes users to new and different music that is outside of English

*Algorithms don't need to necessarily change all that much as it would continue to use the user's taste preferences and song history. 

*Offers a unique approach to learning a language. 

*Creates an immersive Spotify and learning experience using music 

*Could be easily turned on or off with a click (or swipe) of a button and/or am entirely new but familiar Spotify experience (something like DJ X meets Discover Weekly
author: dyzzyD

title: Kids profile
body:
For premium users there should be a choice to activate a kids profile in Spotify. This should be separate from your own profile. The profiles should suggest music separately and have their own history. 
You should be able to be on the kids profile on a separate device on the same network, and play simultaneously. 

This would solve having your discover weekly being overrun with children music when you are a parent. It would also solve the issue of having white noise in the baby room and not being able to have music in any other room
author: kutifan

title: Ability to compare individual users’ weekly listening stats
body: Spotify’s new Listening Stats are a great step forward for weekly insights, and it’s cool that we can now view and compare stats with friends (total minutes, top artists/songs, etc.). However, it currently feels limited to seeing an aggregate or all-friends overview rather than a clean side-by-side comparison between just me and one specific friend (or opted-in public profile). Adding the ability to select and directly compare individual users’ stats, with strong privacy controls, would make it far more social, competitive, and useful for things like friendly rivalries, music discovery, or simply checking how your habits line up with someone else’s. This would turn the feature from good to excellent without relying on third-party apps.”
author: 31ve3hi73blgfqfear6i

title: Make record labels names clickable to improve music discovery
body: When you open any album or single, you can see the name of the label that released the album below the list of songs. Why not make this name clickable so that I can discover everything else released by that label?
author: AndyMarch

title: Music Request: Kaitou Saint Tail (怪盗セイント・テール) OST & Theme Songs
body:
Dear Spotify Content Team,

I am a long-time Spotify user and an anime music fan. I would like to request the addition of the soundtrack and theme songs from the classic 1995 anime Kaitou Saint Tail (Japanese: 怪盗セイント・テール, English: Mysterious Thief Saint Tail).

Why this music matters:

  • It is a beloved 90s anime that aired in many countries, including Japan and China, leaving a strong nostalgic impact.

  • The music includes memorable songs such as:

    • Toki wo Koete (OP1) by Miki Matsubara

    • Ashita e to Kakedashite Yukou (OP2) by Seiko Matsuda

    • Junk Shin (ED1) by Masaki Inoue

    • Yume Miru Melody (ED2) by Miyuki Kajitani

    • Original soundtrack composed by Hayato Matsuo

  • Currently, none of these tracks are available on Spotify. Fans have to rely on unofficial uploads or local files.

What we hope for:
We would love to see the original soundtrack albums and single releases properly licensed and added to Spotify. This would allow both nostalgic listeners and new audiences to discover this hidden gem.

Thank you for considering this request. I truly hope to hear Saint Tail’s beautiful music on Spotify someday.

Sincerely,
author: LOEN1

title: Add '' Discover similar vibes''  button
body:
A button that recommends music with a similar sound and mood.

 

If you’re listening to a song you really like and want to find music with a similar vibe, mood, or genre, there could be a feature button called “Discover Same Vibe.”

It could also include an AI feature where users can describe the kind of music atmosphere or feeling they want at the moment, and the AI would recommend songs that match their current mood and taste.
author: Ceci86

title: Add an Incognito Mode for Listening That Does Not Affect Taste Profile
body:
Spotify should add an Incognito Mode for listening.

Sometimes users want to play music that does not reflect their actual taste. This can happen when we are playing music for friends, working with children, testing songs, listening to sleep sounds, checking out a viral track, or playing music for a specific event. Those listens should not reshape our recommendations, Discover Weekly, Daily Mixes, Spotify Wrapped, or overall taste profile.

Spotify already gives users some control with Private Session and the option to exclude certain tracks or playlists from the taste profile. However, users still need a simpler session-based option.

The feature could work like this:

  • Turn on Incognito Mode
  • Listen normally
  • Spotify still plays music as usual
  • Those streams do not affect the user’s taste profile
  • Similar songs are not pushed into recommendations afterward
  • The mode automatically turns off after a set time, like Private Session

This would give users more control over personalization without removing personalization completely. It would also make Spotify feel more accurate because recommendations would reflect what users genuinely enjoy, not every temporary, social, or situational listening session.

Please consider adding a true Incognito Mode for Spotify listening.
author: riccixrose

title: “Spotify Sing – Live Social Singing Feature”
body:
Idea Summary

I would like to suggest a feature called "Spotify Sing", an interactive live singing experience integrated directly into the Spotify ecosystem.

This feature would allow users to sing along to songs using synchronized lyrics and instrumental background tracks while streaming their performance live to listeners around the world or privately to friends.

Core Experience

Users can:

  • Sing live with synchronized lyrics

  • Use background instrumental music while original vocals are reduced or removed

  • Create public or private singing rooms

  • Perform songs for listeners worldwide

  • Receive comments, reactions, and feedback after performances

Unique Features

Live Singing Rooms:

Users can join live rooms where one singer performs while others listen.

Automatic Audience Mic Control:

Audience microphones remain muted during performances to avoid interruptions and maintain audio clarity.

Collaborative Singing:

Premium users could unlock:

  • Duet mode

  • Group singing

  • Long-distance collaborations

Community Interaction

Listeners can:

  • React to performances

  • Leave comments

  • Support talented singers

  • Discover new creators through music

Why This Feature Could Be Valuable

Many users already sing privately while listening to music. This feature could transform music listening into a social and emotional experience.

Potential benefits:

  • Increased user engagement

  • Higher time spent on platform

  • Stronger creator ecosystem

  • More premium subscription opportunities

  • Viral social interaction through performances and collaborations

Final Thoughts

This idea combines:

  • Music streaming

  • Karaoke-style singing

  • Live social interaction

  • Community engagement

into one immersive experience.

Thank you for taking the time to read this idea.
author: JAYANTH_BANDI

title: interactive activity feature for playlists
body:
I’d love for Spotify to add an optional interactive activity feature for playlists. Playlist creators could see when people are currently listening to their playlist, how many people listened today, and—only if listeners opt in—which friends are listening, replaying songs, saving the playlist, or reacting to songs.

 

This would make playlists feel more alive and social, almost like a shared listening room. It could include privacy settings such as Public, Friends Only, Anonymous, and Private, so listeners stay in control of what they share.

 

This would be especially useful for people who curate aesthetic, mood-based, party, study, workout, or story-driven playlists. It would help playlist creators understand what people connect with while giving listeners a fun way to discover music together.
author: Nikkiyoung

title: JJ's Bizarre Blog #79 - Kingdom of Music Part 2 - How British Steel Was Forged
body:
Greetings, salutations and welcome back! It's time for the second half of the mighty megablog about the music of the United Kingdom! In the previous part, we covered an immense diversity of sounds and ideas and how these have had an immense impact on the music of the entire world. But if all that weren't enough, there is one British-born phenomenon that is unlike anything else in the history of music. Allow me to tell you how one crazy idea by a bunch of eccentric Birmingham teens created one of the most recognizable and passionate fanbases in the world, this of course being heavy metal! Without further ado, let's dive in!

 

spotify:track:2ISPUUp4pzssWuR2Ic09vR:small 

spotify:track:5Cxxh3q7bNWV0RE8ys1ScU:small 

spotify:track:4Alx3P79IVFjAwaqNR2ibQ:small 

spotify:track:7hf0X6r7aRB8eh5kpBvkFN:small 

 

To understand how metal was born, we need to trace the source of the river. Fundamentally, it's a subgenre of rock music and as luck would have it, the UK has had no shortage of amazing talent across any genre of rock imaginable! We already covered much of the more popular ones in the first part, including the British invasion beat sound, soft rock, pop rock and more. Beyond that, there's been plenty of hard rock, rock 'n' roll, psychedelic rock and much more with names such as Dire Straits, Eric Clapton, Jeff Beck, Cream, The Yardbirds just to name a few achieving legendary status worldwide! When the Brits get a hold of guitars, magic happens! 

 

spotify:track:4nFNJmjfgBF7jwv2oBC45b:small  spotify:track:1ydZRag9jS1VAXcUs8DAu9:small  spotify:track:0PNt5WTw092eED0ru9SuIp:small 
spotify:track:4CNL4GBNTdVIU5Nk6hB4LC:small  spotify:track:77wOpffkkQtZpdrvhPKbFq:small  spotify:track:1kgdslQYmeTR4thk9whoRw:small 
spotify:track:2hjAc6x8EoSLhGxRNg8KEw:small  spotify:track:69P1kjXbkuzJNBY32Vr70e:small  spotify:track:0GxV3poTM7Z7fKSUqy5qFw:small 

 

One of the most crucial components of the British rock sphere was the amazing progressive rock movement born in the 60s. We were blessed with a whole litany of bands like  King Crimson, Yes, Emerson, Lake & Palmer, Jethro Tull, Genesis, Procol Harum, Marillion and many more, all producing long, complex compositions with multiple movements, exquisite musicianship, infusions of folk and classical elements, metaphorical lyrics, concept albums and more. Some even got pretty heavy and quite bizarre. This produced an elevated, more intellectual rock experience that inspired many and became a quintessential aspect of British music in the decade! Once again, the unusual managed to captivate the masses and achieve great fanfare! The prog rock movement greatly expanded what was possible within the confines of guitar-driven rock, showing it could be far more than just short, catchy tunes for the radio and the dancehalls. Naturally, this open-minded approach would go on to inspire countless!

 

spotify:track:39AAt95Ts6PIHgMJfnHTNr:small  spotify:track:466C6kwoJHLUJ8VHG98hCY:small  spotify:track:2Zb1oaMRMUsyagT6gKV9TV:small 
spotify:track:1FRi4xi7dnViWnxatYwnxh:small  spotify:track:4Yw37wsXSsz5V61IBS8cYh:small  spotify:track:7fJ336lpndYK0Kn1tLPmGC:small 
spotify:track:3mIFvPNCRsSPh1SyJM3l0A:small  spotify:track:0GSNE8polfFfxGWnyx4knV:small  spotify:track:7DsQgIwg23u9gooCxkRTu3:small 
spotify:track:2QSuqxALyh7JFoQEBxyo0H:small  spotify:track:4Ok5k67CmcMwqTjYyqyR48:small  spotify:track:2bOMgHIlgr7wcPN3LSK9Rq:small 

 

Of course, no other progressive rock band of the era has achieved the success of Pink Floyd! Through their brilliant songwriting, unparalleled musicianship from all members and genius lyricism dealing with powerful subjects both personal and societal, they became a cultural institution! Many of their albums remain some of the finest pieces of music ever produced! David Gilmour and Roger Waters became a songwriting duo to rival Lennon and McCartney and their work was instrumental in bringing progressive rock to the masses and helping open the gate to many, so that they can discover an entire new world of music!

 

spotify:track:3TO7bbrUKrOSPGRTB5MeCz:small  spotify:track:5HNCy40Ni5BZJFw1TKzRsC:small  spotify:track:6zGFOp6rJZbYSp9DQO5AHY:small 
spotify:track:3ieDOGcOqVxScs2VZBzidt:small  spotify:track:2dxfyqdrI8XVOT3H1YcOsU:small  spotify:track:0OtkNB4TnTzrlid4zipYDV:small 

 

All of this amazing music would reach the ears of a certain bunch of weirdos in the underground scenes of Birmingham. The guitarist would lose his fingers in an accident, forcing him to play lower, slower and with different techniques. The vocalist would channel his love of horror films and the occult into some dark and macabre lyrics unlike anything else heard before. And the bassist and drummer would bring their love of blues to form one of the finest rhythm sections ever. This band would be known as Black Sabbath and this new sound would be known as heavy metal! Their work was much darker and more sinister than anything else out at the time and the low tuned riffage was heavy and overwhelming. They were gravely misunderstood at first, but quickly they gained appreciation and suddenly outcasts all over the world resonated with music in a whole new way! Black Sabbath's work is unmatched, their influence immeasurable! Music itself would never be the same again.

 

spotify:track:6KmJMlyPp0rPR7LloToCaF:small  spotify:track:3Jnxngdff0lVu2rza1GVx6:small  spotify:track:079j6LwNuB2sn81TkVl8js:small 
spotify:track:3XclwoQxz4p6Dl7VUf90KW:small  spotify:track:0p9dEwFFw14hWTvS9v5NLU:small  spotify:track:2KzvrcvbF1vivbHpPthtB9:small 
spotify:track:29KYPEsbcMcVKnd6iyu6Q5:small  spotify:track:42aY1pEcjp3GWENE6LUPnx:small  spotify:track:45J20uuWLwg2WluxUHwUd6:small 

 

But as massive as they may be, Sabbath weren't alone in catapulting this new sound. Another crucial player would be the one and only Deep Purple! Having already been around several years before Sabbath, Deep Purple have danced between hard rock and heavy metal throughout their illustrious career, creating a unique balance of heaviness and melody, of simplicity and complexity. While not a prog rock back outright, they bring a lot of the prog influence into their sound as they have no shortage of long, multi-layered and/or chaotic songs and they're known for their lengthy instrumental improvisations sections when playing live. Deep Purple can blow your socks off with speed and energy or soothe your soul with incredible ballads. They can make earwormy hits or advanced compositions you can analize for days. And it's all brilliant! I mean, when your members are all easily among the best at their respective instruments, how could the result be anything other than magical?

 

spotify:track:1shQu8Wqk50TnHek8LEOKk:small  spotify:track:6bofgga9Ld54uPgEHlr0Ne:small  spotify:track:27WqFiOFm2u1hax2ksdyqK:small 
spotify:track:34RISamoO5iubsOanSlpQo:small  spotify:track:2JhJOPGvtqMpj5RQC8cIYf:small  spotify:track:2Vim06YsRb4uJRpbeq6NR6:small 
spotify:track:5xu6aPH06QRtfTqwckG7Pb:small  spotify:track:07W9HcJyjXPnKQSMbUcWZU:small  spotify:track:1XQg8k5LfaOZaRsYefnghq:small 

 

And then, there was Led Zeppelin! Another collective of absolutely genius musicians, this enchanting quartet would round out the "Unholy Trinity" of English bands that helped birth heavy metal! Zeppelin are known for their amazing riffage, infectious energy and rich, yet approachable sound. They have in their catalogue some of the most iconic rock and metal songs of all time! Like Purple, they have no shortage of catchy hit songs, but are also never afraid to be experimental and embrace more of their proggy side! Yet they also never shied away from increasing the intensity and speed to new highs, which was just the fuel needed for heavy metal to blossom!

 

spotify:track:5CQ30WqJwcep0pYcV4AMNc:small  spotify:track:78lgmZwycJ3nzsdgmPPGNx:small  spotify:track:0hCB0YR03f6AmQaHbwWDe8:small 
spotify:track:4PRGxHpCpF2yoOHYKQIEwD:small  spotify:track:0QwZfbw26QeUoIy82Z2jYp:small  spotify:track:6hu1f1cXSw7OAqhpSQ2zDy:small 
spotify:track:2eDdFHgqNJltzlvlZFVDWd:small  spotify:track:4ItljeeAXtHsnsnnQojaO2:small  spotify:track:0GRBFjza23Im7Xb16c9AaK:small 

 

Over the course of the 70s and beyond, many bands would arise and other open-minded experimentalists would fuse the proggy sound of the 60s with something meaner and heavier, with Uriah Heep being one such prime example of an underrated band that was crucial to metal's early years! The movement was a fact and the momentum was undeniable! Something new and powerful had been born. And one couldn't have imagined at the time what would follow from here!

 

spotify:track:6tEjBy9tS1LTECMV3sTGr2:small  spotify:track:3AZWaeq95eyHsak4idN7oh:small  spotify:track:70gPbRC5ZBYJjZ0OEuqyTd:small 
spotify:track:1IXUA3AV9t4IFPcWT7iK9i:small  spotify:track:2XrGq155mgTwVGblyfq0W7:small  spotify:track:06s4tUycpedNdG8JCSWMr7:small 

 

As the 70s rolled on, metal would truly come into its own thanks to the work of one band - Judas Priest! Also from Birmingham (which kind of becomes the metal capital of the UK as you will see), this group of misfits would help metal move away from the blues and progressive rock it spawned from and develop into its own unique style. Infusing a bit of biker culture and new gen edge to their personas, they would forge an even heavier, more distorted and more riff-driven style that would define what heavy metal is! While for older bands the line might've been blurry, there's no hiding it, this definitely isn't hard rock anymore! To this day, Priest remain titans of heavy metal, the gold standard by which all others are judged!

 

spotify:track:55cd3NlqktNjhAZcPvaUvk:small  spotify:track:17hS2mLPFdTzy7YcpBDv0A:small  spotify:track:2yopMqEC6dKePLpRTUbzJN:small 
spotify:track:2iAtvBE3MIBtVV1fkrFdxH:small  spotify:track:28boKyWlmdSup9JoERrQur:small  spotify:track:08hn5vq1XnVmVQI4mfzsXa:small 
spotify:track:5x1ChlBgkxWf0i1n2NFwKf:small  spotify:track:6qopop1Rc9r1RjEIBQhc8d:small  spotify:track:0Ppw2ttPYrzMVbwJbyfZRL:small 

 

Their evolution of the sound even got its own name to signify the movement that followed - New Wave of British Heavy Metal (NWOBHM)! Riffy, heavy and full of attitude, this is where heavy metal truly came into its own! During the late 70s and early 80s, this genre ruled the roost thanks to bands like Saxon, UFO, Diamond Head, Angel Witch, etc. and served as a foundation for everything else the genre would evolve into!

 

spotify:track:2ov8L95QD25TLpZAZPYWXL:small  spotify:track:2E7W1X4maFFcjHrVrFA7Vs:small  spotify:track:1soxeK1ZBX9SsSjK4j4ECa:small  
spotify:track:7623cs9y5l1b6uPXZ4U2ny:small  spotify:track:6Ud2ag3Rxz2ak0KhLG3KXP:small  spotify:track:3jkxNGD8TU9Jv9DId3Zt0X:small 
spotify:track:7LYxaN0EpwhRoheZ3eySLw:small  spotify:track:7Do01GxFyR7InV31rhROwZ:small  spotify:track:55HmWFBCXVjQdx7p1iySET:small 
spotify:track:4Z9dRIoELtDgGXzTAtEHmp:small  spotify:track:58KPGS8ZtjD4NH80UUkjws:small  spotify:track:46PpkKMj6ZZtG4K8Opb7F6:small 

 

By far the biggest name born of this era though is without a shadow of a doubt the one and only Iron Maiden! Right at the dawn of the 80s, these guys came in like a storm and changed the game forever! Their dual guitar work was mesmerizing, the speed and intensity was unmatched and any lingering rock lyrics about romance or partying were substituted with poetic tales of history, mythology, war and fantasy! Their songs were insanely memorable and found great success, but they also never shied of once again showing that prog rock influence with some lengthy and complex compositions full of rich nuance. Iron Maiden had it all and they changed the game! You can literally split the history of metal up into before them and after them. With their unparalleled worldwide renown, a strong argument can be made that this so boldly and proudly British band are the greatest heavy metal band of all time!

 

spotify:track:21wB3DaIk112Ry3mEzqfit:small  spotify:track:71kapcyWLxdv7kQpM6jFTv:small  spotify:track:51S9pXdACCrhputBUl05Cs:small 
spotify:track:4OROzZUy6gOWN4UGQVaZMF:small  spotify:track:4i4t5Asb8cJPzcbmZ5b15u:small  spotify:track:6p8eFfPw3nQkf37aT3AkmK:small 
spotify:track:3wFGRek61NIF330UwJCI52:small  spotify:track:2lIAye5LMhs7EfiWDTGC2y:small  spotify:track:4L6lZetSWtn949AGIhd5aR:small 

 

But let's dial it back a bit. Before we proceed, there's another insane musical movement from the UK we must talk about. While progressive rock was amazing and all and we've already witnessed its long lasting influence, during the 70s, a certain movement across the pond sought to go in the opposite direction - trim the excess, go back to basics and make rock music simple and punchy again! And this new movement had made its way back to the UK. I'm of course talking about punk rock! The intense, rebellious, politically-charged bands of the era became notorious and became emblematic of the counter-culture of the era, anthems for disenfranchised youth. The UK was a most fertile breeding ground for iconic bands and where the genre really exploded into popularity! By far the most legendary act were the one and only Sex Pistols! These wild-cards only needed one album to write their names in the history books and become the poster children of punk for generations to come, visually, sonically and in terms of their rebellious attitude and nonconformist beliefs.

 

spotify:track:4YjWUQQQaoJ86uj1ru7Zin:small  spotify:track:3NnRRCRN0e4ZDypr11FhJZ:small  spotify:track:5DfIXijlQ4ZxA1ZpWuZlkG:small 
spotify:track:5jzma6gCzYtKB1DbEwFZKH:small  spotify:track:6h54MGmPGmerDMBgHTgIA4:small  spotify:track:7p949cKSO5MDSjajSrAcfI:small 
spotify:track:3amzp9lWccX2kgh2zu30B3:small  spotify:track:7xwRAqrRCQCvv7GB2MFuL8:small  spotify:track:1oJXZbdVdFrx1mSQPPWbhf:small 

 

The power of UK punk only became stronger with the advent of hardcore punk! This even heavier and more intense variety was a massive hit and captivated many worldwide to fight for what they believe in! Bands like English titans Discharge or Scottish legends The Exploited defined the sound of the genre and the very essence of what it means to be a punk rocker! Their influence was so strong, the "Britishness" could even be felt rubbing off on bands from all other corners of the globe.

 

spotify:track:5RzgnWHlAQd2L4OCAz5JCW:small  spotify:track:4KbnTUiBGp7kdggQJCybKU:small  spotify:track:2i7krlxMAsbGsLjzUJdg9d:small 
spotify:track:6GaG4UfZdCwifHqrt7P7XV:small  spotify:track:0zVcO7hz4JaznPwF0Me4IP:small  spotify:track:1KrD4rUW1GoRh7y53TYTSG:small 
spotify:track:5Vc9Ozg0PTSXlFwoPwJ7YK:small  spotify:track:0URyCfp1oO1KOCQ8jc3Fz5:small  spotify:track:04zgdvDOg0DJpp5UMulQCg:small 

 

Given that metal was itself a music for and by outcasts, the crossover with punk was inevitable! This would start taking form in the brief speed metal movement! It's early hints already came from pioneering bands, mainly Deep Purple. And while they may reject the label, the rock 'n' roll legends Motörhead defined the sound, with their fast, intense playing, simple no-nonsense songs and free-spirited outlook on life made them beloved by both metalheads and punks alike! Speed metal sought to increase the speed, heaviness and intensity of metal and this would go on to be the nexus point of basically all of the genre's evolution to come!

 

spotify:track:3qnlwueYpz5WZ06FKaXUoT:small  spotify:track:3bLfteEOjNnH7Gpa2NgSlF:small  spotify:track:1iW2ktyrQHNKZwFTvgP0Ta:small 
spotify:track:16CovcwRSjvpz0I4ZFflUv:small  spotify:track:5YxehSAZjBMcCIUlxGPit9:small  spotify:track:5gp9UXTiUKw0EbKdmdln9O:small 

 

The most vital player in sparking the change were the Geordie lords of darkness Venom! Maybe you've heard an album that has spawned a genre before. Well, how about an album that spawned three?! That is the case with their seminal Welcome to Hell in 1981. It and it's follow up Black Metal the next year showcased a level of darkness, heaviness, speed and intensity unheard of at the time. The imagery and presentation was something else too! Just like that, extreme metal was born and there was no going back. It was a true paradigm shift and it will echo throughout all of metal's remaining history!

 

spotify:track:0px83Dlh4SzQLCvYRNZzVF:small  spotify:track:0aKxshm6dqSEuSuLh2xRJG:small  spotify:track:3y0fywMSE5OD4I59jey93S:small 
spotify:track:3GlVq6iVOxZJQ8gPm84avl:small  spotify:track:1yfQXeHWDCVdSnoWWUV4j4:small  spotify:track:7rLqQQUXxvxqjuXbf7fVVw:small 

 

Probably the most pivotal spawn under the extreme metal umbrella would be death metal! While this heavy and ruthless genre was an invention of the US (and Sweden), it didn't take long for the UK, sandwiched between the founders, to join the fray. Already in the late 80s, several bands were building up British death metal and the visceral medical visuals of Carcass, the historical war tales of Bolt Thrower and the oldschool horror goodness of Cancer just to name a few, all managed to make an impact and leave a lasting impression. What stands out is also how brutally heavy these guys are! Their sound is absolutely relentless in terms of speed, heaviness and sheer might, much more so than many other bands of the era. I can't even fathom how people in the 80s must've reacted to this stuff! To this day, death metal has a strong presence across the UK and they remain one of the genre's most fertile breeding grounds!

 

spotify:track:6MChOMxPtyDjDt7LZ4ePYO:small  spotify:track:59mAOFlD6Qg0ygG5l3BdCS:small  spotify:track:27XnhL8BZYitaaPz8IdMfq:small 
spotify:track:0iXeMjJiEFa45KOM1GGXx4:small  spotify:track:1XqgFmOLxWfP0FezPiNsO2:small  spotify:track:46cTlOJsRIN4ajWxX0eJTG:small 
spotify:track:1cX9kgTnu8abiAP2ZRVvLh:small  spotify:track:6tkNuPDvyu3rmXwYuYq5PY:small  spotify:track:04mIoJC6FiJI7mbGPKH1lV:small 

 

A huge influence that helped grow the genre and boost its heaviness is the concurrently developing grindcore movement! This offshoot of punk was built on one principle - pushing the speed and intensity of hardcore to the absolute extreme. With blisteringly short songs, relentless brutality and unfiltered political lyrics, grindcore was the even more rebellious offspring of the punk rock movement and the UK was a prime player here too. As death metal joined the party, it mixed with the grind quite naturally thanks to the significant overlap in sound and thus a feedback loop of extreme sounds was born!

 

spotify:track:54f2jEBmRrrM7E4WnkTPVX:small  spotify:track:2IUO6hWyrG7FYDkJZrFpQP:small  spotify:track:0E0MVWqNolnZjQMMTlrw2e:small 
spotify:track:4UiP2ijOf7UPkmRa19H5fY:small  spotify:track:075jSmV95NQJxazrAEVda1:small  spotify:track:2DqTFM0q1HtVQrpGQBS30n:small 

 

Grindcore itself was pioneered and popularized by one band, one that remains the best of the best and unmatched in their might - the unstoppable Napalm Death. Also from Birmingham, these lads were there in the hayday of punk and were never satisfied with the power it brought. So they pushed further. And further. And further. And further. From the shortest song in the world, to popularizing blast beats in extreme music, Napalm Death are in a league of their own. Despite being known for brutality and heaviness, they also have a massive and varied discography that veers in all sorts of directions, so they're far from a one trick pony! They are the kings of extreme for a reason!

 

spotify:track:6TcTjkptOtIF6sXyTxb76L:small  spotify:track:1fe4LKaAU20ETlbgQQpSSx:small  spotify:track:3MVapVOpPTzWSM91WHRygU:small 
spotify:track:0OhRbz7O9H75Ze9BfLkwo1:small  spotify:track:5KUquxYX4L86p197AaPikA:small  spotify:track:0yuBn8hXlFof1pSY7as6Jm:small 

 

Given that Venom christened the term black metal, it seems natural to go here now! Despite technically inventing the genre, UK black metal only really took off in the modern age and now there's an abundance of amazing bands to choose from! They're known for their atmospheric sound, lengthy compositions and poetic lyrics. Given that black metal fans worship Tolkien and this is his homeland after all, there's no shortage of grandiose fantasy tales with lots of picturesque yet dark natural landscapes and folk legends. Bands like Winterfylleth and Wodensthrone also seek to revive and preserve the old Germanic Anglo-Saxon heritage that the isles was built on, tapping into the same mystical beliefs that their brethren in the mainland and over in Scandinavia would follow! 

 

spotify:track:0RMrP5IfK9O7LBzAcpg5pq:small  spotify:track:0seX6TaYjEH19FjE6En9h6:small  spotify:track:0jmOJj75mUlNk2SUqNia1F:small 
spotify:track:7c0DgUmLcg76YHNXmvOxHl:small  spotify:track:3wuwAS42qPQDkJ45FlaaIp:small  spotify:track:5m49R1MFuJJgiiry0F5f2H:small 
spotify:track:6rwAEMeIocYa5nkUCfgBLU:small  spotify:track:5TfTBnSFYFdRFReACuc04x:small  spotify:track:5IAaVZPHAUdXVuhVVo6z31:small 

 

Speaking of taping into ancient traditions, a unique phenomenon is the Scottish magic by Saor! Fusing atmospheric black metal with authentic folk instrumentation, including the iconic bagpipes, this band masterfully retells Scottish history and revives ancient Celtic myths through some dark, yet stunningly beautiful music! Trust me, no matter where you're from, you're going to feel 1000% Scottish listening to this!

 

spotify:track:2h3t6dEd8EzLSCjWKMZ4ZE:small  spotify:track:07CEGnBazaFxhDOwQX2Uas:small  spotify:track:35tY2FvLKICZMguzsRkYXH:small 

 

But let's dial it back a bit. I mentioned Tolkien just there, so you know what other genre loves them some Tolkien? Power metal of course! And especially in the last few decades, UK power metal has been absolutely popping off with amazing bands soaring to new heights! I mean, quite literally all the ingredients to make power metal came from the UK - Tolkien, a lot of other classic fantasy, Rob Halford and Bruce Dickinson's operatic tenors, Iron Maiden's dual guitar, the elevated musicianship, all of it! To many today, Dragonforce are synonymous with what power metal is and their face-melting solos have been amazing people worldwide, while their nerdy, cheesy approach has been a breath of fresh air to a genre mainly thought stale. So going off of that, many young power metal bands such as Scotland's absolutely epic space-fearers GloryHammer have been pioneers of the goofier, more daft flavour of power metal, taking the genre in bold new directions, while keeping the epicness, fun factor and musical brilliance intact!

 

spotify:track:52fVArVuVU3VeYrehHxezE:small  spotify:track:3Lmu3dtUQo2etxILOEtRrH:small   spotify:track:6YPHNCfBpq0scOlQFRIBFc:small 
spotify:track:18UJqCFHca2UVjZIrAHbE7:small  spotify:track:4caxH2UtmKJnZ3wkDHI6rE:small  spotify:track:4w63cAIVIEZFmozOXVzf1B:small 

 

When it comes to folk metal, the UK have long drawn on their rich heritage that I mentioned in the first half. Even during the prog days of the 60s, many bands were implementing folk elements into their music and tapping into traditional tales for inspiration. So naturally, we got bands like Skyclad from Newcastle, universally regarded as one of the earliest ever folk metal bands! The tradition has not waned since. If anything, it's gotten even stronger with the incredible Scottish party animals Alestorm bursting onto the scene with their pirate metal bangers and conquering the seven seas in short order, quickly becoming not only one of the most popular folk metal bands in the world, but one of the biggest names in metal as a whole!

 

spotify:track:3PuIb9PEB8V97MGICfcxko:small  spotify:track:5K6px63UDlv61Etcs8rED7:small  spotify:track:6h04E4QwtdTZcbMHzNSrSn:small 
spotify:track:719yUkUFKsIrmGA7noLuuk:small  spotify:track:0jGDLQLtQqq34NSgeMmxgv:small  spotify:track:191tuYYkPXaxD25vBjKbFZ:small 

 

We can go back even further though. For when Black Sabbath created heavy metal, they spawned its first subgenre too - doom metal! Many bands were inspired particularly by Sabbath's slow, low tuned sound and dark, macabre lyrics and sought to expand from there. So naturally, throughout all the decades, as metal evolved, branched out and spread worldwide, there was a concurrent doom metal movement enjoying the slower, more melancholic side of life. That's to say, the UK has always been a stronghold for doom and you can say the drab weather definitely helped with setting the right mood to find the inspiration.

 

spotify:track:7sDqKv83Uwg2bhRysgAzzd:small  spotify:track:0dIVW5xGEYJPpeusB6t9dG:small  spotify:track:7adyJzkXS3Txlc0dho3dE7:small 
spotify:track:1pa4nD5y2AgGSW1S5BClBg:small  spotify:track:03HrsQ9ACv7UKtDcKowz96:small  spotify:track:4RgU0JeiVoxUls69DSdc09:small 

 

This has been true for all the major doom metal offshoots too. When stoner metal came into being, the UK quickly sprang into action with some of the finest and most iconic bands the genre has ever seen. Meanwhile, bands like Esoteric (from Birmingham yet again) have pioneered the funeral doom sound, seeking to slow down the music to the absolute extreme and bringing in death metal influences to make the sound into an unfathomably heavy, nigh immovable object. Naturally, they remain one of the finest in the genre to this day and many others have followed along this path less travelled.

 

spotify:track:0GetEYll0UA71r6lA6FFn0:small  spotify:track:2uJ1rFtKH05i4YwfF6nVrz:small  spotify:track:3ZL2kD1p8ch1rRC82VoiCq:small 
spotify:track:6GWBLpyJdf3MOEVENei0UM:small  spotify:track:0lCayTswl9yixRT7kasR8t:small  spotify:track:61k1gqRs1sYwKlpqqpRjtX:small 

 

On a similar melancholic note, we must also talk about gothic metal! I already mentioned how the gothic rock sound was born and spread here during the 70s, so the heavier variety would soon follow just as the influence of metal spread and evolved. The UK is the proud home of the so-called "Peaceville Three", named after the record label they were all under. These are Paradise Lost, My Dying Bride and Anathema. All three followed a similar trajectory - they all started as very heavy and dark death/doom metal bands, helping bring that particular style into the light. Later, all transitioned into the catchier and much more approachable, but equally as dark and melancholic gothic metal sound, full of feelings of loss, dread and failed romance. While the three have followed very different paths from then on, they've left a tremendous legacy and both the death/doom and gothic metal genres have much to thank them for!

 

spotify:track:5OTOUyo18hGb3p1QeLOsp8:small  spotify:track:5WNz7onFzlYrQRH3MgQ299:small  spotify:track:5irUevzIe38NCkXjXK7srD:small 
spotify:track:3mfwwUEFwXERswYBqhKcTC:small  spotify:track:2kLIFARU4q1RIwHwCQUkpp:small  spotify:track:5DMALvd1DmmWc3iVRSXlno:small 
spotify:track:7izYm6GQBOwPRwi8YW6TkX:small  spotify:track:1kBxzHxuYvAEV0wtMNSOJt:small  spotify:track:0luj50yAtvSwjumr5Kd8j3:small 

 

But now for something completely different. In the previous part, we covered the immense legacy the UK have left in the world of electronic music and this has trickled into the industrial sphere too! The genre was pioneered in the 70s by the experimentalists from Throbbing Gristle, who are also credited with coining the genre's name. This is one genre that quickly overlapped with metal, creating a meeting point for outcasts of both realms to enjoy some dark club bangers. During the 80s, the mighty machines Godflesh (created by Napalm Death founder Justin Broadrick, so you'll never guess the hometown) became foundational to industrial metal, helping create an enveloping, distorted sound mixing guitars and crushing bass with computerized drum beats, effects and samples to create bleak, dystopian landscapes. Other bands like Pitchshifter followed suit and later helped evolve the genre into all kinds of crazy directions. The rest, as they say, is history.

 

spotify:track:0NERfJl0vNI9MyrblzdWLx:small  spotify:track:4qVI1SgIKvAjFFQgkLGMKT:small  spotify:track:1kAUZ8xm4hcAn00TnqP2oQ:small 
spotify:track:6PkXXhO1DFwI5ne35TohoH:small  spotify:track:4TU35XXlvDdxLGbsWGlfNu:small  spotify:track:1svC5Gwom4WDLD5vJDYit3:small 

 

As we move into the modern age, metalcore was a genre that became dominant and would go on to define the sound of a generation. Keeping up with their huge impact from the past, UK bands were among the biggest, most popular and most influential during the era. Some even called it a second British invasion, a new gen of metal-infused boy bands to conquer the charts. Call it whatever you want, but the UK metalcore and deathcore scenes have been strong for decades and remain strong to this day!

 

spotify:track:39iwths0yXkYoB2dWYlNn2:small  spotify:track:0ruUpqFjByhpDqn8SN29C2:small  spotify:track:3ZFP6XrE0FHu4duyi7vRoL:small 
spotify:track:0QnDGxWFJDj1mXusWBWIp3:small  spotify:track:2rfrRs263AiX0RBNdR3lmB:small  spotify:track:7heXxhE7xu0aW2ZtqTD1P3:small 
spotify:track:5oxfvvsW3cXmfZzOc1bEqz:small  spotify:track:6X03JVTjxT5bWtEY3W7X4U:small  spotify:track:4537kerrZrPCs8HeJ59BiJ:small 

 

In fact, some of the very roots of the scene as we know it come from the Isles, as one of the most foundational bands to modern metalcore are the Welsh superstars Bullet For My Valentine! With their potent riffs, amazing melodies, infectious hooks and of course frontman Matt Tuck's exquisite blend of clean and harsh vocals, they took the world by storm in the early 2000s and gave lots of exposure to this new and emerging metalcore sound. Many of us had fallen in love with it before we even knew what this style was called! 

 

spotify:track:4KZV2EUOsmBRCAcwOBjLhK:small  spotify:track:3YY0aeelsH2rzffcZ56zjY:small  spotify:track:7tFxCn0Qb75pbx993wqUIQ:small 
spotify:track:2QFPIjaa5E99aGdcVjUdo7:small  spotify:track:2dbKMVQMjThcJ6GIdWK5Tb:small  spotify:track:4mCfa2wz3OUvldLnhJnW2K:small 

 

So there you go, just a sliver of the wide and varied metal that the UK have blessed us with! Just like I mentioned in the previous part, there're countless young bands that still carry the torch onward to this day and keep showcasing that no matter what, that Britain hasn't lost that special air all these years later and they keep producing new brilliant artists to captivate the world. Many of them also defy traditional genre definitions and go in all manner of crazy directions to do whatever they want! You have the melodic metal sensation The Raven Age (formed by George Harris, son of Iron Maiden bassist Steve Harris) fusing metalcore sensibilities with a classic sound for a really enchanting experience! The absolutely insane party that is Skindred from Newport, Wales offer an unbelievable blend of metal, reggae, funk, hip-hop and more for a sound unlike anything else! New age experimental bands like Akercocke fuse 90% of metal subgenres into a chaotic and eclectic vibe. Enter Shikari blend metalcore with electronics for a potent fusion of power. And all of that is just a drop in the English Channel of what is out there today and of all the amazing unique bands the UK has spawned!

 

spotify:track:4Jb9OsiWAid3E1zZxTlik2:small  spotify:track:7FRcx3b2aGYwXVaDY2fYRS:small  spotify:track:5ItFDJsntX2QGNb5ZeoCSG:small 
spotify:track:581mukhDjta60CCwpvnjL9:small  spotify:track:6Rx6cs6WOspk55d0QDedSO:small  spotify:track:31zbtbYSurZtbMWIM4ZXAK:small 
spotify:track:3gvNUbSO3tjs4iKEXd6WlL:small  spotify:track:6E1B1ZcZJrXhq0idD4oDd7:small  spotify:track:102vOKAF3ZrSGgEaDKmiUG:small 

 

Phew, so there it is! The full spectrum of music from the United Kingdom, presented in full! Now, it would be the height of folly to claim I've covered everything, but I'm confident in saying that at the very least, you now grasp the full scope of just how much incredible, world-altering music has come from here! It's this sheer breadth of amazing work from every genre imaginable that inspired me to want to do this project and give these legends their due respect! Not to mention that, on a personal note, I've grown up with so much of this music that it's practically seared into my heart forever. Focusing on the metal side of things, given that it is my favourite genre, I could never thank the Brits enough for inventing and kickstarting this crazy, beautiful thing! There's probably no other musical fandom that's so passionate, devoted, yet also so globally spread as metal! And the craziest part is, as demonstrated in this blog, the British didn't just invent it, they were there every step of the way as it evolved and branched out. It feels like almost every subgenre blossomed there early on or was outright pioneered by a British band. No matter where in the history of metal you look, the footprint is there and the influence is undeniable! While metal today is a worldwide phenomenon, it cannot be denied, this outcast genre is British-born and British-raised! But as I've shown, even all that is just half the story. So I hope that, if you somehow didn't realize how deep the roots went for UK music and how much of the sounds of the world they've influenced, now you know and have gained some newfound appreciation. Enjoy exploring and keep discovering! Take care and see you all next time! Excelsior!

author: Joan

title: Emotion Histogram
body:
Core Idea

Allow listeners/watchers to react to exact moments in a podcast episode using quick emoticons or reaction markers.

Those reactions generate a visual “Emotion Histogram” across the episode timeline, showing where listeners experienced:

  •               suspense
  •               shock
  •               laughter
  •               excitement
  •               confusion
  •               important moments

This helps users discover high-impact moments naturally without needing comments or spoilers.

**bleep**

title: Would like a setting that gives users the option to not be shown ai music
body: I seem to be getting more and more AI generated music suggested to me by the spotify algorithm whether its via the dj, discover weekly, etc. Although i do think it should be allowed for people to generate and list AI generated music i personally don't want to be consuming it so i think it would be nice for users to individually be able to decide for themselves if they want to be shown AI generated music or not. It also brings up the issue of intellectual property rights as I have heard numerous songs that are very clearly AI generated but blatantly copy riffs from popular songs.
author: danjobanjo13

title: Feature Suggestion Add a ‘Play Similar Songs’ Option- Add a ‘Play Similar Songs’  options
body:
I’m a college student and a regular Spotify user, and I wanted to share a small feature idea that I genuinely feel could make the app experience even better.

 

It would be really cool if Spotify added a “Play Similar Songs” option directly in the song pop-up menu (where options like Add to Queue, Add to Playlist, etc. already exist). So if someone is listening to a song and taps this option, Spotify could instantly continue with tracks that match the same vibe, mood, energy, or style.

 

One reason I thought of this is because during hangouts with friends or family, the person controlling the music suddenly becomes responsible for keeping the vibe alive. There’s weird pressure to constantly think of the next song and avoid awkward mood shifts. A feature like this would make the experience feel way smoother and more effortless instead of making people manually search every few minutes.

 

I also feel this could naturally increase listening time and help people discover more music without leaving the flow of the moment.

 

Thanks for making a platform that’s such a big part of everyday life for so many of us. Thought I’d share this idea in case the team finds it interesting.

 

Best,
Rudraksh Gupta

author: MihailY

title: More Variety in Spotify Radio & Generated Playlists
body:
I find it really frustrating that so many Spotify-generated playlists and radios based on my music taste basically contain the exact same songs over and over again.

 

The whole reason I choose a radio or a generated playlist is because I want to discover something different when I’m tired of my own music. But instead, it often feels like I’m just listening to the same playlist in different versions.

 

Even across completely different playlists, many of the same songs keep repeating. I’d love more variety, deeper discovery, and less repetition in Spotify’s recommendations.

title: Feature Request: Remove the 5,000 listener requirement for Spotify Countdown Pages
body:
Hello Spotify Team,
 
I am writing to request a change to the accessibility of the Spotify Countdown Pages feature for albums and EPs. Currently, this powerful pre-release tool is locked behind a strict minimum requirement of 5,000 monthly active listeners. 
 
As an independent artist planning a long-term album campaign, this requirement puts developing creators at a major disadvantage. Competitor platforms, such as Apple Music, provide "Upcoming Release" and "Pre-Add" modules with a hidden track list to every single artist on their platform from day one, regardless of listener count.
 
Allowing all artists to use Countdown Pages would:
  1. Help independent acts organically build momentum, pre-saves, and monthly listeners before their album drops.
  2. Level the playing field between independent creators and established or major-label artists.
  3. Encourage artists to drive traffic directly to Spotify early in their marketing campaigns.
Please consider removing the 5,000 listener threshold or lowering it significantly for full-length album and EP distributions. Thank you for supporting the independent music community!

title: Classical metadata issue: Track titles incomplete, only showing movement markings
body:
I’m seeing a major issue with classical track titles on Spotify. Across many releases, tracks are now displaying only the movement title instead of the full work title + movement title. For example, a track that should appear as something like “Symphony No. 2 in D Major, Op. 43: Allegro” may now only show as “Allegro” or “1. Allegro.” This makes classical music very difficult to browse, because movement markings like Allegro, Andante, Adagio, etc. are not meaningful without the parent work title.

 

This is especially problematic given Spotify’s playlist-forward model. When classical tracks are saved to playlists, mixed with other works, or viewed outside the context of the full album, titles like “Allegro” or “Andante” become impossible to identify. You can’t tell what piece you’re actually looking at while browsing a playlist. This appears to be happening across multiple classical labels and releases, so it does not seem to be an issue with one specific album or a metadata choice by one record label. It seems like Spotify may be displaying only the movement field/title rather than the complete classical track title. I spoke to someone at the BIS record label who said they were also concerned about this change happening across Spotify. 

 

Could Spotify please investigate this as a classical metadata/display issue? It's very important that classical tracks display the full work title along with the movement title, as they did previously. 

 

Plan

Premium
author: jandjfilms

title: #MusicMonday Review - June 2026
body:
#MusicMonday is the hashtag I've been using for quite a while to share music recommendations from up-and-coming artists. Always fresh, and always different, trying to look for trends before they become one. You can check May's review for more music.

 

On this issue, our travels take us to the UK, for a selection of Indie gems, with a bluesy touch at the end. Give them a listen, with a word from the artists themselves. 🎧

 

KATANES – Is It Too Soon to Tell You I Love You?

 

you got me moving on overdrive
you're the calm inside my chaos
you're the reason I want to stay alive

 

and i want to know if you're gonna save me

 

We begin our music trip up in the very north in Caithness, Scotland for an Indie Rock track about taking the initiative for love. Sometimes good things come to those who don't wait:

 

"The song is about a certain person, wanting to take a risk to tell them something that could either go two ways.

 

[Band member] Adam took the risk and it went very well I’d say."

 

spotify:track:3sC4KTwoYy1L4nz7NXoabJ:small

 

Lyra – Way Out


 

Sometimes I feel like I can't get a prayer
Not even a whisper to tell me that you’re there


Running the wheel, breathing without air,

I swear life is but a dream and now

I'm looking for a way out

 

waiting for the day

when you can show me the way

 

We now travel to London, England, for a poppy Alt Rock track about hoping to detach from someone, but not knowing how to do so:

 

"It's the cost of tying your self-worth to external validation. At its core, the track reflects on dedicating everything to a singular purpose—only to realize it doesn’t bring the fulfillment you expected.

 

During the creation process we experimented a lot with building tension and release - keeping parts more stripped back before letting the chorus fully open up. We also leaned further into a bigger pop/rock-inspired sound with driving production, layered vocals and a more euphoric feel overall.


The final version ended up sounding much bigger and more energetic than the original demo, but that emotional core stayed the same throughout mainly thanks to Larry [Hibbitt] for helping massively in creating this energetic song."

 

spotify:track:1kTbQ784yroshrxHtAfuYH:small

 

Tiny Vessels – Fix It Up

 

 

will you call me now

will you call my name

breathing in, breathing out the pain

i miss you

 

and i know it's not the same

crying on the drive home

leave you where you lay

 

Let's go now to Cambridge, for an Indie Rock track about wishing to go back, at least just once, and be together. Band member Harry explains:

 

"So the track started with our guitarist Lee, who arranged the synths and original drum pattern, he sent it over to me and I immediately felt a wave of emotion in the song, I had been working on the theme of loss, losing someone very close to me far too early in their lives, and wishing I had another 5 minutes to say what I didn’t get the chance to say to them, so it’s in essence a sad song! I think it works on multiple levels too, it can also be associated with relationships, etc."

 

spotify:track:6xEcb4fcJCh6uj5jTtOady:small

 

Polo Lounge – Paradise

 

If I see you now and then,

an hour or another day

it doesn’t matter anyway

 

We pick up where we left off

then we go and then we go

until we go again

 

Don’t be a stranger

If you wanna follow me
We can get lost together
Anywhere you wanna be
We can get lost together

 

We drive now to Ramsgate, Kent for another Indie Rock track about that place you, and another person reach, where both would like to stay. It could be anywhere:

 

"The song is a story of friendships. Sometimes you don’t speak or see someone for a long time, but when you do see each other it’s like no time has passed. It’s inspired by different times and different friends. But in the end you’re still good friends and a sign of a good friendship is picking up where you left off!

 

“We could be anywhere”, which is a lyric in the song, is a quote of something me and my mates used to say while growing up in Ramsgate looking out at the sea or the harbour.

 

“You’re the one I’m needing now” that’s just about times when you might be missing your friends."

 

spotify:track:7yGcsbskHJGWaXGObJTE2g:small 

 

Benny Bianco – Mountain Blues

 

I'm going up the mountain just to clear my mind.
The woman that I love is proving hard to find.

 

I still love her ways and her smiling face.
But I feel our love is drifting. Now I need some space.

 

My case is packed. My route's already planned.
I've already made my peace about her having another man.

 

Our last stop is Aylesbury, Buckinghamshire, for a Blues track in the most classic way, a painful song about unattainable love:

 

"It wasn't inspired by an experience per se, more of a mood found in the old blues of Leroy Carr and Scrapper Blackwell, a mood I could almost parachute into, we've all felt like we want to just leave our worldly possessions and just go be a hermit but I think it was realizing bringing that blues idea into being through my own voice and way of saying it."

 

spotify:track:4CojGZzPz2mzWxzoyo722F:small

 

Listen to them and much more on the complete playlist

 

spotify:playlist:2IYRLjcwf2X9vC0QrI0b0P

 

See you next time!

title: Seeking Guidance: Quota Extension for Independent Developers (Aware of recent policy changes)
body:
Hi everyone,

I am an independent developer working on a non-commercial passion project using the Spotify Web API. I’ve recently hit a roadblock with the 5-user limit in Development Mode, and I would love to get some guidance or advice from the community and the Spotify team on how to move forward.

About the Project:
My application is a data-driven musical personality test. It analyzes a user's listening history and provides them with a fun, interactive breakdown of their music identity. To make the experience unique, I’ve integrated an AI engine (via Groq API) that generates highly personalized, witty, and engaging text summaries based on their stats. Users can also visually compare their musical profiles with friends within the platform. 

The Issue:
I want to open a small beta test to gather feedback and optimize the app. I am fully aware of the recent policy changes regarding the Web API and I understand that quota extensions are now strictly geared toward registered businesses. However, as an independent creator building a free hobby project, I do not have a registered company or a tax ID. I started this project with passion and have invested a significant amount of time into it, and I am hoping there is still a path forward so this project doesn't go to waste.

My Questions to the Community & Staff:
Acknowledging the new rules, is there absolutely any alternative review process, exception, or a temporary "Beta tier" for independent, non-commercial developers to test their app with slightly more users (e.g., 50 or 100)?
Has anyone in a similar position successfully requested a manual review of their app recently despite the new restrictions?
If creating a legal entity is the only way, is a basic free sole proprietorship (like a French "Auto-entrepreneur" micro-enterprise) generally accepted by the compliance team to fill out the extension form?

I am fully committed to respecting Spotify’s Developer Terms of Service and guidelines.

I would be more than happy to share screenshots, a video demonstration, or provide a test access to prove the project's compliance, security, and true intent. Thank you very much for your time, your insights, and your help!
Best regards,
Anto005
Independent Developer

author: PaulMac

title: One phone. Two headphones. One perfectly shared listening experience.
body:
Dear Spotify Product Team,

I'd like to share a product concept that I believe could evolve Spotify's social listening experience into something even more intuitive and emotionally engaging.

The Vision

Music creates moments between people. My vision is to make those moments effortless.

One phone. Two wireless headphones. One perfectly synchronized listening experience.

Imagine:

  • Two friends sharing music on a bus from a single phone.
  • A couple dancing together while each wears their own AirPods.
  • Two training partners staying perfectly in sync during a workout.
  • A figure skating pair practicing their entire routine from one phone placed beside the rink.
  • Any shared activity where timing matters and music becomes part of the experience.

The goal is simple:

One tap. Listen together.

Spotify Jam is a great foundation

Spotify Jam already makes it possible for people to share a queue and listen together.

What I believe is missing is a dedicated perfectly synchronized "Listen Together" experience focused on two people sharing the exact same beat.

This isn't primarily about collaborative playlists.

It's about creating one shared musical moment.

Whether you're:

  • training,
  • dancing,
  • practicing figure skating,
  • commuting,
  • or simply walking together,

both listeners experience every beat at exactly the same moment.

A vision bigger than Spotify alone

I understand that this idea also depends on technologies provided by Apple, Android, Bluetooth LE Audio, Auracast, and future operating system capabilities.

I'm not suggesting Spotify should solve every hardware limitation.

Instead, Spotify could become the company that defines the experience.

Where dual Bluetooth audio is available, Spotify could seamlessly use one phone with two wireless headphones.

Where it isn't available, Spotify could intelligently adapt using synchronized playback across two devices, while presenting exactly the same simple user experience.

The technology may differ.

The experience should not.

Why this matters

This isn't just another feature.

It's a new way of experiencing music together.

It's easy to understand.
It's emotional.
And it's highly marketable.

Imagine campaigns such as:

  • Train Together. Same Song.
  • Dance Together. Same Beat.
  • Skate Together. Same Rhythm.
  • Commute Together. Same Music.

Music has always connected people.

This feature would let them experience it together, in perfect sync.

title: Hide album artwork to protect sensitive viewers.

author: Melazurine

title: Re: Ai generated content should be labeled.
body:
@Manulongolo53

The point of art is that it takes practice and effort. By "democratizing" you actually mean "not having to put in any of the effort that real artists do because someone/something else does it all for me". It's the equivalent of commissioning someone to do it for you.

 

There are countless tools to compose music these days, so you don't even have to buy an instrument to produce music, but if you wanted to play a real instrument, it has never been easier to find cheap options. Music knowledge, in this era, is a skill that can be learned by practically anyone with internet access, not a resource only available to a select privileged few. All it takes to learn it is discipline, commitment, and a genuine interest in music, which AI "artists" with no skill seem to lack.

 

When you say "democratize", you are implying that the ability to create music is being unfairly gatekept by those who do know music. You see musicians as a privileged group, perhaps a group of naturally talented people that have an unfair advantage over everyone else. But in reality, the only one stopping you from becoming a musician is you. Because music, and art in general, like any other skill, must be learned and practiced. It's like how you have to practice a lot if you want to be competent at playing a sport, only art is more mental than physical.

 

Effort and practice are part of the process of becoming an artist and absolutely nobody is spared from it. If using an AI model to produce a result requires none of that effort, then you didn't actually do anything, you just told a program to do it for you.
author: 1256789PM_iker

title: Better mood filtering: Distinguish Positive Low-Energy Music from Sad Low-Energy Music
body:
There are users who like quiet, relaxing music, which is bright, peaceful, refreshing and clear-minded. However, when it comes to music that makes people calm, the algorithm considers them to be of the same mood.

 

For instance, there are songs that inspire one to think about sunny, cold, dry and open atmosphere – something optimistic, spacious and relaxing. Then, after a while, the system starts to suggest music which sounds gloomy, rainy, foggy or simply sad because of similar rhythm or instruments.

This is an absolutely different listening experience.

Spotify should distinguish between energy level and mood of songs.

 

Low-energy songs can be:

 

Peaceful and positive
Calm and uplifting
Bright and spacious
Refreshing and clear

 

Low-energy songs can also be:

Sad
Melancholic
Heartbreaking
Depressing

Currently, the system usually combines these types, which makes it hard to keep the atmosphere.

 

It would be really good if Spotify were able to notice the difference or add new controls regarding mood, so the user can choose among:

Positive or Sad
Sunny or Rainy
Bright or Gloomy
Peaceful or Melancholic

title: Re: Why do daily mixes keep disappearing?
body:
Hi there, @Infinitmon 

 

Thanks for reaching out and letting us know about this!

 

Keep in mind that the Daily Mix playlists learn what the user loves and offer up an evolving set of content based on their listening habits. Instead of being centered around music that’s new to you, Daily Mix is built around your favorite songs and songs you listen to frequently. 

 

In that case, if you don't use the app regularly the Daily mix doesn't get refreshed as it should (even though it doesn't refresh daily the songs included in each mix get updated roughly once a week). That might be the reason why the Daily Mix playlist you saved before is now empty.

 

We suggest using the app frequently and also not saving the Daily mix playlist itself if you like it, because it can change from time to time. Instead, you can create a new playlist on your own and select the songs you want from the Daily Mix or select all of them (you can do that with CTRL/CMD+A on the desktop app) and copy them in that new playlist you've created.

 

The Daily Mix playlists should always appear under the Made For You section of the app. Here you can find more info about all of this.

 

Hope this clears things up! If you have any other doubts, the Community will be here for you!

title: Explicit Content Spectrum and Slider
body:
Today's explicit content setting is a simple binary choice. Either allow all explicit music or play only music with no explicit content or inappropriate language. If movies worked this way we would have two choices for settings G rated content and everything else (PG, R, NC17, X). 

Instead, introduce a slider that has various levels of explicit content to allow
Mild - (no explicit content)
Medium - (the occasional or single explicit word used, no sexual or violent verses)
Heavy - (multiple explicit words, may have violent or sexual verses)
Unfiltered - (all content allowed, listen at your own risk)

Spotify Subscribers with school aged children, aging parents or others in our homes, cars, businesses would be able to enjoy a wider variety of songs and playlists on shuffle without having to screen them on our own beforehand.

title: Child friendly music streaming device
body:
Dear Spotify Team,

 

I’m writing as a parent and long-time Spotify user with a suggestion I’d genuinely love to see considered.

 

We have a 7-year-old who uses Spotify Kids and absolutely loves listening to music and stories. The challenge is that access currently depends on using a tablet, which naturally brings extra screen time and distractions that we’re trying to limit.

 

I’d love to see Spotify create a dedicated touchscreen music player designed just for listening — something inspired by old-school iPods or MP3 players. A simple standalone device with:

 

Spotify Kids support

Offline downloads and Wi-Fi sync

Easy touchscreen controls

No browser, games, or distracting apps

Parent controls and volume limits

Durable, kid-friendly design

Optional speaker and headphone support

 

There seems to be a gap between smart speakers (which require voice control) and tablets (which bring full-screen access). A focused music device could help children enjoy music independently while keeping screen time low.

 

I imagine many families would appreciate a simple “music-first” experience.

 

Thanks for considering the idea, 

 

Best regards,

Orla O' Hanlon

title: Audio Only / Studio Mode
body:
Please add an optional Audio-Only / Studio Mode that completely removes visual media from the Spotify experience for users who prefer to focus solely on listening.

This mode could include:

  • Hide music videos.
  • Hide Clips.
  • Disable Canvas animations.
  • Display static album artwork only.
  • Remove video recommendations from Home, Search, and artist pages.
  • Prioritize audio content throughout the app.

Benefits:

  • Distraction-free listening: Ideal for producers, mix engineers, musicians, audiophiles, students, and anyone who wants to focus on the music instead of visuals.
  • Better critical listening: Motion naturally draws attention away from the audio. An audio-only experience makes it easier to evaluate mixes, arrangements, and production.
  • Cleaner interface: A simpler, less cluttered experience for users who prefer Spotify as a music platform rather than a multimedia platform.
  • Improved accessibility: Helpful for users who are easily distracted or find constant visual motion overwhelming.
  • Lower resource usage: Potentially reduced CPU/GPU usage and improved battery life, especially on laptops used in studios.
  • User choice: This would be completely optional. Users who enjoy videos and visual content could continue using Spotify exactly as they do today, while others could opt into a dedicated listening-focused experience.

This feature wouldn’t remove or replace Spotify’s visual features, it would simply give users the choice to experience Spotify as a purely audio-focused platform when that’s what best suits their workflow or listening preference.

title: Standardize Simplified and Traditional Chinese Display According to User Language Preference
body:
Idea Summary

Allow Spotify users to choose whether artist names, song titles, album titles, and lyrics should be displayed in Simplified Chinese, Traditional Chinese, or the original script provided by the artist/label.

 

Problem

Currently, Spotify preserves the original Chinese script submitted by artists and labels. While this respects the original metadata, it creates an inconsistent and sometimes frustrating user experience for Chinese-speaking users.

 

For example:

  • If a user's phone and Spotify interface are set to Traditional Chinese, songs from Mainland Chinese artists are still displayed in Simplified Chinese.
  • Conversely, users who use Simplified Chinese will encounter Traditional Chinese metadata and lyrics when listening to artists from Taiwan, Hong Kong, and other Traditional Chinese-speaking regions.

As a result, users frequently experience a mixture of Simplified and Traditional Chinese throughout their library, playlists, artist pages, and lyrics, leading to inconsistent readability and visual presentation.

 

Why This Matters

Although many Chinese speakers can recognize both writing systems to some extent, most people have a strong preference and greater reading comfort with one script over the other. Having to constantly switch between Simplified and Traditional Chinese creates unnecessary friction in the listening experience.

 

This issue becomes especially noticeable when:

  • Browsing playlists containing artists from different Chinese-speaking regions;
  • Reading synchronized lyrics;
  • Searching for songs and artists;
  • Managing personal music libraries.

Spotify already localizes its user interface into both Simplified Chinese and Traditional Chinese, so extending this localization principle to music metadata would create a more seamless and user-friendly experience.

 

Proposed Solution

Add a user preference setting such as:

 

Chinese Script Display Preference

  • Follow Original Source
  • Always Display Simplified Chinese
  • Always Display Traditional Chinese

Spotify could then automatically convert:

  • Artist names;
  • Album titles;
  • Song titles;
  • Playlist titles (where applicable);
  • Lyrics.

Examples:

  • 后来的我们 ↔ 後來的我們
  • 爱情转移 ↔ 愛情轉移
  • 周杰伦 ↔ 周杰倫

Users who value authenticity can continue using "Follow Original Source," while users who prioritize readability can choose their preferred script.

 

Benefits

  • Improves accessibility and readability for Chinese-speaking users worldwide;
  • Creates a more consistent user experience;
  • Reduces visual inconsistency in playlists and libraries;
  • Respects both original artistic metadata and user preferences;
  • Aligns Spotify with localization practices already used by many global digital platforms and operating systems.

 

This suggestion is not intended to replace or diminish the original cultural and regional identities of artists. Rather, it aims to provide users with the flexibility to experience Spotify in the Chinese script they are most comfortable reading.

 

I believe this feature would significantly improve the experience for millions of Spotify users who use either Simplified or Traditional Chinese.
author: WilsonCheuk

title: Re: PlayPass: Secure Temporary Playlist Access Without Account Login
body:
PLAYPASS

A Spotify Feature Proposal

 

Executive Summary

PlayPass is a secure, temporary playlist access system that lets Spotify users share a playlist on any device without logging into their account. Users generate a time-limited code or phrase, control its validity, and can revoke or regenerate it at any time.

The Problem

People frequently log into Spotify on friends' phones, cars, TVs, or speakers just to access their playlists. This is inconvenient and creates privacy and security concerns.

The Solution

Generate a temporary PlayPass code from a playlist. On another device, enter the code to instantly access that playlist. No account login required.

Core Features

• Custom expiration times

• Instant revoke/regenerate

• Optional single-use

• Device limits

• View active sessions

• Playlist owner remains in full control.

User Journey

Create playlist → Generate PlayPass → Set expiry → Share code → Recipient enters code → Playlist opens → Code expires automatically.

Business Value

Improves sharing, reduces login friction, increases engagement in social listening, and reinforces Spotify as the easiest platform for collaborative music experiences.

Future Enhancements

QR codes, NFC sharing, Bluetooth discovery, event-based passes, and branded PlayPass invitations.

Closing

PlayPass is a simple feature with the potential to create a memorable user experience while respecting licensing and account security.

Prepared by

Chibuikem Anele

Independent Product Idea
author: Chibuikem_Anele

title: PlayPass: Secure Temporary Playlist Access Without Account Login
body:
Spotify Playlist Access Codes

Concept

Users can generate temporary access codes for their playlists, allowing anyone on another device to listen without logging into the owner's account.

User Flow

• Open a playlist. 

• Tap Generate Access Code. 

• Choose: 

• How long the code is valid (15 minutes, 1 hour, 24 hours, 1 week, custom). 

• Whether it's single-use or reusable until it expires. 

• Spotify generates a unique code or memorable phrase. 

• Share the code with anyone. 

On another device:

• Open Spotify. 

• Tap Enter Playlist Code. 

• Enter the code. 

• The playlist appears and can be played until the code expires. 

Owner Controls

• Generate a new code anytime. 

• Revoke an active code instantly. 

• Set an expiration time. 

• View how many devices have used the code. 

• Limit the number of devices that can access it. 

• Choose whether others can only listen or also save the playlist to their library. 

Benefits

• No need to log into your Spotify account on someone else's phone. 

• Great for parties, road trips, gyms, cafés, and classrooms. 

• Reduces the risk of forgetting to log out of another device. 

• Makes sharing playlists much faster. 

One challenge

Spotify would still need to respect music licensing. The playback would probably be tied to the listener's own Spotify account (Free or Premium), so they would experience whatever features their account allows. The code would grant access to the playlist itself, not transfer your subscription benefits.

I could genuinely imagine this fitting into Spotify alongside features like Spotify Codes. Instead of scanning a QR-style code to find a playlist, you'd be creating temporary, owner-controlled access that can be turned off whenever you want. It's a practical idea that solves a common annoyance while giving users control over access. BUT THIS WILL BE PERFECT, THINK OF IT
author: Chibuikem_Anele

title: Focus Listening Mode (Screen Off)
body:
I would like to present an idea for a feature that, in my opinion, could be of interest to a segment of Spotify users, particularly those who view music listening as a conscious and immersive experience.

Many listeners use Spotify not only on smartphones, but also on computers, televisions, audiophile setups, recording studios, and high-end home audio systems. With the continuous improvement of audio quality and the introduction of lossless playback, Spotify has become for many people not only a platform for consuming content, but also a medium for deeply experiencing music.

With this in mind, I would like to propose the introduction of a complete screen blackout mode during music playback. After activating a dedicated icon, the screen of a monitor, television, or smartphone would turn completely black (or effectively switch off) either immediately or after a few seconds, leaving the user with a purely auditory experience. The standard interface would instantly reappear upon detecting mouse movement, a key press, or any other user input.  The feature is designed to allow users to quickly switch between different screen states (active, dimmed, or blacked out) to improve concentration and minimize visual distractions.

The purpose of this feature would be to reduce visual distractions and allow listeners to focus entirely on the music. At a time when most content is consumed primarily through visual stimuli, it may be valuable to create a space where users can concentrate exclusively on what matters most: sound, emotion, and the artistic expression of the creators.

In my opinion, this would be a unique solution in the market and particularly appealing to users of high-quality audio systems and those who practice intentional or focused music listening. The feature could be integrated into the player interface, for example alongside existing secondary controls such as the karaoke button

title: Safari / Spotify Web Player Bug: random sounds automatically plays in fast-forward w/out interaction
body:
https://streamable.com/y16s4q

Plan

Free/

Country

 

Device

MacBook Pro M1

Operating System

Tahoe OS 26

 

My Question or Issue

Hi everyone,

I need some deep technical help with a very specific and alarming audio behavior on Spotify Web Player using Safari on macOS.

The Issue:

Even when I am completely idle on the Spotify homepage and do not click anything, Spotify automatically starts playing random, highly accelerated, and glitchy audio snippets in the background. When this happens, Safari’s standard "This tab is playing audio" speaker icon appears normally on the right side of the address bar, meaning Safari genuinely detects a legitimate active audio stream from the webpage.

The audio sounds like a chaotic, fast-forwarded burst (like an "Alvin and the Chipmunks" time-stretch effect) that forces an audio buffer into the browser completely unprompted.

The Suspicious Background:

I recently made the mistake of sharing my Spotify ⁠sp_dc⁠ and ⁠sp_key⁠ session tokens with a third-party music downloader tool. I highly suspect my session has been hijacked and my account is being used as part of a background stream-farming botnet (to simulate fake plays). It seems like an automated background script is trying to force media playback tokens through Safari's Web Audio API.

What happens technically:

Safari normally blocks autoplay and restricts background tab execution unless there is a physical user gesture (a click). However, whatever has hijacked the session seems to bypass this or trick Safari into thinking it's a legitimate user-initiated playback. When Safari tries to enforce its power-saving policy, the audio buffer piles up, and then it dumps the accumulated fast-forwarded audio burst all at once, with the address bar speaker icon staying active.

What I've tried:

 Cleared Spotify's local storage, site data, and unregistered Service Workers via Safari DevTools.

 Deleted all cookies (⁠sp_dc⁠ included).

 Changed sample rates in Audio MIDI Setup (44.1 kHz / 48 kHz).

 Toggled Web Audio Experimental Features / Feature Flags.

Despite clearing local data, as soon as I log back into Spotify, this automatic, weird fast-forward playback restarts on the homepage without any interaction from my side.

Is it possible that a persistent malware/infostealer has infected my local Safari/macOS proxy settings, or is this a known exploit where compromised ⁠sp_dc⁠ tokens force hidden background playback that messes up the Web Audio graph? How can I completely block Safari from allowing a tab to self-trigger audio playback without human input?

Thank you!
