title: Ongoing Issues Review: June 2026
body:
Hello there, folks!

 

Welcome to the newest edition of our Community Ongoing Issues Review.

 

Here we aim to keep you in the loop with regards to some of the most reported and viewed ongoing issues here in our Spotify Community.

 

We share updates from behind the scenes to let you know what's been fixed, what we’re tracking, and what we need your help with. You can also check the full Ongoing Issue section here. 

 

FIXED

1. Songs skip after a minute when using Spotify Connect in Lossless on LG soundbar

We have confirmation this is fixed. Please make sure your LG soundbars firmware is up-to-date.

 

2. Artist View crashes iPad app

Fixed! A new app version was slowly rolled out. Make sure to update the app following the steps listed here - it should be at least 9.1.46.

 

3. [Mac + Windows] Multi-select is broken on desktop and the Web Player

Reports came in about multi-selection not working properly on both the desktop app and the web player. We understand how important this feature is for all of us! This was fixed on version 1.2.90.

 

4. Back button goes back twice on the browser

We received some reports about the back button going back twice. The relevant team confirmed the fix for this one!

 

5. Saved tracks not showing up on DJ platforms

We received reports that saved tracks on users' playlists in DJ platforms on software like Serato and Rekordbox fail to load or are missing. A fix was put in place Thursday 21st May. This should be resolved now. 

 

6. Google Cast/ Chromecast devices unavailable for Spotify Connect

Reports were raised from Android users indicating Spotify Connect experienced issues showing available Google Cast/Chromecast speaker devices. We can confirm this is fixed.

 

7. CarPlay shows the previous track's album artwork

We believe this should now be resolved for everyone - just make sure you're running the latest version of Spotify available.

 

8. [Android] Playlists not loading

We received reports about self-created playlists (including Liked Songs) not loading on Android devices. The original issue was fixed! If you still have issues, make sure you're running the latest version of the app.

 

9. [Desktop] Home page not loading

The Home page on the desktop app and Web Player was not loading and instead displayed the following error: "Something went wrong. Search for something else?" We believe this should now be fixed for everyone. More info in the thread.

 

10. I can't delete "optional descriptions" from playlist since a last few weeks

A report was raised about being able to edit playlist descriptions, but not to delete them. This is working as it should now.

 

11. Volvo sensus - Blank Home tab

Fixed! The Home screen of the app on Volvo Sensus should be working as expected.

 

12. Spotify not playing on Android devices

We can confirm this is fixed. If anyone is still experiencing any of the issues mentioned in the thread, log out of the app and back in twice in a row. It'll trigger a manual sync of the settings and data of the device.

 

13. Downtime June 16th: Spotify not loading or showing error messages

This was a short-lived ongoing issue, which was quickly resolved! 💚

 

14. Wireless Android Auto Lossless Bug

The relevant team has made some changes backstage so this should now be fixed for everyone running version 9.1.54 and above. Make sure to update the app if you haven't already. 

 

15. Songs and albums originally in Japanese are now displayed in Romaji

It seems that the original titles in Japanese are intended for users in Japan. Users from outside of Japan would get the Romaji versions. We understand how this might be confusing though and we are actively working on improving the experience for everyone. Thanks for sharing your feedback here, we appreciate it.

 

16. Issues with Spotify services (login, subscription status, etc.)

Things should now be resolved and working properly. Thank you for the patience with this one!

 

17. Playlist order changed in Android Auto

The team has discovered the root cause of the issue and has scheduled a fix in an upcoming version of Spotify: 9.1.54.688! Make sure you're always keeping the app updated in the meantime.

 

18. Bug: Issue with dragging liked songs back in to liked songs playlist

Our teams have made some changes backstage and this should be fixed now. Make sure to keep your app up to date so you don't miss out on any developments.

 

UNDER INVESTIGATION

19. Your Episodes, Remove finished episodes after playing doesn't work

The setting to remove finished episodes from the Your Episodes list no longer works for podcasts that have been downloaded either manually or via auto-download.

 

20. Unable to remove local files from Liked songs

We’ve heard from some users that Local files in the Liked Songs playlist can’t be removed and appear greyed out.

 

NEED MORE INFO

21. Spotify disconnects from Bluetooth when open and changes playlists

If you encounter this issue, please reply here with the info we've requested.

 

22. IOS Spotify app resumes playback after disconnecting Bluetooth audio device
We're trying to gather some further info from anyone affected by this, so make sure to share if you're experiencing this. 

 

23. Spotify pixelated grey screen on my Google TV

title: Conversational AI DJ and Prompt-Based Playlist Assistant
body:
Hello Spotify Product Team,

I would like to suggest expanding Spotify’s AI capabilities into a fully conversational personal DJ and music assistant.

The idea is to let users control playback and create or edit playlists using detailed natural-language instructions. For example, a user could say:

“Play this album, but begin with these three songs in this specific order, then continue with the rest of the album.”

“Create a two-hour playlist that starts with upbeat 1980s music, transitions into modern alternative rock, and ends with mellow acoustic songs.”

“Take my current playlist, remove the slower songs, add more bass-driven tracks, and reorganize it so the energy builds gradually.”

The assistant could understand specific albums, songs, artists, moods, genres, eras, tempo, energy levels, transitions, and exact track order. Users could also modify the queue conversationally while listening instead of manually searching for and rearranging songs.

Another useful feature would be the ability to convert playlists into prompts—and prompts back into playlists.

Spotify could analyze an existing playlist and generate a description of its musical identity, such as:

“Bass-driven alternative rock with 1980s influences, energetic opening tracks, and a gradual transition into atmospheric electronic music.”

The user could then edit that description and ask Spotify to create a new playlist from it.

In simple terms:

Prompt → Playlist
Playlist → Prompt

This would transform Spotify from a music-search platform into a genuinely interactive personal music director. It could be especially useful while driving, working, exercising, hosting events, or anytime users want precise musical control without repeatedly handling their phones.

Thank you for considering the idea. I believe it could build naturally upon Spotify’s existing AI DJ while giving listeners significantly more creativity and control.

Best,

Leo Douglas

  • Extract songs from a specific genre or decade

  2. User enters a prompt such as:

  3. Spotify analyzes the playlist and automatically creates one or more new playlists while keeping the original playlist unchanged.

author: ᚲᛈ

title: Re: Can I upload AI generated music to Spotify if it is in no direct relation t
body:
Wow, you're so close.  

YES - when DJs take original works by artists (musicians, engineers, songwriters, vocalists, producers) and reuse them without consent, and often without credit or royalties, that's what we call "stealing". 

And that's the issue with AI.  If you want to use it - as I just did last night - to see what one of my songs sounds like if done in a completely different style, that's fine.  And I liked the result of one version so much I was thinking of just uploading it to Spotify, as is.  I came to this conversation to check on whether that's allowed. 

But the comments really made me understand this:
The vocals (which are excellent in both execution and production) were "scraped" from real, live artists.  And that artist has no idea that I'm using his voice, his phrasing, his talent.  I can't credit him because I don't know who he is.   So he can't consent.  

What if he is morally opposed to my song?  I doubt he would be, it's pretty inoffensive, but let's say it wasn't.  Let's pretend I'm a neo-Nazi and I wrote a song about white supremacy and subjugated other people?  Doesn't he have the right to say "**bleep** no!  I don't want my talent used for that!"  And what about the musicians?  I tried it as a house version, so a lot of it would have been synth generated anyway.  But (and this is important):

AI DOESN'T CREATE ANYTHING.  It steals.  It uses the work - the arrangements, the production ideas, the total effect - of a thousand other songs and doesn't pay diddley to anyone.  So even the synth parts, which I could generate myself, are being done in a way that was the brainchild of a producer, engineer, etc. 

And they don't even know I'm using it.  And they aren't getting recognized.  Or paid.

So, I'm going to have to think long and hard about this   The deals the major labels are cutting with Suno and other AI music generating sites ONLY cover the main artist.  So maybe Beyonce is being covered in an agreement, but literally NONE of the people that actually make her songs sound like a hit are getting any protection at all.  At the very least, we should all have the right of consent as to what our work is being used for. Better, we should have consent, get credited, and get a portion of whatever money is generated.

That's only fair.
author: therealex

title: What if Spotify just knew — the moment you opened it?
body:
What if Spotify had AI?
Not just recommendations. Something that actually understands you.
I commute to office every day. And every morning it's the same thing open Spotify, the music is there, the mood is there, I even have playlists I made myself. But in that moment I can never find the song that matches exactly how I feel.
So I built a concept around this problem.
A feature where you tell it once whether you're commuting, working from home, at the gym, or just relaxing. It learns your environment and combines it with your time of day and weather to build your perfect soundtrack automatically. No input needed every single day.
It also has an AI Search tab. Not keyword search. Emotion search.
I typed "I had a tiring day" and it gave me songs to lift me up.
There's also a Rediscovered section. Because we all have songs we deeply loved and completely forgot about. Songs sitting unplayed in our library for months. The AI brings them back at the right moment.

Got tired of not always finding the right song for the right moment and wondered if AI could just do it.

author: kuritonasu

title: Reporting Fraudulent Artists
body:
I came across a really strange artist profile today, where they had somehow managed to not only claim a song they did not own as the main artist (account in question is 'p6yment' for those interested), but also overwrite the actual song itself with an AI 'cover'. In fact, I only even discovered it as I had the original song in my playlist, only to see the song photo changed, vocals changed, and all the lyrics butchered despite being listed as the same song. The songs in question don't even show up on the original artists' pages anymore, not to mention that this profile somehow has 300k monthly listeners which is a level of grifting that I was genuinely shocked to see. (Granted, this could be inherited from the songs it has stolen as they seem to have more or less retained their metrics). It's one thing for the general AI grift accounts to get big with little pushback, but literally replacing the original works from the platform with a knockoff amalgamation is so many steps over 'unacceptable' for a large commercial platform.

 

All this to say, despite my best efforts I was unable, as a listener and not the original artist, to find a suitable or accurate label with which I could report this artist and that is very frustrating for me (I honestly wanted to report it for deception, but this profile has no information to report and I was not given an option to report the songs themselves). I can see the world where allowing general users to indiscriminately report songs could lead to an influx of nonsense reports, but that said, I genuinely think that in the face of this kind of blatant behavior there should be an option to do so. Or, alternatively, allow an option to report the profile as a rather than one specific aspect (as mentioned, this grifting account's crimes are it the songs it forcibly takes over, so no report of the profile details would be accurate). Requires a written justification and enforce a word count for all I care. I guess another option would be for Spotify to somehow apply safeguards that prevent this, but at this point I can't say I'm all that confident in that.

author: Marion15

title: AI music tranparency
body:
I would like to suggest greater transparency around AI-generated music on the platform.

 

 

To be clear, I am not asking Spotify to remove or ban AI-generated music. Listeners should be free to enjoy whatever music they like, and artists should be free to experiment with new technologies. My request is simply that users have access to information that helps them make informed choices.

 

 

Proposed feature:

 

 

• Add a small badge or disclosure icon on tracks, albums, or artist profiles to clearly indicate when music is substantially generated by AI.

 

Why this matters:

 

Recently, I discovered a song that I genuinely enjoyed, but I realized there was no easy way to determine whether it had been primarily created by human artists or generated through AI systems. As AI-generated content becomes more common, I believe many listeners will want greater transparency.

 

 

 

This proposal is not about restricting access to AI-generated music. It is about providing information and giving users more control over their listening experience.

 

 

Spotify has already demonstrated leadership in AI-powered features such as DJ and personalized recommendations. Implementing clear disclosure standards for AI-generated music would be another opportunity for Spotify to lead the industry in transparency and consumer choice.

 

 

People do not need to agree on the role AI should play in music, but they should have access to information that allows them to make their own decisions.

 

 

If this is something you would like to see, please vote and comment so Spotify can better understand community interest.

author: HiteshSurya

title: Smart Shuffle Should Learn Evening Listening Habits
body:
I would like Smart Shuffle to better understand when a listener is transitioning into a calmer evening mood.

For example, after 8:00 PM, if a user skips 3 Smart Shuffle recommendations and manually chooses songs with a calmer atmosphere, softer vocals, or a more relaxing mood, Smart Shuffle should temporarily adjust its recommendations for that listening session.

This would not require a separate mode. Instead, Smart Shuffle could learn from real-time behavior and slightly shift recommendations toward a more relaxed atmosphere during the evening.

The feature should focus more on current user actions than on long-term listening history. If the user keeps selecting calmer songs and listening to them fully, Smart Shuffle should understand that the listener wants a different mood than earlier in the day.

Many people use Spotify while preparing dinner, commuting home from work or school, reading, or winding down before sleep. In those moments, energetic recommendations can feel out of place, while calmer songs create a much better experience.

Examples of the type of songs that might influence these recommendations include artists and tracks such as Billie Eilish, Ed Sheeran, Xcho, Djo - End of Beginning, Porch Light - Oxygen, Olivia Dean - Dive, Glass Animals - Heat Waves, and Verzache - Needs.

This could reduce skips, increase Smart Shuffle usage, improve listening time, and make Smart Shuffle feel more personal and responsive to the listener's current mood.

Smart Shuffle feels at its best when it understands that, after a busy day, the listener wants a relaxing atmosphere.

title: hi res for brasil
body:
Spotify is charging premium prices while still delivering inferior audio quality compared to its competitors.

Apple Music, TIDAL, and Qobuz already include lossless and high-resolution audio in their subscriptions. In 2026, there is no technical or business justification for Spotify users to keep paying similar prices for lower-quality audio.

Spotify excels in playlists, recommendations, and user experience, but sound quality is one of the core aspects of a music streaming service. For users who care about audio, Spotify is falling behind.

If Spotify wants to remain competitive among music enthusiasts and audiophiles in Brazil, lossless and high-resolution audio should no longer be treated as a future feature—it should be the standard.

Bring High-Resolution Audio to Brazil.

title: Re: Your Episodes, Remove finished episodes after playing doesn't work
body:
It's frustrating that Spotify hasn't communicated any progress on this issue. The way I use the Your Episodes playlist is to enable automatic downloads on my favourite shows so that new episodes are downloaded and added to Your Episodes alongside any episodes that I have added ad-hoc based on recommendations and search.

 

I typically listen to this playlist via a Siri Shortcut (Hey Siri, in Spotify play Your Episodes), but because the playlist still contains episodes which haven't been automatically removed when finished, I end up restarting episodes I've already heard, unless I remember to open the app and manually remove each download.

 

As partial workaround, I have ended up disabling the automatic downloads for all my shows. Instead I use a Python script found on github called "auto-spotify-podcast-playlist" which checks each of the shows I follow and adds any new episodes to the Your Episodes playlist. I have the script scheduled to run in the background a few times a day on my Mac.

 

It's an extreme workaround but others might find it useful while we wait for Spotify to address the issue.

author: sinensys

title: "Moment" – Auto-detect and loop the most-listened part of a track
body:
Summary:

Add a "Moment" feature that identifies the most-listened-to segment of a song (similar to YouTube’s "most replayed" graph) and allows users to seamlessly loop that specific section.

​The Problem:

Many users find themselves listening to music for specific "hooks," drops, or repetitive rhythms—especially when trying to focus, study, or stay in a flow state. Currently, to hear that specific part again, users must manually scrub the progress bar, which is imprecise and disrupts the flow.

​The Solution:

Introduce a "Moment" feature with the following functionality:

​Data-Driven Markers: Using existing engagement data, Spotify would highlight the most popular/replayed section of a track directly on the progress bar.

​One-Tap Looping: A dedicated "Moment" button (or a simple double-tap gesture) would lock the playback to that specific loop.

​User Customization: Users would have the freedom to drag and adjust the "Start" and "End" handles to define their own preferred loop if they don't want to use the default community-selected segment.

​Persistence: The app would remember the user's saved "Moment" for that track for future listens.

​Why this adds value:

​Increased Engagement: It transforms passive listening into an active, interactive experience.

​Perfect for Productivity: For users who listen to music while working, coding, or exercising, this provides a "rhythm anchor" that keeps them in their zone without needing to manually skip or rewind.

​Community Insight: It leverages the collective listening habits of the community to help users find the "peak energy" of a song instantly.

​User Impact:

This feature respects the user's specific intent. By allowing us to focus on the parts of the music we love most, Spotify becomes more than just a player—it becomes a tool for curation and focused listening.

author: rtsrooia

title: Spotify Listening Time Sync for Innioasis Y1 Users ( or broadly mo3 players )
body:
Hello,
I am a student and a user of the Innioasis Y1, and I wanted to share a feature idea that I believe could benefit both Innioasis and Spotify users.
One of the main reasons I purchased the Y1 was to reduce distractions and separate my music from my smartphone. As a student, I often use the device while studying, reading, exercising, and commuting because it allows me to focus on my music without being tempted by social media, notifications, or other apps.
The offline-focused design of the Y1 is one of its greatest strengths, and I understand that it is intended to be different from a smartphone or streaming device. However, there is one issue that many users like me may experience: all of the listening time spent on the Y1 is effectively invisible to Spotify.
As a result, a significant portion of my daily listening habits is not reflected in my Spotify account. My recommendations, listening statistics, favorite artists, and Spotify Wrapped data only represent the time I spend listening on Spotify-enabled devices, while many hours of listening on my Y1 go unrecognized.
Rather than full Spotify streaming support, I would love to see an optional feature that allows users to link a Spotify account and synchronize listening activity. Some possible approaches could include:
• Syncing listening history when connected to Wi-Fi.
• Matching locally stored tracks with Spotify's catalog.
• Updating play counts and listening statistics.
• Allowing offline listening on the Y1 to contribute to Spotify Wrapped and recommendations.
I believe this would create a unique balance between digital wellness and modern music personalization. Users could enjoy a distraction-free, dedicated music player while still maintaining an accurate record of their listening habits within Spotify.
As more people look for ways to reduce screen time and use technology more intentionally, features like this could make dedicated music players even more appealing.
Thank you for taking the time to consider this suggestion. I appreciate the effort that goes into creating products that help people enjoy music in a more focused and meaningful way.

author: leonthemodic

title: Feature Request: Direct Track-Add API for Third Party Playlist Management
body:
I use Claude (Anthropic's AI assistant) integrated with Spotify to get personalized music recommendations and create playlists. The experience highlights a significant limitation in the current integration: all playlist creation is routed through Spotify's AI playlist generator, which produces unreliable results.

 

The problems with the current AI-based approach:
• Hits token limits and fails silently, resulting in playlists that don't appear in my library
• Substitutes incorrect tracks when it can't find exact matches, with no notification
• Generates generic results that don't reflect the specific tracks being requested
• Slow and resource-intensive compared to a simple database operation

 

The irony is that I get significantly better music recommendations from Claude than from Spotify's AI playlist feature. But when it comes to actually building the playlist, we're forced to route through a less capable AI layer that introduces errors and failures.

 

The fix is straightforward: a direct track-add API endpoint that accepts track IDs and adds them to a playlist. Licensing and content controls are already solved at the track metadata level and don't require AI interpretation. This would be faster, cheaper for Spotify to run, more reliable for users, and produce better results than the current AI middleman.

 

This isn't a request to remove AI playlist features — keep them for users who want them. This is a request for a reliable programmatic alternative that lets third party integrations like Claude deliver their full value to Spotify users.

author: Billy-J

title: Enable Hi-Res Losless / Dolby Atmos Streaming and Downloading
body:
With the launch of Spotify Lossless in September 2025, Premium subscribers finally got FLAC streaming up to 24-bit/44.1 kHz — a great first step. However, Spotify still trails the competition in two key areas: true Hi-Res audio and immersive/spatial formats.

The request:

  1. Hi-Res Lossless streaming beyond the current 24-bit/44.1 kHz ceiling, ideally up to 24-bit/192 kHz where the source masters allow it.
  2. Dolby Atmos / spatial audio support for tracks that have a dedicated Atmos mix.
  3. Offline downloads in both formats, so the full quality is also available without a connection — essential for travel, areas with poor coverage, and professional use cases (DJs, audio engineers, reviewers).

Why Apple Music is the benchmark:

Apple Music has offered Lossless (up to 24-bit/48 kHz), Hi-Res Lossless (up to 24-bit/192 kHz) and Dolby Atmos since May 2021 — at no extra cost, including downloads. Amazon Music Unlimited and Tidal offer comparable tiers. The catalog of Atmos mixes and Hi-Res masters already exists at the labels; Spotify is currently the only major platform not delivering it to listeners.

Why this matters:

  • Many subscribers currently pay for a second service (Apple Music or Tidal) purely for audio quality, while preferring Spotify's recommendations, playlists and ecosystem. Closing this gap removes the last reason to maintain two subscriptions.
  • Hi-Res-capable hardware (DACs, LDAC/aptX devices, wired IEMs, home audio systems) has become mainstream and affordable. The audience for this feature is no longer a niche.
  • Spotify Connect combined with Hi-Res and Atmos would be a genuinely unique selling point: no competitor matches Spotify's device integration.

Suggested implementation:

  • Add "Hi-Res Lossless" and "Dolby Atmos" as selectable quality options in the existing audio quality settings, with clear badges on eligible tracks (similar to Apple Music's labeling).
  • Make both available for downloads, with a visible storage/bandwidth warning given the larger file sizes.
  • If cost is a concern, even a paid add-on tier would be preferable to no option at all — though competitors have shown it can be included in the standard subscription.

Spotify Lossless proved the infrastructure for high-quality delivery exists. Extending it to Hi-Res and Atmos is the logical next step to make Spotify the complete platform for both casual listeners and audiophiles.

author: HellsBellstoo

title: Auto-Remove or Prompt to Remove Songs You Repeatedly Skip
body: I have huge playlists and it’s hard to clean them up manually. My idea is that Spotify should notice when you keep skipping the same song and either remove it automatically or just ask if you want to. You guys already track skips for recommendations so the data is already there. A few things to consider though — skipping in the last few seconds shouldn’t count because that’s basically a full listen. And it should take a few skips before anything happens, not just one, because sometimes you skip a song just because you’re not in the mood even though you still like it. If those edge cases are handled right this could make playlist cleanup basically automatic just from listening normally.
author: SawyerE

title: Fix Spotify DJ PLEASE
body: The old DJ sucked but was way better than this newer version. I understand there’s some people who want to always listen to new music and some people who want to listen to the music they already know. Spotify DJ has no way to discern this and the only thing you can ask for is genre which they frequently miss. I will ask DJ to play a type of music and they will give me the exact same playlist repeatedly despite me trying to give additional directions to what I want. Thinking time is incredibly slow. Get rid of the annoying yapping in between sets. If I’m feeling a section of music I want to continue it not completely change it six songs later. DJ needs to be familiar with the preferences of the individual, for example if I say I want to listen to morning music the DJ should be able to look at what I typically listen to in the morning (or look at playlists named for such) and suggest that music, not whatever strict presets it has for morning music which are likely inaccurate because everybody has different music they consider morning music. Old DJ used to be able to make sense of the command to only play music from my liked songs. This DJ cannot. Sometimes I want to play music I haven’t heard before but it’s a pain in the ass to toggle between that or music I do have in my liked because DJ thinks if I listened to a song for five days in a row that I want to listen to that song everyday for three months straight despite constantly skipping it. I used to be able to bully my old DJ into playing the music I want but this DJ is much less able to learn from feedback it seems. It’s not that hard: people want to be able to ask for a type of music, differentiate if it’s new music or already known music, and receive a playlist based on that. DJs memory also needs to be extended because it never replays songs I liked a year ago, it only focuses on the music I’ve listened to extremely recently which gets repetitive very fast. If it does replay old songs it’s the same six songs in the same order that it will bring up randomly, and it’s always exactly the top songs from that time period never a hidden gem that I liked for a week or something. Everyone wants something different out of DJ and DJ should be able to adapt to that.

title: Adding favourites on lock screen media player
body: I often listen to music on my phone and often use your song radios that are based on one song. So naturally there song recommendations, that I haven’t heard yet but that I really like. To add them to my favourites I always have to unlock my screen. I think it would be great to add the little “+” on the lock screen media player, so those new favourites can be saved quicker!

title: Custom tags for sorting playlist.
body:
I’ve been using Spotify for years, and one thing I’ve always wished for is the ability to add custom tags to songs within a playlist.

 

For example, I have a large Spanish playlist with hundreds of songs. Some are great for parties, some are more chill, some are perfect for driving, and others are good for working out. Right now, if I want to separate those moods, I have to create multiple playlists, which can become repetitive because many songs fit into more than one category.

 

It would be great if users could create their own tags (such as “Party,” “Chill,” “Gym,” “Road Trip,” etc.) and assign them to songs within a playlist. Then, when listening to that playlist, users could choose to filter or shuffle songs based on a specific tag while still keeping everything organized in one playlist.

 

The reason I think this would be useful is that sometimes I want to hear my entire playlist shuffled, but other times I’m in a specific mood or setting and only want a certain vibe without having to skip through songs or maintain several similar playlists.

 

I think this would make playlist organization much more flexible and reduce the need to create multiple playlists that contain many of the same songs.

title: AI Artist / Content Notification
body:
I asked an AI app about my concern that AI artists and material are not being flagged by Spotify prior to their release. It told me to write the following.

 

You can use the chat/contact options and state that:

  • You want clear labeling of fully AI-generated music.
  • You want AI-generated artists identified as such.
  • You want the ability to filter AI-generated content out of recommendations and playlists.
  • You believe listeners should be informed whether they are hearing human performers, AI-generated performers, or a mixture of both.

I believe AI has its place (example above) but should be required to make it known when it has "created" material. I believe that Spotify should be leading the promotion of music and video artists, not undermining their value.
author: paulewoolverton

title: Add a “note” feature to attach to individual songs in a playlist.
body:
Hello Spotify team,

I would love to see a feature that allows users to attach notes to individual songs within playlists.

Many people use playlists as more than just collections of music—they use them to document memories, organize creative projects, tell stories, track different periods of their lives, or associate songs with specific people, characters, emotions, or experiences. A note feature would give users a way to record those thoughts directly alongside each track.

for example, users could:

  • explain why a song was added to a playlist
  • attach personal memories or journal-style entries
  • write character notes for playlists made for books, fanfiction, roleplay, or original stories
  • add recommendations or comments when sharing playlists with friends
  • keep track of song meanings, lyrics, or interpretations

The feature could be optional and appear when a user taps a song’s menu within a playlist. Notes could remain private by default, with an option to make them visible when sharing playlists if desired.

This would make playlists feel more personal, creative, and interactive, while giving users another way to express themselves through music.

Thank you for your consideration!

author: malinskij

title: Re: ripoff
body:
Hey there @ripoff101,

 

Thanks for reaching out.

 

I get that audiobook hours can feel limited if audiobooks are the main thing you're using Spotify for. Longer titles can use up the monthly listening time pretty quickly.

 

That said, it's worth keeping in mind that Premium Family is primarily a music and audio plan for multiple people in the same household. The plan includes things like ad-free music listening, offline downloads, on-demand playback, multiple individual accounts, personalized recommendations, playlists, podcasts, and other Premium features for the family members on the plan.

 

The audiobook listening time is more of an added benefit on top of the Premium plan, rather than the whole plan being priced only around audiobooks. For some people, the included hours are enough for occasional audiobook listening. For heavier audiobook listeners, extra hours or a separate audiobook option may make more sense depending on how much they listen each month.

 

I definitely understand the feedback though. It would be great to see more flexible audiobook options in the future, especially for Family plan members who listen a lot. If audiobook value is the main concern, it's worth sharing that feedback in the Ideas section so other users can vote and add their thoughts too.

title: AI Settings Page
body:
1. DJ AI Settings

 

  • Overview: Users should have the ability to customize the DJ AI to play specific genres, moods, or artists.
  • Implementation:
    • Genres and Moods: Users can select from a predefined list of genres or moods.
    • Artist and Song Suggestions:Users can specify favorite artists, bands, albums, or songs.
    • Playlist Preferences: Users can create playlists and specify their preferences for the DJ AI to follow.

 

2. AI/Prompted Playlist Settings

 

  • Overview: Users can define specific prompts or themes for AI-generated playlists.
  • Implementation:
    • Prompt Input: Users can input a text prompt that the AI will use to generate the playlist.
    • Theme Selection: Users can choose from predefined themes or create custom ones.
    • Playlist Options: Users can specify the length of the playlist or the number of songs.

 

3. Mixed Playlists Settings

 

  • Overview: Users can customize the AI to mix playlists based on specific criteria.
  • Implementation:
    • Criteria Selection: Users can choose from different criteria such as song popularity, genre diversity, or tempo.
    • Mixing Preferences: Users can specify the mixing style (e.g., smooth, fast-paced).
    • Playlist Customization: Users can adjust the number of tracks or the time between songs.

 

4. AI Artists/Bands/Albums/Songs Settings

 

  • Overview: Users can manage and customize AI recommendations for artists, bands, albums, and songs.
  • Implementation:
    • Artist and Band Suggestions:Users can input their favorite artists or bands.
    • Album and Song Recommendations: Users can specify genres or themes for album and song suggestions.
    • Personalized Recommendations:Users can receive personalized recommendations based on their listening history.

 

5. Control Over AI Playback

 

  • Overview: Users should have the ability to control the playback of AI-generated content.
  • Implementation:
    • Pause and Play: Users can pause and play AI-generated content.
    • Skip Tracks: Users can skip tracks if they are not enjoyable.
    • Volume Control: Users can adjust the volume of AI-generated content.

 

6. Feedback and Improvement

 

  • Overview: Users should have the option to provide feedback on the AI's performance.
  • Implementation:
    • Rating System: Users can rate the AI's performance on a scale of 1 to 5.
    • Comments Section: Users can leave comments on their experience with the AI.
    • Suggestions: Users can provide suggestions for improvement.

By implementing these settings, users will have a comprehensive and customizable experience with the AI features in Spotify, allowing them to tailor the music recommendations to their preferences and enjoy a personalized listening experience.

author: Tkik

title: Workout flow
body:
I'd love Spotify to introduce a Workout Playlist mode designed specifically for strength training, HIIT, interval training, Hyrox, and other structured workouts.

Key features I'd like to see:

• Create workout sets and rest periods directly within a playlist.
• Set custom durations for work and rest intervals.
• Assign specific songs to specific sets or exercises.
• Select a specific section of a song (for example, the drop, chorus, or a custom timestamp range) to play during a particular set.
• Automatically transition between songs when a set changes.
• Voice guidance that announces:
- "Start set"
- "Rest"
- "10 seconds remaining"
- Exercise names
- Next exercise
• Ability to create complete workout programs with multiple exercises and rounds.
• Integration with Apple Watch and other fitness wearables.
• Hands-free operation during workouts.
• Workout templates for common formats such as HIIT, Tabata, EMOM, AMRAP, circuit training, strength training, and Hyrox.
• Option to automatically match song intensity to workout intensity.
• AI-powered recommendations that suggest songs based on workout phase, tempo, and personal preferences.
• Ability to share workout playlists that include both music and workout structure.
• Support for coaches, personal trainers, and fitness instructors to create and distribute workouts.
• Visual progress indicators showing current set, round, and remaining workout time.
• Offline support for downloaded workout playlists.

Why this would be valuable:

Spotify already plays a major role in people's workouts, but users currently need separate timer apps to manage intervals and exercise changes. A built-in Workout Playlist mode would combine music, coaching, and timing into a single experience, creating a more immersive and motivating workout while eliminating the need for multiple apps.

title: Quick Offline Mode Toggle on the Home Screen
body:
Hello, 

I would like to suggest a feature that could improve the user experience for Spotify Premium subscribers.

Currently, enabling or disabling Offline Mode requires navigating through Settings → Data Saver and Offline, which can be inconvenient for users who switch between online and offline listening frequently.

Proposed Feature

Add a dedicated Offline Mode toggle button directly on the Spotify home screen, in a quick-access area such as the top bar, quick settings menu, or profile menu.

User Benefits

  • Faster access to Offline Mode.
  • Improved convenience for users who regularly switch between Wi-Fi and mobile data.
  • Reduced need to navigate through multiple settings screens.
  • Better control over mobile data consumption.

Example Use Case

As a Premium user, I often enable Offline Mode whenever I leave home to avoid using mobile data while still listening to my downloaded music at the highest quality. When I return home, I disable Offline Mode again.

Since I do this multiple times a day, having to open Settings each time becomes repetitive and time-consuming. A one-tap toggle on the home screen would make this process much more efficient.

title: Desktop UI Redesign
body:
I would like to propose a redesigned Spotify Desktop layout focused on clearer navigation, better use of screen space, and a more contextual now-playing panel.

Current issue:
The desktop interface shows Library, Discovery, Now Playing, lyrics, video, artist info, and player controls in separate areas. This creates visual density and splits user attention.

Proposal:
- Move primary navigation into a simplified left sidebar.
- Keep the center area focused on discovery and recommendations.
- Convert the right panel into an integrated now-playing module with:
- current visual / video
- lyrics
- song information
- artist / track details
- playback controls
- device / queue / volume controls

Benefit:
This reduces clutter, improves hierarchy, and makes the desktop interface feel more intentional, especially on laptop screens.

I attached a wireframe/mockup to explain the idea visually.

title: DJ X History & Replay
body:
Spotify’s AI DJ is great at creating personalized listening sessions, but currently there is no way to view or revisit previous DJ segments.

 

I would like Spotify to add a DJ History page where users can see previously played DJ sessions and segments.

 

Features could include:

• See which songs were played and which were skipped

author: renemuala

title: Idea: optional filter to exclude AI-generated music from recommendations
body: Hi everyone, I’ve been a Spotify user for years and music has always meant something deeply personal to me. Not background noise — real human connection. Recently I found out that an artist I genuinely enjoyed, Morgan Luna, doesn’t exist. There’s no real person behind the voice. It’s a fully AI-generated project designed to look and feel like a real singer, with a profile, album covers, and millions of streams. I had no idea. Spotify gave me close to zero signals — I had to figure it out myself outside the platform. I’m not against AI music existing. But I am against not being able to choose. My idea is simple: give users an optional filter in Settings to exclude AI-generated music from recommendations, radio, and search results. Think of it like Waze letting you avoid toll roads. Nobody bans the tolls — they just give you the choice. I want to listen to music made by humans, for humans. I just want Spotify to let me choose that. Please vote if you feel the same way. The more of us ask for this, the harder it is to ignore. Thanks for reading.
author: Alberto_MG

title: Soundtrack Your Moment
body:
Soundtrack Your Moment

AI-powered Playlist Generation from Photos

Overview

Music is often chosen to match a moment, mood, or environment. However, users currently need to manually search for playlists that fit their situation.

Soundtrack Your Moment allows users to upload a photo and instantly receive a personalized playlist generated from the image’s atmosphere, visual characteristics, and the listener’s preferences.

 

User Journey

Step 1

User uploads a photo.

Examples:

  • Mountain sunrise
  • Night cityscape
  • Ocean beach
  • Forest trail
  • Rainy street
  • Café interior

Step 2

AI analyzes:

  • Colors
  • Lighting
  • Environment
  • Weather
  • Visual mood
  • Activity context

Step 3

Spotify generates a personalized playlist using:

  • Detected mood
  • User listening history
  • Favorite genres
  • Discovery recommendations

Step 4

User starts listening immediately or saves the playlist.

 

Example Scenarios

Mountain Sunrise

Detected mood:

  • Inspirational
  • Calm
  • Expansive

Generated playlist:

  • Indie Folk
  • Ambient
  • Cinematic Acoustic

 

Neon Night City

Detected mood:

  • Energetic
  • Futuristic
  • Urban

Generated playlist:

  • Synthwave
  • Electronic
  • Alternative Pop

 

Ocean Beach

Detected mood:

  • Relaxed
  • Warm
  • Carefree

Generated playlist:

  • Chill Pop
  • Reggae
  • Tropical House

 

Benefits for Users

  • Faster music discovery
  • More emotional personalization
  • Increased engagement during travel and outdoor activities
  • Creates memorable music experiences connected to real-life moments

 

Benefits for Spotify

  • Increased session time
  • More playlist creation and saving
  • Strong showcase of Spotify AI capabilities
  • Unique social sharing opportunities
  • New pathway for music discovery

 

Future Expansion

The feature can evolve beyond photos:

  • Photos
  • Videos
  • Camera Live Mode
  • Location-based context
  • Text prompts (“Night train ride”, “Walking through Tokyo”, etc.)

The long-term vision is to create a personalized soundtrack for every moment of a user’s life.

 

Concept Tagline

“Capture a moment. Hear its soundtrack.”

title: Re: Liked Songs Playlist playing unliked songs
body:
Hey, @Matthewwells,

 

Welcome to the Spotify Community!

 

Sounds like you might have Smart Shuffle toggled on - the feature adds similar song recommendations to the queue which if you like, can save to your playlist.

 

If you have Premium and would like to turn it off for the specific playlist just cycle through the different Shuffle modes, those being Linear > Shuffle > Smart Shuffle.

Cheers 🤘
author: Yordan

title: #MusicMonday Review - May 2026
body:
#MusicMonday is the hashtag I've been using for quite a while to share music recommendations from up-and-coming artists. Always fresh, and always different, trying to look for trends before they become one. You can check April's review for more music.

 

It doesn't matter if you want to raise attention to government, environmental, or personal issues, music is the perfect way to express yourself, whatever genre you may prefer. Give them a listen, with a word from the artists themselves. 🎧

 

June Holland – Mouldy Orange

 

I spent so much time just
Sat on my own
They said I disrupted the rest of the kids
So they left me alone


But I was a fire until you put out my flames
Labelled me naughty and stupid
When deep down I knew I had brains

 

Can't help but be mad that school
Could've been easier
If only I'd have had an educated teacher

 

We begin our trip in South Manchester, England for a soulful Indie Folk track about a girl who has being punished for being herself, but with time understood she's perfect just as she is:

 

"It’s a personal experience, I was always labelled the naughty kid in primary school I got in trouble a lot but no one ever suggested it could be ADHD, I’m not sure it was really explored in my school especially in girls…

 

When I reached my twenties I finally got diagnosed with ADHD and although it didn’t come as a surprise it did bring up a lot of emotions including anger and grief for the young misunderstood girl I was growing up.

 

So this song was kind of like therapy for me. Getting out all the emotions I felt and reflecting on my life. But I also wanted to create this song and share my experience because I know there will be many others like me out there and I want to make them feel seen and know that they aren’t alone.

 

The response has been amazing so far it’s been wonderful to see people connecting with the track. 🥹🧡😉"

 

spotify:track:4KepSGT6osc3dZwyCldBAC:small

 

Shines – Late for Days

 

Since you went away
it's hard to fall asleep
your t-shirt smells the same
you left it here with me


Let's travel east to Grimsby, England, for an Indie Rock track about those really long conversations that seem to go on for hours in the middle of the night:

 

"I think it's one of them open ended songs that sort of content different meanings. Whether it be sort of the breakup of a relationship, or a friendship or just sort of whatever's left after that's happened.

 

But also it came together really quick for us. Just got sort of jamming over a little riff and we're sort of working like that for all our stuff this year. It's not overthinking stuff, not polishing stuff, and just keeping it nice and fresh, and just getting it out there.

 

I'd say the style of this song is more shoegaze than what we've done prior, but there's a lot of elements in our first album that's shoegaze. It's probably inspired by great bands like Beach Fossils, Wild Nothing, you know, The Drums. I like all them type of bands. They've all got a good groove."

 

spotify:track:2s5fhMX6PvJNh3Sx2heiMb:small

 

Fine Night Elements – All We Know

 

Valleys low to mountains high

Rivers running from the beginning of time

Sun shines through to the forest floor

Last flower raises its head

But only once more


Leave us alone I hear them cry

Don't speak your language but why should we die?

Do you ever think that this world does not revolve around you?

 

Up north in Gateshead, England, we get an Alt Rock track blended with some Indie vibes about human's inherent appetite to destroy what's in our path, and take everything we can. It's all we know:

 

"Initially the music was laid down just through jamming, but the experience that inspired the lyrics are just the constant information about global warming that we’re consistently ignoring. The track discusses the path we’re on as a species and how we need to change."

 

spotify:track:2vZ5Hd8r42VoqrmtMz61kP:small

 

Draken – It Serves You Right

 

 

You screwed up, you crossed the line
So don’t act so surprised, my friend

We always knew it was a matter of time
Before you’d be knee deep in sh*t again

 

Slowly dying
Thought you were death defying
They got you dead to rights
And truth be told… it serves you right

 

We fly now to Oslo, Norway for a Metal track that tells what happens when arrogance makes you blind, while leaving your decency behind. Bassist Hallward explains:

 

"It's about power hungry politicians, and corruption. I wrote the lyrics when there was a series of political scandals in Norway; politicians taking advantage of the system, misuse of public funds, etc."

 

spotify:track:3ZqpEOcb4Xq0Yo6T2FKqpV:small

 

dimblue – One Hundred Thoughts

 

 

I'm not defined by my sense of revenge
I drop my innocence and I don't want it back
Thoughts travel so fast that I cannot bend
My heart is broken
Some things you cannot mend

 

Compelling answers don't always let you rest
Satisfaction isn't always the best
Some say that truth lies in the middle ground
But our subconscious wish is still to be found

 

We now travel to Bari, Italy for an Indie Folk track with some Lo-Fi details about taking time to reflect on something you have experienced, and trying to come up with an adequate response:

 

"Overthinking is exactly what gave life to this track. All those thoughts that flow incessantly, often leaving us exhausted.

 

I believe the song was born from the desire to bring some order, while at the same time immersing myself in the creative flow. I think the filter was the instrument itself—the acoustic guitar—which somehow created the sense of order I was searching for in that creative moment.

 

The electric guitar lines, on the other hand, allowed me to push into the background all those thoughts that tend to overlap."

 

spotify:track:60fe6PmHwQuwrc9mcbaG2s:small

 

Ismena – How Are You?

 

You came into my life and you said you were mine
And started a fire in my mind
I thought I knew who you were but you're a liar
And now I don't trust a single word

 

Maybe one day it will turn out right
Who do you think you're standing beside?
Finally I am enlightened
I see you for who you are

 

We end out travels in Utrecht, NL. for a dreamy, melancholic Indie Folk track about being hurt to the point of making sure you won't cross paths with that person ever again:

 

"This was a very personal experience that affected me deeply. It's about trusting someone and gradually finding out that this person is not who they say they are.

 

It can be hard to grasp when someone turns out to be completely different than what you always thought they were. It's also about finally accepting their true nature and not letting them affect you anymore."

 

spotify:track:0ubUu2y2iaCqUFyLZsdF65:small

 

Listen to them and much more on the complete playlist

 

spotify:playlist:2IYRLjcwf2X9vC0QrI0b0P

 

See you next time!

title: Re: Playback issues: Songs going silent on Android app
body:
This happens to me literally every single time I try to listen using a wired connection and firmly believe that if Samsung had kept an aux port, this wouldnt be an issue. 

  • The day, time and time zone this has last occurred on: moat recently, 10+ times today alone, 5/25/26 between 7am-12pm PST. 
  • Your device's OS version: Android
  • The version of Spotify you're running: 9.1.50.1906
  • Does the app switch to the next song or is it stuck on the current one (with the timer still counting)? The app never skips songs or pauses them when this happens - Spoitfy continues playing the song but there is no audio output. I usually have to unplug my connection twice to get things working again. 
  • Whether this occurs when playing from the mobile device directly or when connected to another device (Bluetooth, headphones, etc.): this happened exclusively when trying to listen through a wired audio connection.

title: Re: Mark / Disable AI Generated Songs
body: Just signed up for a 1 month free trial for Deezer based on a comment I saw on this post. Deezer clearly marks AI slop and does not include AI generated content in its recommendations. Did some research and Deezer has shown a real commitment to transparency for users and to protecting artists' rights - they have AI detection technology to make sure they catch AI slop.  Super easy to transfer your Spotify playlists/favorites, etc. I'll miss Spotify's insane playlist names  that it gives my daylist but other than that Deezer seems great, and if they continue to stand against AI content, then I'll be sticking with Deezer.
author: AnnaK1

title: Loved Songs
body:
I think Spotify should have a second tier above regular liked songs called “Loved Songs.”

 

A lot of people probably use likes the way you think — if you enjoy a song, you like it. But that also means it's easy to end up with a lot of liked songs that all get treated the same, even though there are certain tracks I'm sure we all care about way more than others.

 

For example, I like every song from Hail The Sun but there are still a smaller number of songs that I come back to constantly and consider my personal favorites. Right now the only real way to separate those songs is by making a playlist. But that would mean creating a separate playlist for every artist whose discography I fully enjoy, just to isolate a handful of my absolute favorites.

 

It would be cool if Spotify added:

  • the current green checkmark for liked songs
  • a second option, like a red heart, for “Loved Songs”

Then users could:

  • filter only loved songs from an artist
  • shuffle only loved songs
  • quickly revisit their favorite tracks without needing extra playlists

I also think this could help Spotify recommendations since it gives a stronger signal about which songs users actually connect with the most, instead of treating every liked song equally.
author: Zero011

title: remove track from top songs
body:
Currently, there is no way for a user to edit or delete specific tracks from their historical listening data. If an account is compromised, left logged in on a public device, or suffers a third-party API glitch, phantom streams permanently corrupt the user's "All Time Favorites" and statistics.

While "Exclude from Taste Profile" helps fix future recommendations, it does not clean up the historical data ledger. We need a simple menu option—similar to "Hide or Delete this song"—that allows us to permanently delete or hide specific tracks or albums from our historical playback data and top lists.
author: bassoclock

title: Re: DJ Recommending the same set of songs
body: I was able to fix the AI DJ's recommendations so that it wouldn't keep suggesting the same songs I'd liked before. Previously, I had created a playlist that completely duplicated the “Liked Songs” playlist, and that was causing the recommendations to go haywire. I went to the settings for that playlist and selected “Exclude from your taste profile” After that, I added a couple of albums by other users that I found on playlost.fm, and my recommendations were restored; now it suggests new songs much more often.
author: aearone

title: Profile “Favorite Songs” feature
body: Nowadays Spotify is mainly used individually (each person listens to their own music and there is little social interaction within the app). However, many young people love sharing recommendations and discovering songs through other people on social media. I thought it could be interesting to add a section on user profiles where people could display their 3 to 5 favorite songs at the moment (similar to Letterboxd). I believe this would give profiles more personality and make it easier to discover new music through friends and other users. It could also increase interaction within Spotify and make the app feel not only like a music streaming platform, but also more like a social network.
author: andrea2005

title: Channel/Radio Session options
body:
I recently came over to Spotify from Pandora sort of reluctantly. I had been with Pandora for 10 years, and I had plenty of reasons I chose to stay that long, but now I had to switch and I have some recommendations of features I really liked on Pandora. 

 

When I would listen to the radio function of an artist (my most preffered way of listening to music) I was able to select the mood of the session on the radio. It had roughly 8 functions ranging from Artist only to Relax or Energize and the function worked extremely well. It would play music in that genre or similar to that artist but it could be tailored to how you were feeling. It helped me find new music with Discover and it helped me find music in my favorite genre or by my favorite artists that was maybe more calm and relaxing for when I wanted to study or wind down for the day.
author: rgb101125

title: Re: February 2026 Spotify for Developers update: thread
body:
@jsneij wrote:

Request for endpoint access review — single-user personal case (Client ID eb476afb75a04275babd1ebfaec1a1b8)

Hi Spotify team,

Posting here following the February 2026 update. I'd like to request a case-by-case review of endpoint access for my Development Mode app "Claude Code ProjectSpotify".

My case in one paragraph: I'm a single individual using the API exclusively against my own Premium account (jsneij, Premium since 2014) to manage my own library — dedupe playlists by ISRC, merge themed collections, batch-rename, archive old ones. The Spotify client supports all of this manually, but at my scale (142 playlists, ~2,400 saved tracks) it becomes prohibitive. The app runs locally on my laptop. No third-party users, no public service, no monetisation, no commercial path. User Management is 0/5 because there's no one else to add.

Why I'm posting: The February 2026 post explicitly states Development Mode is meant to support "learning, experimentation, and personal projects for non-commercial use by individual developers." That's exactly my profile. However, the restricted endpoint set for newly-created Client IDs returns 403 on the core read/write playlist endpoints I need, which makes basic personal-account hygiene impossible. The March 9 update postponed these restrictions for existing integrations after community feedback — I'd appreciate that same spirit being applied to a single-user personal case.

Specific endpoints requested:

  • GET /v1/playlists/{id} (with tracks.items)
  • GET /v1/playlists/{id}/tracks
  • POST/PUT/DELETE on /v1/playlists/{id}/tracks

Not asking for audio-features, audio-analysis, recommendations, or any November 2024 deprecated endpoints.

Constraints I'd accept: Lock User Management to my single account, signed attestation, periodic review, read-only access only — whatever scoping works for Spotify. Even read-only on my own playlists' tracks would cover most of the use case.

Happy to provide any additional context. Thanks for considering this!
Jorge

I agree with you. We also believe the personal use-cases should get a lot more freedom. There are security and legal hoops to jump through - but our ambition is to fully support your ability to manage your Spotify.
